snappy: Forms reporting various non-employee income types — independent-contractor pay, interest, dividends, government payments.
detail: Multiple variants: 1099-NEC (non-employee compensation, separate form since 2020), 1099-MISC (rents, royalties, prizes), 1099-INT (interest), 1099-DIV (dividends), 1099-G (government payments), 1099-K (third-party payment networks like PayPal). Payor must issue if payments meet thresholds ($600 standard for most types). $600 threshold for 1099-K causing significant burden — repeatedly extended at higher temporary thresholds. Triggers IRS matching against recipient's tax return.

snappy: A sale of debtor's assets outside the ordinary course of business — often used for major corporate-asset sales in Chapter 11.
detail: 11 U.S.C. § 363(b). Often used to sell distressed business as going concern, free and clear of liens. Court-supervised auction process common — stalking-horse bidder negotiates initial purchase agreement; competing bidders permitted at auction. Notable 363 sales: Chrysler, GM, Lehman Brokerage businesses, FTX exchanges. Faster than full plan confirmation; bypasses plan-confirmation requirements. Limited by Czyzewski v. Jevic (2017) — can't violate absolute-priority rule via structured dismissals.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Employer-sponsored retirement plan — pre-tax employee contributions plus often employer match.
detail: 2024 contribution limit: $23,000 employee plus $7,500 catch-up if 50+. Employer match commonly 3-6% of salary. Traditional pre-tax (deductible now, taxable at withdrawal) and Roth (after-tax now, tax-free at qualified withdrawal). Vesting schedule for employer contributions. Early withdrawal (before 59½) typically 10% penalty plus tax. Required minimum distributions (RMDs) begin at age 73 (post-SECURE 2.0). Most common employer retirement-plan structure since 401(k) provision added 1978.

snappy: Strict test for independent-contractor classification — worker is presumed employee unless all three prongs satisfied.
detail: Originally Massachusetts (1990); spread to California (AB5, 2019) and others. Prongs: (A) worker free from control and direction, (B) work outside usual course of hiring entity's business, (C) worker customarily engaged in independent trade. Significantly harder to satisfy than common-law tests. California Proposition 22 (2020) carved out app-based drivers. AB5 has been amended repeatedly to add industry exceptions. Major influence on FLSA reform debates.

snappy: Misusing a legitimate legal procedure to harass or coerce — different from filing frivolous suits.
detail: Distinct from malicious prosecution (which targets the filing itself); abuse of process targets misuse after filing — e.g., using discovery to humiliate, subpoenaing third parties to gain leverage in unrelated business deals, oppressive collection tactics. Damages cover the harm caused. Free-speech protections sometimes overlap (litigation as petitioning). Increasingly recognised as tool against weaponised litigation.

snappy: Contract provision making all future payments immediately due upon a triggering event (typically default).
detail: Standard in mortgages, commercial loans, equipment leases. Triggering events: missed payment, bankruptcy, breach of covenant, material adverse change. Limits on enforcement: state anti-deficiency laws (residential mortgages), forbearance practices, sometimes prepayment-penalty interaction. Foreclosure typically begins with acceleration. Most modern variable-rate loans contain detailed acceleration triggers and cure rights.

snappy: Agreement to the exact terms of the offer — that's what forms the contract.
detail: Under the 'mirror image rule', acceptance has to match the offer; a counter-offer kills the original. The UCC relaxes this for sales of goods (§ 2-207, the 'battle of the forms'). Acceptance can be by signing, by performance, or sometimes by silence if the parties have a course of dealing. The 'mailbox rule' makes acceptance effective when sent, not when received — important when offers are revoked in the gap.

snappy: A federal sentencing guideline reduction for defendants who admit guilt and assist the prosecution.
detail: U.S.S.G. § 3E1.1 — typically a 2-3 level reduction off the offence level, often saving years of prison. Granted for guilty pleas, candid statements at sentencing, and 'truthful' admission of conduct. Going to trial nearly always forfeits the reduction; rare exceptions for cases tried on legal issues only (e.g., constitutional challenge with stipulated facts). Powerful incentive driving the federal system's 95%+ guilty-plea rate.

snappy: Aiding, encouraging, or abetting a crime — you're punished as if you committed it yourself.
detail: Federal aiding-and-abetting statute (18 U.S.C. § 2) treats accomplices as principals. Requires (1) the underlying crime was committed and (2) the defendant intentionally facilitated it. Rosemond v. United States (2014) clarified that 18 U.S.C. § 924(c) firearms-during-drug-trafficking accomplice liability requires advance knowledge of the gun. The getaway driver in a robbery is the classic accomplice; the parent who lets a child commit crime can fall on the wrong side of the line.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: A new agreement that replaces and discharges an old contractual obligation.
detail: Accord is the new agreement; satisfaction is its performance. Common when parties dispute the amount owed: debtor sends a check marked 'payment in full' for less than claimed; creditor cashing it accepts the accord. Some states require the disputed-claim element; others let any new agreement substitute. Different from novation (which substitutes a new party as well).

snappy: An investor meeting income, net worth, or sophistication thresholds — qualifies to invest in private offerings.
detail: Income test: $200K individual / $300K joint for two prior years. Net worth test: $1M excluding primary residence. 2020 amendments added professional credentials (Series 7, 65, 82) and entity criteria. SEC has discussed inflation-adjusting thresholds; no major changes through 2024. Issuers rely on accredited-investor exemption under Reg D for unregistered offerings. Verification requirements stricter for Rule 506(c) general-solicitation offerings.

snappy: Knowing falsity or reckless disregard for truth — what public figures must prove to win defamation suits.
detail: New York Times v. Sullivan (1964) — born of civil-rights-era libel suits against the New York Times by Alabama officials. Reckless disregard requires 'high degree of awareness of probable falsity' (St. Amant v. Thompson, 1968). Justice Thomas and Justice Gorsuch have suggested revisiting Sullivan; despite repeated invitations, the Court hasn't taken up the question through 2024. Clear-and-convincing evidence standard, not preponderance.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: The physical act or omission that constitutes a crime — the conduct element.
detail: Paired with mens rea (the mental element) to form criminal liability. Voluntary acts only; reflexes, sleepwalking, and convulsions generally don't qualify. Omissions count only when there's a legal duty to act — parents for children, contractual obligation, statute requiring action. Mere thoughts are never punishable; the criminal law requires conduct.

snappy: Federal statute banning disability discrimination in employment, public services, and accommodations.
detail: 42 U.S.C. §§ 12101 et seq. (1990); amended by ADAAA (2008) which broadened 'disability' definition. Title I covers employment — applies to employers with 15+ employees. Requires reasonable accommodation absent undue hardship. Major Supreme Court applications: PGA Tour v. Martin (2001), Sutton v. United Airlines (1999, overturned by ADAAA). Disability defined broadly post-2008 amendments. EEOC primary enforcement; private right of action also available.

snappy: Federal statute banning employment discrimination against workers age 40 and over.
detail: 29 U.S.C. §§ 621 et seq. (1967). Applies to employers with 20+ employees. Gross v. FBL Financial Services (2009) held ADEA requires 'but-for' causation — higher bar than Title VII's mixed-motive standard. Older Workers Benefit Protection Act (1990) imposes specific waiver requirements for severance agreements. Major issues: layoff-targeting older workers, mandatory retirement, age-based stereotypes in hiring.

snappy: If you have reasonable grounds for insecurity about the other side's performance, you can demand assurance — and treat failure as repudiation.
detail: UCC § 2-609 and Restatement (Second) § 251. Insecurity must be reasonable (financial deterioration, performance problems on related contracts, public defaults). Demand must be in writing. 30-day response window typical. Failure to provide adequate assurance is treated as repudiation. Critical tool in long-term supply contracts, construction agreements, and franchise relationships when counterparty stability becomes uncertain.

snappy: Total income minus specific 'above-the-line' deductions — the figure most tax thresholds key off.
detail: IRC § 62. Above-the-line deductions include: traditional IRA contributions, student-loan interest (limited), HSA contributions, half of self-employment tax, alimony pre-2019 divorces. AGI determines eligibility for many credits and deductions (medical expenses 7.5% AGI threshold, charitable contributions percentage limits). Modified AGI (MAGI) used for IRA deduction limits, Roth IRA eligibility, Net Investment Income Tax, ACA premium tax credits.

snappy: Process to obtain a green card while already in the US — without returning to home country for consular processing.
detail: INA § 245. Eligibility: lawful entry (with some exceptions), immigrant visa immediately available, admissibility, generally maintained lawful status. Common path: spouse of US citizen filing Form I-485 alongside immigrant petition. § 245(i) provides limited grandfather waiver for some out-of-status applicants. Concurrent EAD (employment authorisation) and AP (advance parole) typically granted during pendency. Alternative for those not eligible: consular processing abroad.

snappy: Rules governing how agencies operate — procedures, rulemaking, adjudication.
detail: Federal Administrative Procedure Act (1946) sets the framework: notice-and-comment rulemaking, formal hearings, judicial review under 'arbitrary and capricious' standard. Major Supreme Court doctrines shape agency power: Chevron deference (1984, overruled 2024), Auer/Kisor deference, major-questions doctrine (West Virginia v. EPA, 2022). Agencies wield extraordinary authority — the modern 'fourth branch' of government.

snappy: The 1946 statute setting baseline procedures for federal agency rulemaking and adjudication.
detail: 5 U.S.C. §§ 551–706. Default framework for all federal agencies unless specific statute provides otherwise. Two main types of agency action: rulemaking (creates rules of general applicability) and adjudication (decides specific cases). Establishes judicial review standards: arbitrary-and-capricious, contrary to law, unsupported by substantial evidence. Companion to the Freedom of Information Act and Government in the Sunshine Act for transparency requirements.

snappy: Legally creating a parent-child relationship between unrelated parties — court-supervised process.
detail: Terminates biological parents' rights and creates new legal parents. Types: agency, private (independent), stepparent, second-parent, international. Pre-adoption home study by social worker typically required. Indian Child Welfare Act (ICWA) governs Native American children — challenged in Haaland v. Brackeen (2023). Post-adoption: amended birth certificate issued; original sealed. Modern trend: open adoptions with continuing biological-family contact.

snappy: A lawsuit within a bankruptcy case — discharge challenges, fraudulent-transfer recovery, claim objections.
detail: Federal Rules of Bankruptcy Procedure Part VII (mirrors civil-procedure rules). Common types: non-dischargeability complaints, preference and fraudulent-transfer recovery, lien-validity disputes, intercreditor priority disputes, breach of stay. Often the substantive heart of complex bankruptcies. Discovery, summary judgment, and trial available. Settlements common — bankruptcy court approval typically required.

snappy: Acquiring legal title to land by openly using it long enough.
detail: Elements: actual possession, open and notorious, hostile (without permission), continuous, and exclusive — for the statutory period (often 10–21 years depending on the state). Originated to keep land productive when boundaries were unclear. Modern uses: boundary disputes between neighbours, abandoned-lot squatters, fence-line encroachments. Some states require the adverse possessor to pay property taxes for the full period.

snappy: A written statement of facts sworn under oath — used as evidence when live testimony isn't available.
detail: Routinely attached to motions to back up factual assertions. The signer (the 'affiant') swears under penalty of perjury that the statements are true. Many courts now accept the substitute 'declaration under penalty of perjury' which doesn't require a notary. False statements in either are prosecutable as perjury.

snappy: Executive-branch body with delegated authority to regulate a field.
detail: Examples: FDA (food/drugs), FCC (telecoms), FTC (consumer protection), SEC (securities), EPA (environment), FAA (aviation), NHTSA (vehicles). Agencies issue rules, investigate, bring enforcement actions, and adjudicate. Some are independent (FTC, SEC) — heads serve fixed terms, can't be removed at will; others are 'executive' (FDA, FAA) — directly under presidential control. Independent-agency structure is constitutionally contested (Seila Law, 2020).

snappy: A circumstance that makes a crime worse and bumps up the sentence — vulnerability of victim, weapon use, planning.
detail: Federal Sentencing Guidelines list dozens. State capital-sentencing schemes require specific aggravating factors (heinous, atrocious, cruel; multiple victims; pecuniary gain) before death penalty applies (Apprendi v. New Jersey, 2000; Ring v. Arizona, 2002). Aggravators must be charged in the indictment and proved beyond a reasonable doubt when they raise the statutory maximum.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Ongoing payments from one ex-spouse to another after divorce — to address economic disparity.
detail: Court considers: length of marriage, earning capacity, age, health, contributions to marriage. Types: temporary (during proceedings), rehabilitative (limited duration for retraining), permanent (long-term), reimbursement (for funding spouse's education). Recently shortened durations and presumptive end dates have become more common. Modifiable upon material change in circumstances. TCJA (2017) made post-2018 alimony non-deductible by payor and non-taxable to recipient — reduced settlement leverage.

snappy: A parallel tax system that disallows certain deductions — to ensure high-income taxpayers pay a minimum.
detail: TCJA (2017) dramatically reduced AMT scope: exemptions raised, phase-outs raised, far fewer households subject. Pre-TCJA, AMT hit millions of upper-middle-income filers — particularly in high-tax states. AMT exemption (2024): $85,700 single, $133,300 joint. Phaseout begins around $609,350 single. Corporate AMT eliminated by TCJA but new 15% Corporate Alternative Minimum Tax (CAMT) created by Inflation Reduction Act (2022) for $1B+ companies.

snappy: An updated complaint filed after the original — to correct errors, add claims, or respond to a motion to dismiss.
detail: Federal Rule 15(a). One amendment as of right before responsive pleading; afterwards by consent or leave of court ('freely given when justice so requires'). Repeated amendments draw 'futility' objections — court denies leave when the amended claim would still fail. Statute-of-limitations issues handled by 'relation back' under Rule 15(c) — amendments tied to original filing date in specified circumstances.

snappy: A court order declaring a marriage void or voidable — treats marriage as never having existed.
detail: Distinguishable from divorce (which ends a valid marriage). Grounds: bigamy, incest, lack of capacity (mental incapacity, intoxication), fraud, duress, underage, sham marriages (immigration fraud). Religious annulments (Catholic) are church recognition not legal status. Property and child issues handled separately if marriage produced them. Increasingly rare relative to divorce; usually pursued only for religious or specific legal reasons.

snappy: The defendant's first written response to a complaint — admit, deny, or 'lack knowledge'.
detail: Due roughly 21 days after service in federal court. The answer responds paragraph-by-paragraph to the complaint and raises affirmative defences (statute of limitations, contributory negligence, release). Failure to deny an allegation is treated as admission. The answer may also include counterclaims against the plaintiff.

snappy: DMCA prohibits breaking digital locks (DRM) on copyrighted works, even for otherwise lawful uses.
detail: 17 U.S.C. § 1201. Targets technological protection measures. Triennial Library of Congress rulemaking creates narrow exemptions (e.g., for vehicle diagnostics, security research, jailbreaking phones). Anti-trafficking provision also bars tools designed to circumvent. Tension with First Amendment, fair use, and right to repair has driven ongoing debate. Notable enforcement: DVD-Jon case (DeCSS), iPhone jailbreaking battles.

snappy: The federal government can't conscript state legislatures or officers to enforce federal law.
detail: New York v. United States (1992) struck down portions of federal radioactive-waste law. Printz v. United States (1997) struck down portions of the Brady gun-control law. Murphy v. NCAA (2018) extended to invalidate the federal sports-betting ban. Limits federal authority over states even when acting within constitutional powers. Allows federal preemption (displacing state law) but not affirmative state-actor commandeering.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Statutes that let defendants quickly dismiss lawsuits aimed at chilling protected speech or petition activity.
detail: California's anti-SLAPP statute (Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 425.16) is most influential; 30+ states have similar laws. Procedure: defendant moves to strike; plaintiff must show probability of prevailing. Successful motions get attorneys' fees. Federal anti-SLAPP legislation has been proposed repeatedly. Has been criticised as letting wealthy defendants escape accountability; recent reforms aim to narrow application to truly chilling cases.

snappy: Repudiating the contract before performance is due — the other side can sue immediately.
detail: Once one party clearly indicates they won't perform (statement or unequivocal act), the other can treat the contract as breached without waiting. Hochster v. De La Tour (1853) established the rule. Risky for the repudiator: misjudging the other side's response leads to liability. Retracting before the other side relies typically nullifies the repudiation.

snappy: Federal statute prohibiting government from spending money or incurring obligations without congressional appropriation.
detail: 31 U.S.C. §§ 1341–1342, 1349–1352. Cornerstone of separation-of-powers fiscal discipline. Triggers government shutdowns when appropriations lapse — non-essential federal functions suspended. Exceptions: emergencies involving the safety of human life or property. Criminal penalties for willful violations. Origin of the 'exceptions to the law' that keep essential services running during funding gaps.

snappy: Federal and state laws prohibiting monopolisation, anticompetitive agreements, and harmful mergers.
detail: Federal trio: Sherman Act, Clayton Act, FTC Act. State antitrust laws (often the 'Donnelly Act' in NY, Cartwright Act in CA, etc.) parallel federal. Civil enforcement by DOJ, FTC, state AGs, private plaintiffs. Criminal enforcement for hardcore cartel conduct (price-fixing, bid-rigging) by DOJ Antitrust Division. Treble damages and attorney fees for private plaintiffs. Lina Khan-era FTC pursued aggressive merger and conduct enforcement (2021-2024).

snappy: Asking a higher court to review and possibly overturn the trial court's decision.
detail: Appellate courts don't retry the facts; they review for legal error and abuse of discretion. The federal system has 13 circuit courts of appeals and the Supreme Court at the top. Most appeals affirm. A successful appeal can mean reversal, remand for a new trial, or modification of the judgment. Strict deadlines apply — usually 30 days from judgment in federal civil cases.

snappy: Dissenting shareholders can demand judicial determination of 'fair value' instead of accepting deal consideration.
detail: DGCL § 262 — extensive procedural requirements: pre-vote demand, no merger vote in favour, post-closing demand. Court of Chancery determines fair value via DCF, comparable companies, or other methodologies. Aruba Networks (Del. 2019) and Dell (Del. 2017) emphasised deal price as evidence in arm's-length transactions. Practice has waned post-Dell — courts now often defer to deal price absent process failures. Appraisal arbitrage strategy has declined.

snappy: Western US water doctrine: first user in time has first priority — 'first in time, first in right'.
detail: Replaces or supplements riparian rights in 17 Western states. Rights tied to actual beneficial use of the water; non-use can lead to forfeiture under 'use it or lose it' rules. Junior appropriators bear shortages during drought. Modern water markets in California, Colorado, and the Mountain West allow trading appropriative rights. Compactly governs interstate rivers like the Colorado, increasingly stressed by climate and population.

snappy: Standard for judicial review of informal agency action — courts overturn decisions that lack reasoned analysis or factual support.
detail: APA § 706(2)(A). Hard-look version under Motor Vehicle Manufacturers v. State Farm (1983): agency must examine relevant data, articulate satisfactory explanation, consider important aspects, not act counter to evidence. Increasingly stringent: DACA-rescission case (Regents, 2020) and census-citizenship-question case (Department of Commerce v. New York, 2019) reversed agency actions for inadequate reasoning. Pre-textual reasons can also trigger reversal.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: A common word used in a way unrelated to the product — 'Apple' for computers, 'Amazon' for retail.
detail: Among the strongest forms of mark — high distinctiveness without needing secondary meaning. Distinguishable from 'fanciful' marks (made-up words like 'Kodak', 'Xerox', 'Exxon') which sit at the very top of the strength spectrum. Both arbitrary and fanciful marks get the broadest protection, most easily enforced against later users. Combined with brand investment, they're commercially the most valuable category of mark.

snappy: Contractual provision requiring disputes to be resolved by private arbitration rather than court.
detail: Federal Arbitration Act (1925) generally requires courts to enforce. AT&T Mobility v. Concepcion (2011) and Epic Systems v. Lewis (2018) made class-action waivers in arbitration enforceable. Recent reform: Forced Arbitration Sexual Assault Act (2022) carved out sexual-assault and harassment claims. Cases involving consumer protection, employment, and antitrust often arbitrated despite policy concerns. Streamlined dispute resolution; criticism centres on access-to-justice and lack of public review.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: First court appearance after charging — defendant hears the charges and enters a plea.
detail: Defendant is advised of rights (right to counsel, right to remain silent), formally told the charges, and asked to plead guilty, not guilty, or no contest (nolo contendere). Bail is typically addressed at or near arraignment. Federal Rule of Criminal Procedure 5 governs initial federal appearances. State practice varies but the basic structure — advice, charges, plea — is universal.

snappy: Deliberately setting fire to a building or property — historically only dwellings, now most structures.
detail: Common-law arson required burning a dwelling; modern statutes cover commercial property, vehicles, and even one's own property if done to defraud insurers. Federal arson statute (18 U.S.C. § 844) requires interstate-commerce connection. Often charged alongside insurance fraud when an owner torches their own business. Sentencing escalates for occupied buildings and deaths. Forensic fire investigation has improved dramatically since the 1990s after wrongful convictions revealed flawed 'arson science'.

snappy: The corporate charter — filed with the state to create the corporation, contains foundational governance terms.
detail: Required contents under state law: corporate name, registered office, authorised capital stock, incorporator. Optional but common: classified board, supermajority voting requirements, indemnification, exculpation under DGCL § 102(b)(7), perpetual duration, dual-class stock, blank-check preferred. Amendments require shareholder approval. The senior governance document — bylaws can't conflict. The hardest governance document to change.

snappy: Intentionally causing apprehension of imminent harmful contact — no touching required.
detail: Tort assault is about the threat, not the impact (the impact is battery). A raised fist and a credible 'I'm going to hit you' qualifies; words alone usually don't unless paired with threatening gesture. Conditional threats ('Apologise or I'll punch you') can still be assault. Commonly pled together with battery: 'assault and battery'. Criminal assault statutes use the term more loosely, sometimes including actual contact.

snappy: Buying a company's assets rather than its stock — leaves liabilities (mostly) with the seller.
detail: Generally allows buyer to pick specific assets and contracts, reject others. Successor liability risks: product liability (continuity-of-enterprise doctrine), environmental (CERCLA), labour (some collective-bargaining contexts). Tax: buyer gets stepped-up basis in assets; seller may face dual-level tax. Contracts requiring assignment consent (anti-assignment clauses) complicate execution. Often used in distressed-company sales and carve-outs.

snappy: Transfer of contractual rights from one party to another.
detail: Generally permitted unless prohibited by contract or by the nature of the right (personal-services contracts can't be assigned). Anti-assignment clauses limit transfers but vary in strictness — some require consent, others bar absolutely. The assignor remains liable unless released. Common in M&A, financing (lenders assigning loan portfolios), and intellectual-property transactions.

snappy: Medical procedures helping people conceive — IVF, intrauterine insemination, donor gametes, gestational surrogacy.
detail: Largely unregulated at federal level beyond FDA oversight of clinical procedures. State laws govern parental rights, especially for donor-conceived children. Uniform Parentage Act 2017 update addresses ART. Religious-objection laws permit some providers to decline. Dobbs v. Jackson (2022) raised concerns about IVF embryo regulation — Alabama 2024 Supreme Court ruling threatened access (legislatively addressed). Donor anonymity increasingly challenged by direct-to-consumer DNA testing.

snappy: Plaintiff knew of and voluntarily accepted a risk — bars or reduces recovery.
detail: Express (signed waiver — football, ski lift, gym) and implied (continuing to act despite known risk). Murphy v. Steeplechase Amusement (1929) — boardwalk ride: 'The Flopper, you take what you find.' Largely subsumed into comparative-fault in many jurisdictions. Express waivers under public-policy and gross-negligence challenges. Sports and recreational liability cases the dominant modern application.

snappy: Protection for someone in the US fearing persecution on protected grounds in their home country.
detail: Standard: well-founded fear of persecution on account of race, religion, nationality, political opinion, or membership in particular social group. Filed with USCIS (affirmative) within one year of arrival or in removal proceedings (defensive) at any time. Discretionary grant. Successful applicants can apply for LPR after one year. Recent restrictive policies: third-country transit bars, expedited credible-fear screenings, expanded country-of-origin disqualifications. Surge in southern-border claims drove policy changes through 2024.

snappy: Default US rule: employment can be terminated by either party at any time, for any reason or no reason — with exceptions.
detail: Exceptions: contracts setting fixed term or 'for cause' termination; statutory anti-discrimination protections; public-policy exceptions (firing for jury duty, whistleblowing, refusing illegal act). Montana is the only state without at-will: requires 'good cause' for termination after probationary period. Implied-contract exceptions in some states (employee handbooks creating expectations). Federal/state employees typically have civil-service protections that override at-will principle.

snappy: Trying but failing to commit a crime — punished almost as severely as the completed offence in most jurisdictions.
detail: Requires intent to commit the underlying crime plus a 'substantial step' toward its completion (Model Penal Code § 5.01). Mere preparation isn't enough; the dividing line is often hotly contested. Impossibility is generally not a defence — attempting to buy what you think is heroin but is actually flour still gets you charged. Sentences track the completed crime closely, with modest reductions in some federal contexts.

snappy: Communications between a lawyer and client for legal advice are confidential and can't be forced into evidence.
detail: One of the oldest evidentiary privileges in common law. Covers communications, not underlying facts — you can be asked what you did, just not what you told your lawyer. Waived by sharing with third parties or putting the advice 'at issue' (e.g., 'I relied on counsel'). Crime-fraud exception: privilege doesn't shield communications in furtherance of a crime. Corporate privilege governed by Upjohn v. United States (1981).

snappy: Special duty to protect children from artificial hazards on property — swimming pools, abandoned refrigerators, construction sites.
detail: Restatement (Second) of Torts § 339. Five elements: owner knows children likely to trespass; condition involves unreasonable risk; children unlikely to appreciate risk; burden of remedy is slight relative to risk; owner failed to exercise reasonable care. Modifies the traditional rule that trespassers (including child trespassers) get minimal duty. Foundation of mandatory pool-fencing ordinances and gate-locking requirements.

snappy: Courts defer to agency interpretations of their own ambiguous regulations.
detail: Auer v. Robbins (1997). Kisor v. Wilkie (2019) limited but didn't overrule — required regulation to be genuinely ambiguous, interpretation to reflect agency's substantive expertise, consider whether it's the agency's authoritative position. Distinct from Chevron's coverage of statutes. Survived the Loper Bright Chevron overruling but expected to face renewed challenges. Hot-button for regulated industries where agencies effectively rewrite rules through interpretation.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Showing that evidence is what its proponent claims it is — the signature is really hers, the document is really from that file.
detail: Federal Rule of Evidence 901. A low bar: 'evidence sufficient to support a finding' the item is what proponent says. Methods include witness familiarity, distinctive characteristics, chain of custody, and self-authenticating categories under Rule 902 (certified public records, business records, newspapers). Digital evidence brings new authentication challenges — metadata, hash values, and forensic analysts have become routine.

snappy: The maximum number of shares the corporation can issue — set in the charter, can be amended by shareholder vote.
detail: Distinct from issued-and-outstanding shares (those actually held by shareholders). Headroom available for future equity raises, stock-option grants, stock-split capacity. DGCL franchise tax based partly on authorised capital. Companies routinely authorise far more shares than they currently use to avoid future amendment costs. Common: 100 million or 500 million for small-cap, billions for large-cap public companies.

snappy: Filing bankruptcy immediately halts most collection actions against the debtor — without further court action.
detail: 11 U.S.C. § 362. Stops: lawsuits, foreclosures, repossessions, evictions, wage garnishments, collection calls. Exceptions: criminal proceedings, family-law domestic-support obligations, certain tax actions. Repeat-filer limitations to prevent abuse (limited stay for second filing in a year; no stay for third). 'Relief from stay' available to creditors with cause (e.g., adequate protection lacking for secured creditor). Major source of immediate relief — bankruptcy's primary therapeutic feature.

snappy: Police can search a vehicle without a warrant if they have probable cause it contains evidence of crime.
detail: Carroll v. United States (1925). The justification is vehicle mobility — by the time police get a warrant, the car (and evidence) is gone. California v. Acevedo (1991) extended the rule to containers within vehicles. Scope: anywhere the suspected evidence could be. Doesn't authorise body searches of passengers (without separate cause) or extend to vehicles in driveways with Fourth Amendment 'curtilage' protection (Collins v. Virginia, 2018).
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Pre-employment investigation of candidate's history — criminal records, credit, education, employment verification.
detail: Federal Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA) governs use of consumer reports: written authorisation required, adverse-action notice required if disqualifying. 'Ban the box' laws restrict timing of criminal-history inquiries — over 35 states and many cities. EEOC guidance on disparate-impact of criminal-records screening. Continued evolution: California Sec. 1024.5 restrictions on credit reports, marijuana-conviction exemptions, fair-chance hiring expansion through 2024.

snappy: Money or conditions allowing an arrested person to be released pending trial.
detail: Set at the bail hearing based on flight risk and danger to the community. Cash bail releases on payment; bond uses a bail-bondsman (typically 10% non-refundable fee). Recognizance release means no money required — your promise to appear. Bail reform efforts (NY, NJ, CA) have largely eliminated cash bail for most misdemeanours, citing wealth-based detention concerns. Federal system uses a presumption-of-detention model for serious charges.

snappy: Temporary transfer of personal property to someone else's care — without transferring ownership.
detail: Examples: leaving your car with a valet, dropping clothes at a dry cleaner, storing items in a warehouse, parking-garage attendant. Three classes by benefit: bailor benefit (gratuitous storage), bailee benefit (borrowed property), mutual benefit (paid storage). Duty of care varies by class — lighter for gratuitous bailment, higher for paid. UCC Article 7 governs warehouse-receipt bailments. Common-law foundation of modern self-storage litigation.

snappy: The federal statute governing bankruptcy proceedings in the US.
detail: 11 U.S.C. Created in modern form by Bankruptcy Reform Act of 1978; major reform Bankruptcy Abuse Prevention and Consumer Protection Act (BAPCPA, 2005). Federal preempts state insolvency law in most respects. Administered by bankruptcy courts (units of the federal district courts) plus US Trustee Program oversight. Five main chapters: 7 (liquidation), 11 (reorganisation), 12 (family farm/fisherman), 13 (wage-earner plan), 15 (cross-border).

snappy: Specialised federal courts handling bankruptcy cases — units of the federal district courts.
detail: Bankruptcy judges appointed for 14-year terms by Court of Appeals (not Article III). Jurisdiction over 'core' bankruptcy matters (cases, plan confirmation, claim allowance) and 'non-core' matters with consent or as related to bankruptcy case. Stern v. Marshall (2011) constrained authority on non-core matters. Direct appeals to district court or bankruptcy appellate panel. National Bankruptcy Conference and ABI propose reforms periodically.

snappy: An official appointed in bankruptcy cases to administer the estate, investigate the debtor, and represent creditors' interests.
detail: Chapter 7: trustee liquidates non-exempt assets, distributes proceeds. Chapter 13: standing trustee receives debtor payments, distributes per plan. Chapter 11: usually no trustee (debtor in possession); trustee appointed for fraud or gross mismanagement. Selected from private-trustee panels (consumer cases) or US Trustee approval (business cases). Compensation tied to estate value distributed. Pivotal role in identifying fraudulent transfers and concealed assets.

snappy: An objection that the other side used a peremptory strike to remove a juror because of race or sex.
detail: From Batson v. Kentucky (1986). Three-step framework: prima facie case of discrimination; opponent offers race-neutral reason; court decides whether the reason is genuine or pretextual. The doctrine has been criticised as toothless — 'race-neutral' reasons (e.g., 'he looked bored') are routinely accepted. Flowers v. Mississippi (2019) reversed a conviction after the prosecutor struck Black jurors in six successive trials.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Intentionally making harmful or offensive physical contact without consent.
detail: Distinct from criminal battery; a tort battery doesn't require injury, just unwanted contact. A doctor operating without informed consent commits battery. So does the pickpocket who never threatens harm. The contact can be indirect — spitting, throwing a punch that lands. Doctrinal cousin: assault (creating apprehension of imminent contact, even without contact). Often pled together as 'assault and battery'.

snappy: What happens when buyer and seller swap conflicting standard forms — the UCC's pragmatic answer.
detail: UCC § 2-207 rejects the strict 'mirror image rule' for sale-of-goods contracts. Acceptance with different terms still forms a contract unless made expressly conditional on assent to new terms. Extra terms become part of the contract between merchants unless they materially alter, the offer expressly limits acceptance, or objection is made. Decades of litigation over which forms control which terms. Modern CISG and Restatement (Second) handle differently.

snappy: A trial decided by the judge alone, with no jury.
detail: Right to jury trial exists in federal criminal cases (Sixth Amendment) and most civil cases at common law (Seventh Amendment) but is routinely waived. Federal Rule 38 governs jury demand; failure to demand timely waives the right. Bench trials are faster, cheaper, and unfold differently — less theatre, more legal argument. Findings of fact get clear-error review on appeal under Rule 52.

snappy: A corporate form requiring directors to consider public benefit alongside shareholder profit.
detail: Adopted by 35+ US states starting with Maryland (2010). Directors must consider impact on workers, community, environment in decision-making. Annual benefit report required. Distinct from 'Certified B Corporation' (third-party certification by B Lab — independent of legal form). Patagonia, Allbirds, Warby Parker, Kickstarter are well-known examples. Provides defensive shield against shareholder claims that mission-driven decisions violate fiduciary duty.

snappy: The 1886 international treaty creating reciprocal copyright protection across member countries.
detail: Administered by WIPO; over 180 member countries. Sets minimum protections: life-of-author-plus-50-years term (US extended to life-plus-70), no formalities required for protection (US joined 1989, eliminating the notice requirement). 'National treatment': member states give foreign works the same protection as their own. TRIPS Agreement (1994) incorporated Berne into the WTO framework, adding enforcement teeth.

snappy: To prove the content of a writing, the original document is required (with limited exceptions).
detail: Federal Rules of Evidence 1001–1008. Modern application is narrow because Rule 1003 freely admits duplicates unless authenticity is genuinely disputed, and Rule 1004 allows other evidence if the original is lost or destroyed without bad faith. The rule still bites in document forgery cases and disputed contract terms. Misnamed — it's not about the 'best' evidence generally, only proof of writings' content.

snappy: The legal standard for custody decisions — what's best for the child trumps parental preferences.
detail: Factors vary by state but commonly: parent-child relationship, child's emotional ties to siblings and community, parents' physical and mental health, stability of home environment, ability to provide for the child, history of domestic violence or substance abuse, child's preference (age-dependent), continuity. Heavy judicial discretion. Custody evaluators (psychologists or social workers) often conduct independent investigations and make recommendations.

snappy: A promise to use reasonable best efforts to achieve a result — softer than guarantee, harder than mere good faith.
detail: Standard in distribution agreements, M&A regulatory clearance commitments, exclusive-dealing arrangements. 'Best efforts' interpretations vary by jurisdiction: Delaware's strict 'best efforts' requires more than reasonable efforts (Macy's v. Strategic Resources, Del. 2014); 'reasonable best efforts' is more common middle ground. Drafting carefully matters: 'reasonable best efforts', 'commercially reasonable efforts', 'good faith efforts' all signal different levels of commitment.

snappy: Splitting a divorce into two parts — granting the divorce status itself first, deciding remaining issues (property, support, custody) later.
detail: Status of being divorced separable from financial and custody resolution. Reasons: remarriage planned, emotional closure, tax filing status, end-of-life concerns. Granted in some states without showing of compelling reason; others require showing of hardship. Risk: post-bifurcation negotiating leverage may shift. California restricted bifurcation after 2008 reforms requiring specific protections (health insurance continuation, undertakings).

snappy: The first ten amendments to the Constitution, ratified 1791.
detail: Originally limited federal government only (Barron v. Baltimore, 1833). After the Fourteenth Amendment (1868), the Supreme Court 'incorporated' most provisions against the states one by one — process effectively complete by the late 20th century, with Bruen (2022) incorporating the Second Amendment against state gun regulations. Some provisions remain unincorporated (grand-jury requirement, civil-jury right above $20).

snappy: A judicially created cause of action for damages against federal officers who violate constitutional rights.
detail: Bivens v. Six Unknown Named Agents (1971) — Fourth Amendment search; Davis v. Passman (1979) — Fifth Amendment equal-protection; Carlson v. Green (1980) — Eighth Amendment medical care. Modern Court is hostile to expansion: Ziglar v. Abbasi (2017) and Hernandez v. Mesa (2020) refused new contexts. Egbert v. Boule (2022) further narrowed. The federal-officer equivalent of § 1983 (which reaches state officers) — but with much more limited reach.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: The body of elected (or appointed) directors who oversee a corporation — the senior governance organ.
detail: Powers: appoint and oversee officers, approve major transactions, set strategic direction, manage external relations. Members elected by shareholders (typically annually) under voting rules in charter and state statute. Modern boards include committees: audit, compensation, nominating/governance. Diversity (gender, race) increasingly subject to disclosure and listing requirements (Nasdaq's controversial board diversity rule was struck down in 2024).

snappy: An employer can hire based on a protected characteristic if it's essential to the job's normal operation.
detail: Narrow Title VII exception. Recognised for sex in narrowly defined contexts: religious clergy, actors playing specific roles, restroom attendants. Sex isn't a BFOQ for general physical labour even if women, on average, would have lower productivity (Dothard v. Rawlinson, 1977 — Alabama prison guard exception was narrow). Religion is BFOQ for clergy in religious organisations (ministerial exception, Hosanna-Tabor, 2012). No BFOQ for race or colour.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: A buyer who pays value for property in good faith without notice of prior claims — protected by recording acts.
detail: Three requirements: paid value (not a gift), in good faith (no actual fraud), without notice (actual, constructive, or inquiry). BFP status defeats prior unrecorded interests under most recording statutes. Critical in foreclosure-sale challenges and fraud unwinding — even a fraudulent transfer can be insulated by a downstream BFP. Heart of the policy choice protecting commerce over absolute property protection.

snappy: Shareholder's statutory right to inspect corporate books and records for a 'proper purpose'.
detail: Delaware General Corporation Law § 220. Proper purposes include investigating potential mismanagement, valuing shares, communicating with other stockholders. Stockholders frequently use § 220 demands as predicate for derivative-litigation 'tools at hand' analysis. Stockholder must establish credible basis of wrongdoing for inspection of corporate documents. Recent decisions: AmerisourceBergen (Del. Ch. 2020) broadened scope; KT4 Partners v. Palantir (Del. 2019) addressed email production.

snappy: Failing to perform a contractual obligation when performance was due.
detail: Material breach excuses the other side from continuing performance and triggers damages. Minor breach (substantial performance) still entitles the non-breacher to damages but not to walk away. Anticipatory breach — making clear you won't perform before the time arrives — lets the other side sue immediately. The choice between repudiation and waiting matters: act too early and you're the breaching party.

snappy: Falling below the standard of care — the negligent conduct itself.
detail: Second element of negligence. Standard varies by context: reasonable person, professional standard of care (doctors, lawyers, accountants), industry custom (relevant but not dispositive — T.J. Hooper, 1932). Hand formula (United States v. Carroll Towing, 1947) frames breach as B < PL (burden less than probability × loss). Custom usually defines minimum care; can exceed legal minimum. Failure of statutory duty supports negligence per se.

snappy: Offering, giving, soliciting, or accepting something of value to influence a public official's actions.
detail: Federal statute (18 U.S.C. § 201) covers federal officials; state laws cover state-and-local. Requires a 'quid pro quo' connection. McDonnell v. United States (2016) narrowed 'official act' to formal exercises of government power, weakening prosecutions of routine influence-peddling. Foreign Corrupt Practices Act extends US bribery law to overseas business deals. Senator Bob Menendez convicted in 2024 for accepting gold bars and cash.

snappy: Online terms purportedly accepted just by using a site — no affirmative click required.
detail: Weakest form of online contract. Courts require evidence that the user had actual or constructive knowledge of the terms — typically a clear and conspicuous notice. Many browse-wraps fail enforcement because the link to terms is buried in the footer. Nguyen v. Barnes & Noble (2014) refused to enforce a browse-wrap arbitration clause for this reason. Modern litigation heavy: SCOTUS denied cert on several browse-wrap cases through 2024.

snappy: Which side has to prove its case, and how convincingly.
detail: In US civil cases the plaintiff usually has to prove its case by a 'preponderance of the evidence' — more likely than not. Criminal prosecutions are much harder: 'beyond a reasonable doubt'. Some civil claims (fraud, deportation, parental-rights termination) sit in between at 'clear and convincing'. Who carries the burden, and at what level, often decides the case before any facts are weighed.

snappy: Entering a building (without permission) intending to commit a crime inside — historically required nighttime entry into a dwelling.
detail: Common-law burglary required breaking and entering at night with intent to commit a felony. Modern statutes drop most of these requirements: any unauthorised entry of a structure (including cars in some states) with criminal intent qualifies. Entry can be aggravated by weapons, occupancy, or time of day. The crime is complete on entry — the underlying crime needn't be committed.

snappy: Courts presume directors acted in good faith on an informed basis — shielding most business decisions from judicial second-guessing.
detail: Powerful presumption: plaintiff must show no rational business purpose, lack of independence, lack of good faith, or grossly uninformed decision. Aronson v. Lewis (Del. 1984) elaborated the standard. Doesn't apply to self-dealing transactions (entire-fairness review applies). Survived (and arguably strengthened) modern case law. Foundation of US corporate director protection — much more protective than UK or European equivalents.

snappy: The corporation's internal operating rules — meeting procedures, officer roles, voting standards, indemnification.
detail: Adopted by initial board (or shareholders if state law allows); generally amendable by board. DGCL § 109. Common provisions: shareholder meeting procedures, director election, advance notice of nominations, board committees, indemnification, forum-selection (Delaware-only litigation). Activist battles often involve bylaw amendments. Modern flashpoint: 'exclusive forum' bylaws making Delaware Chancery the exclusive venue for internal-affairs litigation.

snappy: A standard corporation taxed separately from its owners — the default Subchapter C structure.
detail: Internal Revenue Code Subchapter C. Profits taxed at federal corporate rate (21% since TCJA 2017). Dividends to shareholders taxed again at individual rates — 'double taxation'. Foreign owners and unlimited shareholder count permitted. Multiple stock classes allowed. Public companies, most VC-backed startups, and large operating businesses typically use C corp structure for access to capital markets and qualified-small-business-stock benefits.

snappy: Discretionary relief from removal for certain long-term US residents who meet specific criteria.
detail: Two main types: LPR cancellation (5 years as LPR, 7 years continuous residence, no aggravated felony — INA § 240A(a)); Non-LPR cancellation (10 years continuous physical presence, good moral character, exceptional and extremely unusual hardship to qualifying US-citizen or LPR family member — INA § 240A(b)). Annual cap: 4,000 non-LPR cancellations granted. Discretionary even when statutory eligibility met. Severe restrictions for those with criminal histories.

snappy: Whether a party is legally able to enter a contract at all.
detail: Minors (under 18 in most states) generally can disaffirm contracts; they're still liable for necessaries (food, shelter, basic medical). Mental incapacity — inability to understand the nature and consequences — also makes a contract voidable. Intoxication only voids if the other party knew. Corporations have capacity to contract within their charter; ultra-vires doctrines are now largely vestigial.

snappy: Tax on profit from selling appreciated property — stocks, real estate, businesses, collectibles.
detail: Long-term gains (held over a year): 0%, 15%, or 20% based on income (plus 3.8% Net Investment Income Tax over thresholds). Short-term gains (held a year or less): taxed at ordinary income rates. Real estate principal residence: $250K single / $500K joint exclusion under § 121. Stepped-up basis at death (potentially) eliminates capital gains for inherited property. § 1031 like-kind exchange defers gains on real-property exchanges. Major political flashpoint for tax-rate parity debates.

snappy: Directors' obligation to monitor company operations and information systems — failure to oversee can be a duty-of-loyalty breach.
detail: In re Caremark (Del. Ch. 1996) established the framework. Stone v. Ritter (Del. 2006) confirmed Caremark sits within duty of loyalty. Two paths to liability: utterly failing to implement reporting/information systems, or consciously failing to monitor. Marchand v. Barnhill (Del. 2019) revived Caremark claims — Blue Bell ice-cream listeria deaths showed inadequate food-safety oversight. Recent decisions have raised bar for board attention to 'mission-critical risks'.

snappy: The injury wouldn't have happened 'but for' the defendant's conduct — the factual link between act and harm.
detail: Third element of negligence (preceding proximate cause). Tested by: would the harm have occurred without defendant's conduct? Multiple-cause scenarios: substantial-factor test (Anderson v. Minneapolis, 1920 — two converging fires). Alternative liability (Summers v. Tice, 1948 — two hunters shooting). Market-share liability (Sindell v. Abbott Labs, DES cases, 1980) for indistinguishable product origin.

snappy: An order or letter demanding immediate stop to specified conduct.
detail: Often the first step in trademark, copyright, or false-advertising disputes — cheaper than litigation and sometimes resolves the matter. Agency cease-and-desist orders carry legal force; private ones are leverage but require a lawsuit to enforce. Recipients should respond thoughtfully — silence is often interpreted as defiance, while admissions can boomerang in later litigation.

full: Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation, and Liability Act / Superfund
snappy: Federal statute for cleaning up hazardous-waste sites and assigning cleanup costs.
detail: 42 U.S.C. §§ 9601 et seq. (1980). Imposes strict, joint, and several liability on Potentially Responsible Parties (PRPs): current owners/operators, past owners/operators, generators of waste, transporters. National Priorities List ranks worst sites. Innocent-landowner and bona fide prospective purchaser defenses important for real-estate transactions. State 'mini-CERCLA' statutes parallel federal. Massive litigation generator for decades; cleanup costs running into hundreds of billions.

snappy: Business bankruptcy emphasising reorganisation rather than liquidation — debtor typically stays in control.
detail: Larger business bankruptcies (Lehman, Enron, Toys R Us, FTX, WeWork). Debtor remains 'debtor in possession' with management retained, subject to court and creditor oversight. Automatic stay halts collection. Negotiation of reorganisation plan with creditors organised into classes. Plan confirmation requires majority creditor support per class plus 'absolute priority rule' for crammed-down classes. Subchapter V (added 2020) provides streamlined Chapter 11 for small business under $7.5M debt.

snappy: Consumer bankruptcy where debtor pays back creditors over 3-5 years under court-approved plan.
detail: Available to individuals with regular income — debt limits ($2.75M total 2024). Common reason: preserve home or car threatened by foreclosure or repossession. Plan repays priority debts (taxes, child support) in full, secured debts according to terms, unsecured creditors what disposable income allows. Discharge after plan completion. Statistically, only about 1/3 of Chapter 13 plans complete successfully. Filing fee about $313.

snappy: The 'fresh start' bankruptcy — assets liquidated (with exemptions), most debts discharged.
detail: Most common consumer bankruptcy. Trustee sells non-exempt assets (federal or state exemptions: homestead, retirement accounts, basic necessities). Proceeds distributed to creditors. Discharge typically 4-6 months after filing. BAPCPA (2005) added 'means test' to qualify — income above state median triggers presumption Chapter 13 required. Some debts non-dischargeable: most student loans, child support, recent taxes, fraud-based debts. Filing fee about $338.

snappy: Generally inadmissible to prove a person acted 'in conformity' with a character trait on a specific occasion.
detail: Federal Rule of Evidence 404. The classic 'propensity' bar: prosecution can't introduce defendant's prior fraud convictions to show he committed this fraud. Exceptions: defendant opens the door (404(a)(2)), prior acts admissible for motive/intent/identity/absence-of-mistake (404(b)), prior sexual assaults and child molestation get special treatment (Rules 413–415). Witness credibility character has its own complex rules (608–609).

snappy: Federal courts deferred to reasonable agency interpretations of ambiguous statutes — overruled by Loper Bright (2024).
detail: Chevron v. NRDC (1984) two-step test: (1) is the statute ambiguous? If yes, (2) is the agency interpretation reasonable? Foundation of administrative law for 40 years. Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo (2024) overruled Chevron, returning interpretive authority to courts under Skidmore-style respect rather than binding deference. Massive doctrinal shift; existing decisions relying on Chevron remain valid under stare decisis but new statutory-interpretation cases now governed by the court's reading.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: The legal right and responsibility to care for and make decisions about a child after parents separate.
detail: Two components: legal custody (decision-making — education, healthcare, religion) and physical custody (where child lives). Standard: 'best interest of the child', considering parent-child relationship, stability, parental fitness, child's preference (age-dependent), and many other factors. Joint custody now favoured in most states; sole custody reserved for cases with significant parental impairment. UCCJEA governs jurisdiction across state lines.

snappy: State agency investigating suspected child abuse or neglect and protecting children when found.
detail: Federal Child Abuse Prevention and Treatment Act funds state programs. Mandatory reporter laws require teachers, doctors, etc. to report suspected abuse. Investigation involves home visits, interviews with child and family. Options: case closed unfounded, voluntary services, court intervention (dependency proceedings), removal to foster care. ASFA timelines drive permanency planning. Critics: over-policing of poor and minority families; defenders: protecting vulnerable children. Major recent reforms emphasise prevention and family preservation.

snappy: State formulas calculating child-support amounts — typically based on parental income and time-sharing.
detail: Federal Child Support Enforcement Act (1984) required states to establish guidelines. Two main models: income-shares (most states — combines both parents' incomes, allocates by income ratio) and percentage of obligor income (Wisconsin and few others — fixed percentage of non-custodial parent's income). Deviations allowed with court findings: extraordinary medical needs, shared physical custody adjustments, very high or low incomes, multiple-family obligations. Modification possible upon income changes.

snappy: A tax credit per qualifying child under 17 — partially refundable.
detail: $2,000 per child (2024 rules — post-TCJA, pre-expiration). Up to $1,700 refundable per child as Additional Child Tax Credit. Phase-out begins at AGI of $200K single / $400K joint. American Rescue Plan (2021) temporarily expanded to $3,000-$3,600 per child and made fully refundable; reverted afterward. Expansion vs reversion drives major poverty-policy debates. Companion Credit for Other Dependents ($500) for non-child dependents.

snappy: The status of being a US citizen — by birth (Fourteenth Amendment / INA § 301), descent, or naturalisation.
detail: Rights: vote, run for office, US passport, federal employment, federal jury service, immigration benefits for family. Cannot generally be deported. Acquired automatically by birth in US (jus soli, Fourteenth Amendment) or to US-citizen parents abroad meeting transmission rules (jus sanguinis, INA § 301-308). Naturalisation conveys citizenship to qualified LPRs. Loss possible only through voluntary renunciation or affirmative acts (treason, fraud during naturalisation).

snappy: A legal relationship resembling marriage — recognised by some states before same-sex marriage and as parallel option in some today.
detail: Largely superseded by same-sex marriage post-Obergefell. Still available in some states (Hawaii, Illinois, New Jersey, Colorado) often as option for same-sex or opposite-sex couples. Federal recognition limited — civil unions historically didn't trigger federal marriage benefits even where state-recognised. Some states converted existing civil unions to marriages automatically post-Obergefell.

snappy: A lawsuit where one plaintiff sues on behalf of a large group with similar claims.
detail: Governed by Federal Rule 23: commonality, typicality, adequacy of representation, and either predominance (for damages classes) or cohesion (for injunctive classes). Common in consumer fraud, antitrust, and securities cases. The Supreme Court's Wal-Mart v. Dukes (2011) and Comcast v. Behrend (2013) tightened certification. After AT&T v. Concepcion (2011), arbitration clauses with class waivers have shrunk the class-action footprint in consumer disputes.

snappy: The court's decision to allow a case to proceed as a class action.
detail: The make-or-break moment in class litigation — often more consequential than trial. Plaintiffs must prove the Rule 23 requirements with evidence; defendants attack typicality, predominance, and trial manageability. Denial of certification usually ends the case. Granting certification often forces settlement because individual exposure becomes a class-wide exposure. Interlocutory appeals available under Rule 23(f).

snappy: Contract clause requiring individual arbitration — banning class or collective actions.
detail: AT&T Mobility v. Concepcion (2011) upheld class-action waivers in consumer contracts; Epic Systems v. Lewis (2018) upheld in employment contracts under FAA preemption of state laws. Effective tool for companies facing low-value, high-volume disputes (cell-phone billing, banking fees, low-dollar employment claims). 'Mass arbitration' has emerged as plaintiffs' counter-tactic — thousands of individual filings imposing significant filing-fee burden on companies.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Federal antitrust statute addressing specific conduct — mergers, exclusive dealing, tying, interlocking directorates.
detail: 15 U.S.C. §§ 12–27 (1914). Section 7 governs merger review — agencies challenge deals 'substantially lessening competition'. Section 3 prohibits exclusive-dealing and tying arrangements with similar effect. Section 8 bars interlocking directorates between competitors. Foundation of merger enforcement at FTC and DOJ. Treble damages available to private plaintiffs. The premerger notification regime under Hart-Scott-Rodino is technically under § 7A.

snappy: Federal statute regulating air pollution — administered by the EPA.
detail: 42 U.S.C. §§ 7401 et seq. Originally 1970, major amendments 1990. Sets National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS) for six 'criteria pollutants'. Title V permits for major stationary sources. New Source Review for new and modified facilities. Climate-change regulation under Massachusetts v. EPA (2007) — greenhouse gases reach 'air pollutant' definition. West Virginia v. EPA (2022) and Sackett v. EPA narrowed EPA's reach. Major-questions doctrine applications in climate context.

snappy: Federal statute regulating discharges to and protection of US surface waters.
detail: 33 U.S.C. §§ 1251 et seq. Originally 1972, major amendments 1977. Section 402 establishes NPDES permits for point-source discharges. Section 404 governs dredge-and-fill permits in 'waters of the United States' (WOTUS). Sackett v. EPA (2023) sharply limited WOTUS definition to 'continuous-surface-connection' waters. State implementation varies. Civil and criminal penalties available; citizen-suit provision empowers NGOs to enforce.

snappy: Online contract requiring affirmative click ('I agree') to accept terms.
detail: The gold standard for online contract formation. Courts almost uniformly enforce clickwrap when the terms are reasonably available and the user must take an affirmative action signalling assent. Specht v. Netscape (2002) is the foundational case distinguishing clickwrap from browse-wrap. Modern best practice: 'unmissable' button, terms visible or linked clearly, button text clearly indicating assent.

snappy: Events that must occur before a buyer is obligated to complete an acquisition — regulatory approval, no MAC, accurate reps.
detail: Common conditions: HSR clearance, third-party consents, no MAC, accuracy of representations, performance of covenants, no injunction. Specific deal: shareholder approval, key-employee retention, tax opinion, financing. Buyer's leverage: walk if conditions fail; seller's interest: tight, narrow conditions. Reverse-termination fees and specific-performance remedies allocate risk if buyer claims condition failure. 2020-2024 saw heavy litigation over MAC claims in pandemic and inflation contexts.

snappy: Federal statute letting workers continue group health insurance for a limited time after job loss — at full cost.
detail: 29 U.S.C. § 1161 et seq. (1986). 18-month default continuation (29 months for disability, 36 months for some events). Premium: full cost plus 2% administration fee. Notice requirements strict — failure triggers $110/day penalty plus statutory damages. Election period 60 days. Premiums prohibitive for many: $700-2,000+ monthly for family coverage often unaffordable post-job-loss. ACA marketplaces have provided lower-cost alternatives for many.

snappy: Both spouses and their attorneys commit to negotiating a settlement out of court — if it fails, the lawyers must withdraw.
detail: Distinguished from regular settlement negotiations by the binding disqualification provision — creates incentive for compromise. Process includes neutral specialists: financial advisor, child specialist, mental-health counsellor, mediator. Heavily reliant on transparency and good faith — both spouses fully disclose assets and concerns. Successful collaborative divorces typically faster and cheaper than litigation; failures expensive (lose lawyers, restart). Most popular in higher-asset, professional-class divorces.

snappy: Negotiating wages, hours, and working conditions between an employer and a union representing employees.
detail: NLRA § 8(d) requires good-faith bargaining over mandatory subjects: wages, hours, terms and conditions. Permissive subjects (corporate decisions) and illegal subjects (closed shops in right-to-work states) handled differently. Collective bargaining agreements (CBAs) typically 3-5 year terms. Grievance and arbitration provisions handle CBA disputes. Recent organising surges at Starbucks, Amazon, REI, university teaching assistants — challenges around bargaining-table tactics and refusal-to-bargain charges.

snappy: Article I power letting Congress regulate commerce 'among the several States' — basis for most federal regulation.
detail: Wickard v. Filburn (1942) read it broadly enough to reach a farmer's home wheat consumption. United States v. Lopez (1995) and Morrison (2000) imposed limits — required activity to be economic. NFIB v. Sebelius (2012) held the ACA's individual mandate exceeded the Commerce Clause but was a valid tax. The clause underpins federal authority over civil rights, environmental, securities, and labour regulation.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: UCC's relaxed version of impossibility — performance excused if a basic assumption fails.
detail: UCC § 2-615: a seller can be excused if performance has become impracticable by occurrence of a contingency the non-occurrence of which was a basic assumption. Bare price increases aren't enough; the change has to be extreme and unforeseeable. The pandemic revived the doctrine; supply-chain shortages and force-majeure clauses overlapped extensively. Common-law contracts use the parallel Restatement § 261 standard.

snappy: Law developed by judges through court decisions — based on precedent rather than statute.
detail: Inherited from English legal tradition by all US states except Louisiana (mixed civil-law system). Foundation of property, contracts, torts, and many other areas. Distinguished from civil-law systems (continental Europe, Latin America) which prioritise comprehensive codes. Modern common law coexists with statutory law and administrative regulations — increasingly statutes dominate in many areas. Federal courts in diversity apply state common law (Erie Railroad v. Tompkins, 1938).

snappy: The standard class of corporate ownership — last to be paid in liquidation, but the residual claimant on profits.
detail: Typical rights: vote (usually one per share), residual claim on assets after creditors and preferred, no fixed dividend (board declares from earnings). Foundation of most public-company capitalisation. Dual-class structures (Class A vs Class B with different voting power) raise governance debates. New listings increasingly use dual-class (founders retain control); proxy advisors and index providers push back.

snappy: Marriage recognised without ceremony or licence — based on cohabitation and presenting as spouses.
detail: Now recognised by only ~10 US states (Colorado, Iowa, Kansas, Montana, Oklahoma, Rhode Island, South Carolina, Texas, Utah, plus DC). Once formed, common-law marriages are full marriages for all purposes — requiring divorce to dissolve. Recognised states require: intent to be married, holding out as married, cohabitation, capacity. Other states recognise common-law marriages validly formed in another state under full-faith-and-credit principles.

snappy: Federal statute creating the FCC and regulating wired and wireless communications.
detail: 47 U.S.C. §§ 151 et seq. Title II governs common carriers (traditional telephony, sometimes broadband under net-neutrality debates). Title III governs broadcasting and licensing. Title VI governs cable. Section 230 (added 1996) immunises online platforms from third-party content liability — one of the most consequential internet-law provisions. Modern updates: Telecommunications Act of 1996 deregulated broadly; major rewriting proposals through 2024.

snappy: A property regime in 9 US states presuming assets and income earned during marriage are jointly owned 50-50.
detail: States: Arizona, California, Idaho, Louisiana, Nevada, New Mexico, Texas, Washington, Wisconsin (plus Alaska as opt-in). Property acquired before marriage and gifts/inheritances remain separate. Death and divorce both implicate community-property rules. Income from separate property: community in California; separate in Texas. Quasi-community-property doctrines handle moves between regimes. Significant impact on estate planning and divorce settlements.

snappy: Damages are reduced by the plaintiff's share of fault — the modern rule in most states.
detail: Replaces older contributory-negligence rule. Two flavours: 'pure' (recovery reduced by any percentage of plaintiff fault, even 99%) — used in California, Florida, New York; 'modified' (no recovery if plaintiff is 50% or 51%+ at fault) — used by most states. Apportionment usually a jury question. Set-offs apply against awards. Cigarette and asbestos litigation rewrote much modern comparative-fault doctrine.

snappy: A work formed by collecting and arranging preexisting materials — protectable in the original selection and arrangement.
detail: 17 U.S.C. § 103. Copyright in a compilation extends only to the compiler's contribution, not the underlying materials. Anthology editors, database designers, and curated playlist creators rely on this protection. Feist (1991) eliminated 'sweat of the brow' as a basis — selection or arrangement must show originality. EU's sui generis database right has no US analogue, leaving US database makers more reliant on contract law.

snappy: The opening document of a lawsuit — what the plaintiff says happened and what they want.
detail: Filed in court, served on the defendant. It names the parties, lays out the facts, identifies the causes of action (negligence, breach of contract, etc.), and asks for specific relief (money, an injunction, declaratory judgment). Sloppy complaints can be dismissed under Federal Rule 12(b)(6) for failure to state a claim — Bell Atlantic v. Twombly (2007) raised the bar.

snappy: Internal processes ensuring an organisation follows applicable laws and regulations.
detail: Modern compliance departments cover privacy (GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA), financial reporting (Sarbanes-Oxley), anti-corruption (FCPA, UK Bribery Act), sanctions (OFAC), securities (Reg FD, Reg S-K), and industry-specific rules. Heavy regulated industries (banks, pharma, healthcare) have the deepest functions. Companies that violate can face fines, consent decrees, deferred-prosecution agreements, and individual prosecution of officers.

snappy: A statutory mechanism letting users licence copyrighted works at preset rates without negotiating with the owner.
detail: Music industry has the most prominent example: 17 U.S.C. § 115 mechanical licence for cover versions of songs. Rate set by Copyright Royalty Board. Music Modernization Act (2018) created a blanket compulsory licence for digital streaming via the Mechanical Licensing Collective. Cable retransmission (§ 111), satellite (§ 119), and ephemeral recordings have their own compulsory regimes. Distinguishable from voluntary licensing in most other content industries.

snappy: Two or more employees acting together for mutual aid or protection — protected under NLRA § 7 even without a union.
detail: Examples: discussing wages, complaining about working conditions, signing petitions, walking out. Covers non-union workplaces broadly. Social media discussions about workplace conditions often qualify. Single-employee complaints can be 'concerted' if seeking to initiate or induce group action. NLRB has broadly protected social-media activity. NLRA-protected employees can't be fired for engaging in concerted activity; remedy is back-pay and reinstatement under § 10(c).

snappy: Two or more people owning the same property at the same time.
detail: Three classic forms: tenancy in common (separate shares, no survivorship), joint tenancy (survivorship), and tenancy by the entirety (spousal survivorship plus creditor protection). Community property (in nine states plus Puerto Rico) is a fourth: marital property automatically owned 50/50. Title documents determine which form: 'joint tenants with rights of survivorship' vs 'tenants in common' — words of art matter.

snappy: An event that must happen before a duty to perform arises.
detail: Common examples: 'subject to financing' in real-estate contracts; 'subject to board approval' in corporate deals; 'on receipt of regulatory approval' in mergers. Failure of the condition discharges the duty. Distinguished from a promise (which would be breached if it didn't happen). Often blurred at the boundary — courts interpret to avoid forfeiture where possible.

snappy: An event that, if it happens, terminates an existing duty to perform.
detail: Rarer than conditions precedent in modern drafting. Insurance contracts use them — coverage exists until a triggering event (claim denial after fraud finding). The party arguing for termination has the burden of proving the condition occurred. Modern courts often re-characterise apparent conditions subsequent as conditions precedent to maintain consistent allocation of burdens.

snappy: Individual ownership of a unit within a multi-unit building, plus a fractional share of common areas.
detail: Created by recording a condominium declaration and plat. The condo association governs common areas (lobby, pool, roof) through bylaws and assessments. Each unit owner gets a deed for their unit. Mortgage-eligible like detached homes. Most states have a Condominium Act setting baseline rules. Florida's Surfside collapse (2021) drove sweeping reforms requiring structural-integrity inspections and reserve funding.

snappy: Court approval of a Chapter 11 or Chapter 13 reorganisation plan — making it binding on creditors and debtor.
detail: Requirements (11 U.S.C. § 1129): plan complies with bankruptcy law, proposed in good faith, feasible, at least one impaired class accepts, best-interests test (creditors receive at least what they would in Chapter 7 liquidation), absolute priority rule. Voting by class; creditor objections heard at confirmation hearing. Court order binding on all parties. Post-confirmation modifications under § 1127 only on showing of substantial cause.

snappy: A fiduciary's personal interest threatens to interfere with their duty to the principal.
detail: Triggers heightened review: 'entire fairness' standard rather than business-judgment rule. Disclosure and cleansing procedures (disinterested director approval, shareholder vote) can restore deferential review. Common contexts: M&A involving controlling shareholders, related-party transactions, executive compensation, conflicts among directors with overlapping board service. SEC rules require public-company disclosure of related-party transactions over $120,000.

snappy: Sixth Amendment guarantee that criminal defendants can cross-examine the witnesses against them.
detail: Crawford v. Washington (2004) restored a robust reading: out-of-court 'testimonial' statements are inadmissible unless the declarant testifies or was previously subject to cross-examination. What's 'testimonial' has spawned much litigation: 911 calls (Davis v. Washington, 2006), forensic-lab reports (Bullcoming v. New Mexico, 2011), expert reliance on others' findings (Smith v. Arizona, 2024). Major impact on hearsay analysis in criminal trials.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Federal statute letting Congress pass a joint resolution to overturn recent federal regulations.
detail: 5 U.S.C. §§ 801–808. Major recent uses: 16 Obama-era regulations overturned in early 2017; some Trump-era regulations targeted under Biden. Effect of disapproval: rule has no force, agency can't issue 'substantially the same' rule without new statutory authorisation. Resolutions can't be filibustered in the Senate. Lookback typically covers rules from the last 60 legislative days of prior session.

snappy: A court-approved settlement requiring specific conduct, enforceable by contempt.
detail: Combines features of contract (negotiated) and judicial order (court enforces).
