Common in DOJ antitrust enforcement (the Microsoft consent decree, 2001), FTC consumer-protection actions, civil-rights cases (police-department reforms after DOJ pattern-or-practice investigations), and environmental cleanup. Lasts for years or decades; modifying requires court approval. Plays a major role in keeping regulated entities in line after one-off violations.

snappy: A warrantless search is lawful if the person with authority over the place voluntarily consents.
detail: Schneckloth v. Bustamonte (1973) — voluntariness is the test, not knowledge of right to refuse. 'Co-tenant' consent (Georgia v. Randolph, 2006) is overcome by a present, objecting co-occupant. Apparent authority can suffice (Illinois v. Rodriguez, 1990) if officers reasonably believe the consenter has authority. Scope is limited by what a reasonable person would understand the consent to cover. Withdrawal of consent must be honoured.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Indirect harms flowing from a breach — lost profits, downstream business losses — recoverable if foreseeable.
detail: Hadley v. Baxendale (1854) — mill-shaft case — established foreseeability rule: damages recoverable if (1) arising naturally from breach, or (2) reasonably contemplated by parties at contracting. UCC § 2-715(2) codifies for sale of goods. Commonly excluded in contracts via 'no consequential damages' clauses — heavily negotiated in commercial agreements. Tech and services contracts routinely cap exposure to direct damages only.

snappy: Court-appointed authority to manage another person's property or affairs — often used for incapacitated adults.
detail: Distinct from guardianship in some states (where conservatorship handles property, guardianship handles person); in other states the terms are synonymous. Britney Spears's high-profile conservatorship (terminated 2021) brought public attention to abuses. Procedures: capacity hearings, conservator appointment, ongoing court reporting, accounting requirements. Modern reform: supported decision-making frameworks, narrower-scope conservatorships, clearer termination paths.

snappy: Something of value each side gives up — what makes a promise legally binding rather than just a gift.
detail: Could be money, goods, services, forbearance from a legal right, or a return promise. Courts won't second-guess whether the exchange is fair (no 'adequacy of consideration' inquiry) — $1 for a house is enforceable as long as both sides meant it. Past consideration doesn't count; nor does a 'pre-existing duty'. Without consideration, you're looking at gift, not contract — see promissory estoppel for the workaround.

snappy: An agreement between two or more people to commit a crime, plus (usually) at least one overt act in furtherance.
detail: Federal conspiracy statute (18 U.S.C. § 371) imposes up to five years even for misdemeanor objects. Drug and RICO conspiracies have their own statutes with much higher penalties. Pinkerton v. United States (1946) made conspirators liable for foreseeable acts of co-conspirators. Statements of co-conspirators escape hearsay rules (Federal Rule of Evidence 801(d)(2)(E)). Withdrawal is a defence only if effective and timely.

snappy: Conditions so intolerable that the employee 'quits' — legally treated as if they were fired.
detail: Plaintiff must show conditions a reasonable person would find intolerable — high bar. Common scenarios: severe harassment with no employer response, demotion to humiliating role, dramatic pay cut, hostile work environment unaddressed. Pennsylvania State Police v. Suders (2004) confirmed constructive discharge can satisfy 'tangible adverse action' element of supervisor harassment liability. Resignation typically forfeits unemployment benefits; constructive-discharge finding can restore eligibility.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: When a landlord's conduct makes the property uninhabitable, the tenant can leave and stop paying rent.
detail: Requires: substantial interference with use and enjoyment caused by landlord, plus the tenant actually leaving within reasonable time. Lesser interferences (occasional outages, minor repairs) don't qualify — the standard is high. Common triggers: broken heating in winter, persistent flooding, severe pest infestation landlord refuses to address. Tenants often invoke constructive eviction as a defence to landlord's suit for unpaid rent.

snappy: Legally being deemed to know something even if you don't actually — typically because it was publicly recorded.
detail: Foundational to recording systems: once a deed is properly recorded, all later buyers are charged with knowledge of it. Inquiry notice extends further — facts that should have prompted investigation impute knowledge of what reasonable investigation would have revealed. Bankruptcy filings, lis pendens, and various agency liens all rely on constructive notice. Failing to record removes constructive-notice protection.

snappy: Obtaining an immigrant visa at a US consulate abroad — alternative to adjustment of status from within the US.
detail: Required for most applicants outside US or those ineligible for adjustment. National Visa Center processes documents, then schedules interview at consulate. Medical exam, fingerprints, document submission required. Visa issued at successful interview; visa-holder enters US and becomes LPR upon admission. Required for many family-based and employment-based cases. Wait times vary: some consulates years-long, others weeks.

snappy: Federal agency created by Dodd-Frank (2010) to enforce consumer-finance laws.
detail: Jurisdiction: mortgages, credit cards, payday loans, debt collection, student loans, prepaid cards. Combined authorities formerly scattered across Fed, FDIC, OCC, FTC, HUD. Funded by Federal Reserve, not annual congressional appropriations — survived constitutional challenge in CFPB v. Community Financial Services Association (2024). Leadership structure changed by Seila Law (2020) — President can remove director at will. Major enforcement actions against Wells Fargo, Equifax, and various FinTech firms.

snappy: A law that targets speech because of its message — presumptively unconstitutional, subject to strict scrutiny.
detail: Reed v. Town of Gilbert (2015) clarified: a regulation is content-based if it 'applies to particular speech because of the topic discussed or the idea or message expressed'. Almost always invalidated. Limited exceptions: defamation, true threats, incitement to imminent lawless action, fighting words, obscenity, fraud, child sexual abuse material. Government-speech doctrine and government-as-employer permit content-based regulation in narrow contexts.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: A law that regulates speech without regard to the message — gets intermediate scrutiny.
detail: Ward v. Rock Against Racism (1989). Time, place, and manner restrictions on speech are content-neutral if (1) justified without reference to content, (2) narrowly tailored to significant government interest, and (3) leave open ample alternative channels. Park noise ordinances, parade permits, sound limits — classic examples. Distinguishable from content-based laws (which get the much tougher strict-scrutiny treatment).
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: A follow-on patent application that claims priority to an earlier-filed application — covers variations on the original invention.
detail: Three types: continuation (same disclosure, new claims), continuation-in-part (CIP, adding new matter), and divisional (split from a parent due to restriction requirement). Strategic tool: keep applications pending to claim products as competitors release them ('continuation chains'). USPTO's RCE practice (requests for continued examination) added complexity. Submarine-patent concerns from extended pendency drove the 20-year-from-filing rule.

snappy: A promise the law will enforce — the spine of every deal, lease, job offer, and online click-through.
detail: A contract is an agreement courts will hold the parties to. Three things have to line up: a clear offer, acceptance of that offer, and consideration (something of value each side trades). Once formed, breach exposes the breaching party to damages or specific performance. The Restatement (Second) of Contracts is the closest thing US law has to a contract textbook; the UCC governs sales of goods.

snappy: A standardised take-it-or-leave-it contract — sometimes scrutinised by courts for fairness.
detail: Most consumer contracts qualify: phone agreements, software licences, gym memberships, online terms of service. Not automatically unenforceable, but courts apply heightened scrutiny to ambiguous or one-sided terms. Reasonable expectations doctrine: terms inconsistent with consumer's reasonable expectations may be unenforceable. Concept developed in insurance contracts; now central to mass-consumer arbitration debates.

snappy: A defendant who pays more than its share can demand reimbursement from co-defendants.
detail: Common-law no-contribution rule reversed by Uniform Contribution Among Tortfeasors Act and similar statutes. Pro-rata or comparative-fault apportionment depending on jurisdiction. Distinguishable from indemnity (full reimbursement from primary wrongdoer). Settlement complications: contribution claims typically barred against settling defendants under good-faith settlement protections. Industry collateral targets for joint-tortfeasor analysis.

snappy: The old rule barring all recovery if plaintiff was at all negligent — still alive in a handful of states.
detail: Survives in Alabama, Maryland, North Carolina, Virginia, and DC. Even 1% plaintiff fault bars recovery. Mitigated by 'last clear chance' doctrine: plaintiff can still recover if defendant had the final opportunity to avoid injury. Most states replaced contributory with comparative starting in the 1970s. Criticised as harsh; reform efforts continue in remaining holdout jurisdictions.

snappy: Treaty-based protection from removal for someone likely to be tortured upon return.
detail: Article 3 of UN Convention Against Torture (1984). Implemented by Foreign Affairs Reform and Restructuring Act (1998), 8 C.F.R. §§ 208.16-208.18. Two types: withholding (more likely than not, available even if barred from asylum) and deferral (limited to those barred from withholding for criminal grounds). No family inclusion, no permanent status, no work authorisation eligibility until later. Standard: torture by or with consent/acquiescence of government.

snappy: Treating someone else's property as your own — civil cousin of theft.
detail: Plaintiff recovers the full value of the converted property, plus consequential damages. Differs from trespass to chattels (interference without taking) and theft (criminal). Examples: a contractor using salvaged materials from a job site for personal projects; an employer keeping a former employee's tools. Online conversion theories have been pressed for stolen domain names, NFTs, and cryptocurrency — courts are still working out the doctrinal fit.

snappy: Apartment ownership through shares in a corporation — you own stock plus a proprietary lease, not real estate.
detail: Common in New York City and a few other cities; rare elsewhere. The co-op corporation owns the building; shareholders elect a board and lease their units. Board approval often required for sales — basis for famous celebrity rejections. Less mortgage-friendly than condos because lenders take security in shares, not real estate. Maintenance fees include the building's mortgage and property tax — often higher than condo fees.

snappy: Automatic protection for original creative works fixed in tangible form.
detail: Covers books, music, code, photographs, paintings, films, choreography, architectural works. Protection arises automatically on creation; registration (with the US Copyright Office) is required before suing for infringement and unlocks statutory damages and attorneys' fees. Term: life of the author plus 70 years (works for hire: 95/120 years). Fair use is the main defence; the four factors come from § 107 of the Copyright Act.

snappy: Senior executives appointed by the board to run day-to-day operations — CEO, CFO, COO, general counsel, secretary.
detail: Fiduciary duties to the corporation parallel to directors'. Indemnification typically broader than for directors. Officer titles often defined in bylaws; modern statutes give substantial flexibility. Public-company officers face SEC Section 16 reporting and short-swing-profit liability for personal stock trades. CEO/CFO certifications under SOX § 302 expose officers to personal liability for accurate disclosure.

snappy: A director or officer can't take a business opportunity that belongs to the corporation for personal benefit.
detail: Restatement framework: opportunity in corporation's line of business, financial means to pursue it, fiduciary became aware in corporate capacity. Disclose to board and let it decline before pursuing personally. DGCL § 122(17) allows charter waivers — common in VC investments where investors have multiple portfolio companies. Broz v. Cellular Information Systems (Del. 1996) and Guth v. Loft (Del. 1939) are the foundational cases.

snappy: A legal entity separate from its owners — shareholders, directors, officers each have different rights and roles.
detail: Created by filing articles of incorporation with a state. Default tax treatment: 'C corporation' — taxed at entity level; dividends taxed again on shareholders ('double taxation'). Limited liability for shareholders. Perpetual existence. Required formalities: annual meetings, board minutes, separate finances. Most public US companies and many large private ones are Delaware corporations (DGCL). State of incorporation determines internal-affairs law; state of operation determines state tax.

snappy: Examining whether a regulation's benefits exceed its costs — required for many federal rules.
detail: Executive Order 12866 (1993) and subsequent orders require OMB review and CBA for 'significant' regulations. Quantification methodologies extensively litigated — discount rates, value of statistical life, climate-cost estimates. Michigan v. EPA (2015) required EPA to consider costs in deciding whether to regulate mercury emissions. CBA practice mature in EPA, FAA, Treasury; less developed in some independent agencies. Criticised by environmental advocates as undervaluing non-monetary benefits.

snappy: A response that changes the terms of the original offer — it rejects the offer and proposes a new one.
detail: Under the common-law 'mirror image rule', any deviation in acceptance creates a counter-offer rather than acceptance. The original offer dies and the original offeror becomes the new offeree, free to accept or reject. The UCC § 2-207 ('battle of the forms') relaxes this for goods, letting acceptance form a contract despite differing terms — the differing terms then drop out or get handled by gap-fillers.

snappy: A defendant's claim back against the plaintiff in the same lawsuit.
detail: Federal Rule 13. 'Compulsory' counterclaims arise from the same transaction as plaintiff's claim and must be raised or are forever waived. 'Permissive' counterclaims are unrelated claims the defendant can choose to bring or save for another day. Counterclaims can flip the strategic dynamics — a defendant who'd settle quickly with no counterclaim may dig in once one exists.

snappy: Past business practices between the same parties — used to fill gaps in a current contract.
detail: UCC § 1-303. If parties have repeatedly handled defective shipments by quick replacement in past deals, that pattern shapes interpretation of the current contract even if silent. Course of dealing precedes the current contract; 'course of performance' is the analogous concept for repeated conduct within the same contract; 'usage of trade' draws on industry-wide practice.

snappy: Delaware's specialised equity court — the dominant US forum for corporate disputes.
detail: Five Vice Chancellors plus the Chancellor (Chief Judge). No juries — bench trials only. Specialised expertise in corporate, alternative-entity, fiduciary, and equity matters. Decisions widely influential due to Delaware-dominated corporate domicile. Fast docket relative to most state and federal courts. Appeals go directly to Delaware Supreme Court. Famous current and recent Vice Chancellors include Lori Will, Kathaleen St. J. McCormick, Travis Laster, and J. Travis Laster.

snappy: A buyer's right under the UCC to substitute goods elsewhere when the seller breaches.
detail: UCC § 2-712: buyer purchases substitute goods in good faith and without unreasonable delay, then recovers the difference between cover price and contract price, plus incidental damages. The duty of good faith means the buyer can't run up the price by picking the most expensive substitute available. Common-law contracts don't have a formal 'cover' doctrine but mitigation operates similarly.

snappy: Forcing approval of a reorganisation plan over a class of creditors' objections — possible if statutory requirements met.
detail: 11 U.S.C. § 1129(b). Requires: at least one impaired class accepted, fair and equitable treatment of dissenting class, absolute priority rule satisfied. Cramdown common against secured creditors (paid present value of collateral over time) and against equity (wiped out unless senior creditors agree otherwise). Strict construction by courts. Up Holdings (Del. 2024) addressed equitable mootness for cramdown appeals.

snappy: Anyone owed money or property by the debtor.
detail: Categories: secured (collateral-backed loans), priority (specific high-priority categories — wages, taxes, alimony), general unsecured (most credit cards, medical bills, unsecured loans). Filing proof of claim required by deadline. Creditors' committees in major Chapter 11 cases represent classes of creditors. Adversary proceedings within bankruptcy address creditor-specific disputes — non-dischargeability, preferences, fraudulent transfers.

snappy: A claim one co-party brings against another — e.g., defendant A sues co-defendant B in the same case.
detail: Federal Rule 13(g). Allowed only if it arises out of the same transaction as the main case or relates to property at issue. Common in product-liability cases where multiple manufacturers and sellers blame each other. Distinct from counterclaims (against an opposing party) and third-party complaints (bringing in new parties). State practice tracks the federal rule closely.

snappy: Questioning the other side's witness — leading questions allowed, scope tied to direct.
detail: Considered 'the greatest legal engine ever invented for the discovery of truth' (Wigmore). Federal Rule of Evidence 611(b) generally limits cross to the subject matter of direct plus credibility. Tactics: lock the witness into prior statements, expose bias and inconsistency, control the narrative with short leading questions. Failing to cross-examine on a critical point can be deemed waiver in some contexts.

snappy: Eighth Amendment ban on barbaric, disproportionate, or arbitrary punishments.
detail: Three contexts: methods of execution (mostly lethal injection challenges — Glossip v. Gross, 2015, upheld midazolam); categorical bars on capital punishment (intellectually disabled, juveniles, non-homicide); proportionality challenges to lengthy sentences. 'Evolving standards of decency that mark the progress of a maturing society' (Trop v. Dulles, 1958) provides the framing, though originalist critique has gained traction.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Voting structure giving each shareholder a number of votes equal to shares × directors-to-elect — votes can be concentrated.
detail: Lets minority shareholders pool votes on one or two candidates to elect them. Disfavored in modern Delaware companies; required by California Corporations Code § 708 for California-incorporated entities unless opted out. Charter or bylaws can provide; otherwise straight voting (one vote per share per director seat) is default. Largely a corporate-governance footnote in public companies but still important in close corporations and private-company structures.

snappy: Seller can fix non-conforming goods after rejection — within the contract time, or sometimes beyond.
detail: UCC § 2-508 gives seller right to cure when (1) time for performance hasn't expired, or (2) seller reasonably believed non-conforming goods would be acceptable. Seller must notify buyer and substitute conforming tender within reasonable time. Limits the harsh edges of the perfect-tender rule. Modern e-commerce: returns and replacements are routine but technically governed by these UCC provisions.

snappy: The area immediately surrounding a home — protected by the Fourth Amendment as if it were the home itself.
detail: Four-factor Dunn test (United States v. Dunn, 1987): proximity to the home, enclosures, use, and steps to protect from observation. Front porch, fenced backyard, driveways immediately adjacent to the house typically qualify; open fields beyond curtilage don't (Oliver v. United States, 1984). Florida v. Jardines (2013) held drug-sniffing-dog approach to front porch is a search; Collins v. Virginia (2018) extended curtilage protection to motorcycles parked under home overhangs.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Registering a domain name matching someone else's trademark in bad faith — usually to extort the trademark owner.
detail: Anticybersquatting Consumer Protection Act (1999, 15 U.S.C. § 1125(d)) creates federal cause of action. Remedies include forfeiture of the domain and statutory damages up to $100,000. Alternative: ICANN's Uniform Domain-Name Dispute-Resolution Policy (UDRP) — fast, cheap, no damages, just domain transfer. Marathon battles over .sucks, .xyz, and country-code TLDs continue. Major IPR enforcement front for brand owners.

snappy: Obama-era 2012 executive program shielding certain undocumented people who came to US as children.
detail: Memorandum-based, not statute. Eligibility: came to US before 16, continuously present since 2007, under 31 in 2012, in school or military or graduate, no significant criminal record. Provides: two-year renewable deferred-action status, work authorisation, advance parole eligibility. Trump rescission attempt blocked by DHS v. Regents (2020) on APA grounds. Texas v. United States (5th Cir. 2023) found DACA unlawful but stayed remedies — only renewals processed (no new applicants) through 2024.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Money awarded to make the non-breaching party whole.
detail: Expectation damages are the default — put the plaintiff in the position they'd have been in had the contract been performed. Reliance damages refund what they spent. Restitution returns what was paid. Consequential damages cover foreseeable losses beyond the contract itself (Hadley v. Baxendale, 1854). Liquidated damages clauses pre-set the number; courts strike them as 'penalties' if they aren't a reasonable forecast.

snappy: Federal courts' test for whether scientific expert testimony is reliable enough to reach a jury.
detail: Daubert v. Merrell Dow (1993) replaced the older Frye 'general acceptance' test in federal court. Judges act as 'gatekeepers' weighing: testable methodology, peer review, error rate, standards, and acceptance in the field. Kumho Tire (1999) extended Daubert to all expert testimony. Federal Rule of Evidence 702 codifies the standard. Major battleground in toxic tort, product liability, and patent cases.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: The person or entity that owes the debt and filed for bankruptcy.
detail: Individual debtors (personal bankruptcy) or entity debtors (corporate, LLC, partnership). Distinguishable from 'debtor in possession' in Chapter 11 — debtor remaining in control of business operations during reorganisation. Debtor's bankruptcy estate includes most assets at filing date plus certain post-filing assets (inheritances within 180 days, divorce settlements). Discharge ultimately wipes debt for individual debtors satisfying requirements.

snappy: The written document transferring ownership of real estate.
detail: Three main flavours: warranty deed (seller guarantees clear title — strongest), special warranty deed (seller guarantees only against their own actions), quitclaim deed (seller conveys whatever interest they have, no guarantees — used between family members or to clear clouds on title). Must be in writing, signed, and typically recorded in the county registry. The deed conveys title; the closing happens when it's delivered and accepted.

snappy: Mortgage-equivalent used in many Western states — three parties: borrower, lender, neutral trustee.
detail: Functionally identical to a mortgage but procedurally distinct. The trustee holds bare legal title and can foreclose non-judicially on default. Used in California, Texas, Virginia, and many other states; mortgages dominate in Florida, New York, and the Midwest. Non-judicial foreclosure is faster and cheaper for lenders — sometimes 90 days vs the year-plus for judicial foreclosure. Borrower protections vary by state.

snappy: Publishing a false statement of fact that damages someone's reputation.
detail: Libel (written, more durable) and slander (spoken). Public figures must prove 'actual malice' — knowledge of falsity or reckless disregard — under New York Times v. Sullivan (1964). Private figures only need to prove negligence in most states. Truth is an absolute defence; opinion is generally protected. Damages: actual harm to reputation, sometimes presumed for libel per se (accusations of crime, professional incompetence, disease, sexual misconduct).

snappy: Statements requiring extrinsic facts to show their defamatory nature — plaintiff must prove special damages.
detail: Contrast with defamation per se. Example: 'X was at the hospital yesterday' — innocent on its face but defamatory if combined with extrinsic fact that X claimed to be at work that day. Plaintiff must plead and prove specific monetary loss tied to the statement. Combined with per-se categories' shrinkage, per quod is the dominant modern framework in many jurisdictions.

snappy: Statements so plainly damaging that the plaintiff doesn't have to prove special damages.
detail: Four traditional categories: imputing crime, imputing loathsome disease, harming business/profession, imputing unchastity. Modern doctrine in some jurisdictions has shifted to actual-harm requirement (Gertz v. Welch, 1974). Where retained, eliminates need to prove specific monetary loss — presumed damages available. Defamation involving public figures still requires actual malice regardless of per-se status.

snappy: 2016 federal statute creating a federal cause of action for trade-secret misappropriation.
detail: 18 U.S.C. § 1836. Operates in parallel with state Uniform Trade Secrets Act regimes — federal jurisdiction without diversity. Allows ex parte civil seizure orders to prevent dissemination. Whistleblower immunity protections (§ 1833(b)) required in confidentiality agreements. Massively increased federal trade-secret litigation. Famous cases: Uber/Waymo over self-driving technology (settled 2018), Anthony Levandowski's parallel criminal conviction.

snappy: The party being sued in a civil case or charged in a criminal one.
detail: The defendant is whoever the lawsuit or prosecution is aimed at. In criminal cases, defendants have a wide set of constitutional protections (right to counsel, right against self-incrimination, presumption of innocence) that don't apply the same way in civil cases. Civil defendants can file counterclaims, third-party complaints, or motions to dismiss before they ever answer the substance.

snappy: The signed contract committing the parties to a merger or acquisition — binding subject to closing conditions.
detail: Replaces or supersedes earlier letter of intent. Major sections: parties, consideration, structure, representations and warranties, covenants (interim operations, no-shop, financing), closing conditions (regulatory approval, no MAC, accuracy of reps), termination rights and fees. Heavily negotiated. Public targets file as exhibit to 8-K. 'Material adverse change' clauses became hot litigation in COVID-19 era and increasingly geopolitical disruption scenarios.

snappy: Transfer of contractual duties — distinct from assignment of rights.
detail: Generally permitted unless the duty is personal (paint my portrait — not delegable) or prohibited by contract. The delegator stays liable; the delegatee becomes secondarily liable. Often confused with assignment but conceptually distinct: assignment moves rights, delegation moves duties. Together they make a complete transfer of the contractual relationship.

snappy: Before bringing a derivative suit, a shareholder must demand the board take action — or plead that demand would be futile.
detail: Aronson v. Lewis (Del. 1984) framework: demand futile if directors lack independence or particularised facts show non-business-judgment conduct. Rales v. Blasband (Del. 1993) modified for new-board scenarios. Universal Demand jurisdictions require always demanding regardless of futility. Failure to comply with demand pleading is most common dismissal basis. Recent shift: Zuckerberg (Del. 2021) consolidated tests into unified three-part framework.

snappy: A state-court motion (especially California) saying: 'Even if everything in the complaint is true, you still don't have a case.'
detail: Functionally the state cousin of Federal Rule 12(b)(6). California Code of Civil Procedure § 430.10 lists grounds. Other states use different terminology: 'motion to dismiss', 'motion for judgment on the pleadings', or simply 'demurrer'. Sustained demurrers usually come 'with leave to amend' the first time around. Repeated failures lead to dismissal with prejudice.

snappy: Pretrial questioning of a witness under oath, transcribed by a court reporter.
detail: Lawyers from both sides attend; the witness answers questions live, usually for a single day capped at seven hours under the federal rules. The transcript can be used at trial to impeach inconsistent testimony or, if the witness is unavailable, as substantive evidence. Video depositions are now standard, especially after the pandemic normalised remote testimony.

snappy: A lawsuit brought by a shareholder on behalf of the corporation — against directors, officers, or third parties harming the company.
detail: Recovery goes to the corporation, not the suing shareholder (unless ordered as plaintiff's-attorney fee). Federal Rule 23.1 governs in federal court; state rules parallel. Standing requires shareholder status at time of wrong and continued ownership. Demand requirement (or excused demand futility) precedes filing. Most are settled — fee awards drive plaintiff's-counsel economics. Delaware Chancery dominant forum due to corporate-domicile statistics.

snappy: A new work based on a preexisting one — translations, adaptations, sequels, remixes.
detail: 17 U.S.C. § 101. The exclusive right to prepare derivative works belongs to the copyright owner (§ 106(2)). Translations of novels, film adaptations of books, remixed songs, derivative tattoo designs, and AI outputs incorporating copyrighted training data all implicate this right. Fair use can defeat infringement claims (Campbell v. Acuff-Rose, 1994 — '2 Live Crew' Pretty Woman parody). Authorised derivative works can have their own copyrights.

snappy: A mark that describes the goods or services — registrable only after acquiring 'secondary meaning' in the market.
detail: Examples: 'Cold and Creamy' for ice cream, 'Best Buy' for electronics retail. Secondary meaning shows consumers associate the term with one source despite descriptiveness (often via long use, advertising spend, sales volume, survey evidence). Five years of substantially exclusive and continuous use creates rebuttable presumption (Lanham Act § 2(f)). Geographically descriptive and surname marks face parallel rules.

snappy: A product's design makes it unreasonably dangerous — the whole product line, not just the specific unit.
detail: Two tests: consumer-expectations (would an ordinary consumer find it more dangerous than expected?) and risk-utility (do risks outweigh benefits, considering feasible alternatives?). Restatement (Third) Products Liability favors risk-utility. Famous cases: Ford Pinto fuel-tank cases (1970s), GM ignition-switch litigation (2010s), Roundup glyphosate cases (ongoing through 2024). Requires often-expensive expert testimony on alternative designs.

snappy: Protects the ornamental, non-functional appearance of a product.
detail: 15-year term from issue (no maintenance fees). Famous battles: Apple v. Samsung over iPhone design patents. Examination is faster than utility-patent examination; less prior-art-driven. Infringement test: would an ordinary observer find the designs substantially the same? Egyptian Goddess (Fed. Cir. 2008) framework. Important strategic tool especially in consumer products, furniture, and packaging.

snappy: The Delaware statutory code governing corporations — the dominant US corporate-law regime.
detail: Title 8 of Delaware Code. Most large US public companies incorporated in Delaware; over 60% of Fortune 500. Reasons: sophisticated Court of Chancery, predictable case law, modern statutory provisions, business-friendly judiciary, flexible governance options. State of incorporation determines internal-affairs law under the internal-affairs doctrine — Delaware law follows companies even when operations are elsewhere. Annual franchise tax structure incentivises incorporation regardless of operations location.

snappy: The questioning of a witness by the side that called them — must use open-ended, non-leading questions.
detail: The witness tells their story. Federal Rule of Evidence 611(c) bars leading questions on direct except for preliminaries, hostile witnesses, or witnesses identified with an adverse party. Effective direct lets the witness rather than the lawyer dominate. Preparation focuses on structure, exhibits, and anticipating cross-examination. Failure on direct — confusing testimony, missing elements — can sink a case before cross even starts.

snappy: Court order releasing the debtor from personal liability for most pre-filing debts — the ultimate goal of consumer bankruptcy.
detail: Chapter 7: discharge typically 4-6 months after filing. Chapter 13: discharge after plan completion (3-5 years). Non-dischargeable debts: most student loans, recent taxes, alimony, child support, debts from fraud or wilful injury, criminal restitution. Discharge violations: creditors continuing collection attempts violate the discharge injunction — actionable in bankruptcy court. Effect: personal liability eliminated but liens on collateral generally survive.

snappy: Contractual language eliminating implied warranties — must be conspicuous and use specific words.
detail: UCC § 2-316 sets requirements: 'as is', 'with all faults', or similar language conspicuous (in caps, bold, distinct font). Merchantability disclaimers must mention 'merchantability'. State consumer protection laws (Magnuson-Moss, lemon laws) limit disclaimers against consumers. Express warranties harder to disclaim — usually requires careful contract drafting integrating the warranty section. Standard practice in commercial sales contracts.

snappy: The pretrial phase where each side exchanges evidence — documents, depositions, interrogatories.
detail: Discovery is how parties learn what the other side knows before trial. It's expensive and often the most contentious phase: document productions can run into the millions in big commercial cases. Modern e-discovery covers email, Slack, text messages, and cloud storage. Rules limit scope to information 'relevant to any party's claim or defense' and proportional to the case.

snappy: The statute of limitations starts when the injury is (or should reasonably have been) discovered, not when it occurred.
detail: Mitigates harsh results in latent-injury cases: toxic exposure (asbestos, contaminated water), medical malpractice with delayed manifestation, sexual abuse repressed memories. Variations: actual discovery, constructive discovery (should have known with reasonable diligence). Tolling and statute-of-repose interactions create complex timing puzzles. Tort-reform efforts often target broad applications of the rule.

snappy: A facially neutral policy that disproportionately harms a protected group — unlawful unless job-related and consistent with business necessity.
detail: Griggs v. Duke Power (1971) recognised the theory under Title VII. 42 U.S.C. § 2000e-2(k) codified the burden-shifting framework: plaintiff shows disparate impact, employer shows job-relatedness and business necessity, plaintiff shows less-discriminatory alternative. Applies to recruitment criteria, testing, credentialing, layoff procedures. Ricci v. DeStefano (2009) addressed tension between disparate-impact and disparate-treatment claims.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Intentional discrimination — treating someone differently because of a protected characteristic.
detail: Most straightforward Title VII theory. Proven by direct evidence (rare) or circumstantial evidence (McDonnell Douglas burden-shifting framework). Pattern-or-practice cases involve broader policies of discrimination. Mixed-motive cases (Price Waterhouse v. Hopkins, 1989; codified in 1991 Civil Rights Act) require showing protected characteristic was 'a motivating factor'. Distinguishable from disparate-impact theory which addresses neutral policies with disproportionate effect.

snappy: Contract between manufacturer and distributor governing the sale of products through specified channels.
detail: Major terms: territory, exclusivity, minimum-purchase commitments, marketing obligations, termination provisions, post-termination inventory disposition. Antitrust limits on resale-price maintenance and territorial restrictions (Leegin v. PSKS, 2007 — vertical price-fixing rule-of-reason). Many states have specific dealer-protection laws (especially for cars, alcohol, agricultural equipment) that override contractual terms. Foreign distribution adds export controls and tax complexity.

snappy: Federal courts can hear state-law cases between citizens of different states if more than $75,000 is at stake.
detail: 28 U.S.C. § 1332. Requires 'complete diversity' — no plaintiff can share citizenship with any defendant. Designed historically to protect out-of-state defendants from local-court bias. Corporations are citizens of their state of incorporation and principal place of business (Hertz v. Friend, 2010). Class Action Fairness Act (2005) relaxed diversity rules for big class actions to channel them into federal court.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: A distribution of corporate profits to shareholders — declared by the board.
detail: Forms: cash, stock, property, in-kind. Cash dividends declared from corporation's surplus or net profits (DGCL § 170). Tax treatment: qualified dividends taxed at favourable rates (currently 0%, 15%, or 20% based on income). REITs and BDCs have special pass-through dividend regimes. Share buybacks have largely supplanted dividends as preferred capital-return mechanism — both directly compete. Stock dividends and splits don't change ownership percentages.

snappy: Legal termination of a marriage — handled in state family courts.
detail: Either party can file. Modern process: file petition, serve other spouse, exchange financial disclosures, negotiate or litigate settlement (property, support, custody), final judgment. Average duration 6-18 months; contested cases longer. Major issues: property division, alimony, child custody, child support. Mediation, collaborative divorce, and arbitration increasingly common alternatives to litigation. Average legal cost varies enormously: simple uncontested under $1,000; complex contested cases over $100,000.

snappy: 1998 law providing copyright safe harbours for online services that promptly take down infringing content.
detail: Notice-and-takedown is the core operational mechanism: the rights holder sends a takedown notice, the platform removes the content, the user can counter-notice. § 512 grants service providers (YouTube, Reddit, GitHub) immunity if they comply. § 1201 separately bars circumvention of digital rights management. Heavily criticised on both sides: rights holders say takedowns are too slow; users say they're abused for censorship.

snappy: Online services that host user content aren't liable for user infringement if they comply with notice-and-takedown procedures.
detail: 17 U.S.C. § 512. Four safe harbors: transitory communications, system caching, hosting, and information location tools. Requirements include designated agent, repeat-infringer policy, and prompt response to takedown notices. Foundational to user-generated-content platforms (YouTube, Reddit, Twitter/X). Viacom v. YouTube (2010, 2012) clarified knowledge standards. Repeat-infringer-policy enforcement (BMG v. Cox Communications, 2018) is the live battleground.

snappy: An infringer can't escape liability by making trivial changes that fall outside the literal claim language.
detail: Graver Tank v. Linde (1950) recognised; Warner-Jenkinson (1997) reframed in elements-by-elements terms. Tests: function-way-result, or insubstantial differences. Prosecution-history estoppel limits the doctrine — claim amendments to overcome rejections forfeit equivalents within the surrendered scope (Festo v. Shoketsu, 2002). Counterbalances pure literal-infringement analysis; ensures patent protection isn't lost to design-around minor substitutions.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

full: Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act
snappy: Sweeping 2010 financial regulation enacted after the 2008 financial crisis.
detail: 12 U.S.C. §§ 5301 et seq. Created the CFPB and Financial Stability Oversight Council. Imposed Volcker Rule banning proprietary trading by banks. Required derivatives clearing through central counterparties (CFTC and SEC rules). Established 'orderly liquidation authority' for failed large financial institutions. Whistleblower bounties via SEC Section 21F. Partially scaled back by Economic Growth, Regulatory Relief, and Consumer Protection Act (2018).

snappy: A state or local registration giving unmarried couples some marriage-like rights.
detail: Coverage varies dramatically: hospital visitation, healthcare decisions, sometimes inheritance and property. Few states retain robust domestic-partnership regimes; California's still functions parallel to marriage. Many employers offer domestic-partner benefits regardless of state registration. Federal tax/immigration/Social Security benefits unavailable. Often used by older couples avoiding marriage penalty in survivor benefits.

snappy: Violence or threats between people in a domestic relationship — spouse, partner, family.
detail: Most states have specific DV statutes with enhanced penalties beyond ordinary assault. Federal Violence Against Women Act (VAWA, 1994) funds prosecution and shelter services. The Lautenberg Amendment (1996) bars firearm possession by anyone convicted of misdemeanor DV — upheld in United States v. Hayes (2009). Civil protective orders frequently accompany criminal cases. 911 hangup-call protocols and pro-arrest policies developed after research showed historical under-enforcement.

snappy: The flip side: states can't burden interstate commerce, even where Congress hasn't acted.
detail: Reads negative restriction into the Commerce Clause's affirmative grant. Tests differ for facial discrimination against interstate commerce (almost per se invalid, Philadelphia v. New Jersey, 1978) versus even-handed laws with incidental burdens (Pike v. Bruce Church, 1970 balancing). Modern flashpoints: state laws on agricultural production methods (National Pork Producers v. Ross, 2023, upheld California's Proposition 12), interstate alcohol sales, online sales taxation.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Fifth Amendment ban on being tried twice for the same offence after acquittal.
detail: Attaches once a jury is sworn (or first witness called in bench trial). Doesn't bar retrial after a mistrial declared with the defendant's consent or for manifest necessity. Doesn't apply across separate sovereigns: federal and state can both prosecute the same conduct (Gamble v. United States, 2019, reaffirmed this). Doesn't bar civil suits after criminal acquittal — O.J. Simpson civil case is the famous example.

snappy: Holding a controlled substance without legal authority — the most-prosecuted drug crime.
detail: Controlled Substances Act (21 U.S.C. § 844) and parallel state statutes. Simple possession is generally a misdemeanor or low-level felony; quantity, type of drug, and prior record drive severity. 'Constructive possession' covers drugs not on the person but within their control (the trunk, a stash spot). Marijuana possession enforcement has dramatically diverged: legal in many states, still federal Schedule I. Diversion-to-treatment programs handle many low-level cases.

snappy: Fair procedure before government deprives anyone of life, liberty, or property.
detail: Fifth Amendment (federal) and Fourteenth Amendment (state) both contain due-process clauses. Procedural due process requires notice, an opportunity to be heard, and a neutral decision-maker. Substantive due process protects certain fundamental rights (privacy, marriage, parental autonomy) from arbitrary government interference. Roe v. Wade (1973) was decided on substantive-due-process grounds; Dobbs (2022) overruled it.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Operating a vehicle while impaired by alcohol or drugs — measured by blood-alcohol content or observed impairment.
detail: Per-se limits in all 50 states: 0.08% BAC for adult drivers (Utah leads at 0.05%); commercial drivers 0.04%; under-21 zero-tolerance. Implied-consent laws make refusal to test trigger automatic licence suspension. Felony DUI applies to repeat offences, deaths, or serious injury. Birchfield v. North Dakota (2016) held warrant required for blood draws but not breath tests. Ignition-interlock devices increasingly mandated after first offence.

snappy: Improper threats that overcome free will — make the contract voidable.
detail: Restatement (Second) §§ 174-176. Three types: physical compulsion (void), improper threats (voidable if no reasonable alternative), economic duress (controversial; usually requires wrongful threat plus lack of alternatives). Modern economic-duress doctrine narrow: hard bargaining alone insufficient. Settlements signed under economic pressure rarely set aside. Long-time presence of duress in employment-arbitration debates — many courts treat take-it-or-leave-it adhesion contracts as not duress.

snappy: The legal obligation to act with reasonable care toward those who might be harmed by your conduct.
detail: First element of negligence. Existence and scope are legal questions for the judge. Palsgraf v. Long Island R.R. (1928) established Cardozo's foreseeable-plaintiff test. Special duties arise from relationships (doctor-patient, innkeeper-guest, school-student), undertakings, statutes, and special vulnerabilities. Generally no duty to rescue a stranger, but exceptions for special relationships or having created the peril.

snappy: Directors must make informed decisions using the care a reasonable person would exercise — focus on process, not outcome.
detail: Delaware-law standard: gross negligence triggers liability (Aronson v. Lewis, 1984). Business judgment rule (presumption of due care) shields informed decisions even when they turn out badly. Smith v. Van Gorkom (1985) found gross negligence in cursory M&A approval. Most companies have DGCL § 102(b)(7) charter provisions eliminating monetary liability for breach of care (but not loyalty). Process matters: agendas, minutes, expert advice, time to deliberate.

snappy: Directors must put corporation's interests ahead of their own — no self-dealing, no usurping corporate opportunities.
detail: Cannot be eliminated by charter (DGCL § 102(b)(7) excludes loyalty). Subcategories: avoid self-interested transactions, don't take corporate opportunities, don't compete, act in good faith. Self-dealing transactions face entire-fairness review unless cleansed by disinterested-director or shareholder approval. Stone v. Ritter (Del. 2006) embedded good-faith and Caremark duties within duty of loyalty.

snappy: The discovery of digital evidence — email, Slack, text messages, cloud storage, database records.
detail: Federal Rules amended in 2006 to address electronically stored information (ESI). Drives most discovery cost in modern litigation. Disputes focus on custodians, date ranges, search terms, deduplication, and review-tool selection. Predictive coding (machine-learning review) increasingly common after Da Silva Moore v. Publicis (2012). The Sedona Conference issues influential e-discovery guidance.

full: Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act
snappy: Federal statute (2000) giving electronic signatures and records legal equivalence to paper.
detail: 15 U.S.C. §§ 7001-7031. Preempts inconsistent state law except where UETA or substantially similar state law applies. Consumer-protection provisions require affirmative consumer consent and disclosure for electronic delivery of legally required disclosures. Carved-out categories: wills, court documents, family-law records, eviction notices. Companion to state UETA statutes; foundation of modern electronic commerce.

snappy: A refundable tax credit for low- and moderate-income workers — among the largest US anti-poverty programs.
detail: IRC § 32. Refundable: can produce refunds beyond tax owed. Maximum credit (2024): about $7,830 for families with three or more qualifying children. Phase-in based on earned income; phase-out at higher incomes. Eligibility: earned income, valid SSN, US citizen or resident, AGI below threshold, no investment income above $11,600. Annually ~25 million households receive EITC. Significant problem with eligibility complexity, error rates, and IRS auditing — disproportionately auditing low-income filers.

snappy: A right to use someone else's land for a specific purpose.
detail: Utility easements let power companies run lines across private property; access easements let landlocked neighbours reach the road; conservation easements restrict development for environmental reasons. Easements can be appurtenant (tied to a neighbouring parcel) or in gross (held by a person or company). They run with the land — they don't disappear when the property is sold. Search for them before buying.

snappy: An easement that benefits a specific neighbouring parcel — transfers automatically when the parcel is sold.
detail: Two parcels involved: the 'dominant tenement' (benefited) and 'servient tenement' (burdened). Classic example: a driveway easement giving a back-lot owner access across a front-lot owner's property. The easement 'runs with the land' — burdens later buyers of the servient tenement automatically. Often the most-litigated property right because they tend to be undocumented, ambiguous, or overlooked at sale.

snappy: An easement that benefits a person or company rather than a parcel — utility easements are the classic example.
detail: No 'dominant tenement' — the easement attaches to the holder personally or institutionally. Power lines, gas pipelines, cell towers, water lines: all use easements in gross. Historically not transferable for personal use; commercial easements in gross (utility rights) are transferable. Conservation easements (now codified in nearly all states) are a modern form: a land trust holds the easement, restricting development on private land.

full: Electronic Data Gathering, Analysis, and Retrieval
snappy: The SEC's online database where public companies file required disclosures — freely accessible to all.
detail: Operational since 1993; now hosts virtually all SEC filings. Search-and-retrieval interface plus structured XBRL data for financial reports. Critical research tool for investors, analysts, journalists, lawyers, academics. Integrations with commercial data providers. SEC's modernisation efforts include 'EDGAR Next' authentication and accessibility upgrades through 2024. Foreign private issuers file Form 20-F via EDGAR.

snappy: Federal agency enforcing employment discrimination laws — Title VII, ADA, ADEA, Equal Pay Act, GINA.
detail: Pre-suit administrative requirement: most federal employment-discrimination claims require EEOC charge filing within 180 days (extended to 300 in states with parallel agencies). Charge process: filing, investigation, conciliation, right-to-sue letter (90 days to file lawsuit). EEOC can also litigate cases itself. Annual case volume: 60,000-80,000 charges. Enforcement priorities and guidance documents shape employer compliance practices.

snappy: Contract between egg donor and intended parent(s) covering compensation, medical procedures, parental rights waiver, and future contact.
detail: Egg donation involves substantial medical procedures (hormonal stimulation, egg retrieval surgery) — higher compensation than sperm donation (typically $5,000-$25,000+). ASRM guidelines suggest compensation limits. Anonymous donation reduced by direct-to-consumer DNA testing — most modern arrangements address possibility of future contact. Health-history disclosure obligations increasingly extensive. Surrogacy arrangements often combined with egg donation for complete genetic separation from gestational carrier.

snappy: You take your victim as you find them — even abnormally fragile victims get full damages.
detail: Vosburg v. Putney (Wis. 1891) — schoolboy kick to a pre-injured leg caused unforeseeable serious harm; defendant liable for full extent. Modern application: pre-existing conditions made worse by accidents, psychiatric vulnerabilities exacerbated by minor trauma. The defendant doesn't escape liability because the victim was unusually susceptible to greater harm than ordinary. Foundational fairness doctrine.

snappy: Bans excessive bail, excessive fines, and cruel and unusual punishments.
detail: Cruel-and-unusual-punishment clause: bans torture, disproportionate punishments, executing intellectually disabled (Atkins v. Virginia, 2002) or juveniles (Roper v. Simmons, 2005), and mandatory life-without-parole for juveniles (Miller v. Alabama, 2012). Excessive-fines clause: Timbs v. Indiana (2019) incorporated against states, applied to civil forfeitures. Excessive-bail clause: rarely litigated. Originalist textual analysis (Bucklew v. Precythe, 2019) frames modern Eighth Amendment.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Stealing from someone who entrusted you with their property — the employee bookkeeper who siphons company funds.
detail: Requires lawful initial possession (unlike larceny) followed by fraudulent conversion. Common in corporate fraud cases: executives diverting funds, financial advisors looting client accounts, bank tellers helping themselves. Federal embezzlement statutes cover bank employees (18 U.S.C. § 656), public officials (§ 641), and ERISA plan fiduciaries. Bernie Madoff's Ponzi scheme included massive embezzlement of client funds.

snappy: Government's power to take private property for public use, paying just compensation.
detail: Fifth Amendment grants the power; the just-compensation clause limits it. Public use was broadly defined in Kelo v. New London (2005) to include economic development — sparking a backlash and state-law reforms restricting takings for private redevelopment. Just compensation is usually fair market value at the time of taking, established through appraisal and (if contested) jury trial.

snappy: Contract setting out terms of employment beyond at-will defaults.
detail: Common content: title, duties, compensation, benefits, vacation, IP assignment (for inventions and work product), confidentiality, restrictive covenants, termination conditions, severance. Without an agreement, most US employment is 'at-will' (either side can terminate any time, for any non-discriminatory reason). Executive agreements add change-of-control protections, golden parachutes, and good-reason termination rights.

snappy: Permanent residency through employer sponsorship — five preference categories.
detail: EB-1: priority workers (extraordinary ability, outstanding researchers, multinational managers) — no labour certification needed. EB-2: advanced degrees or exceptional ability; National Interest Waiver (NIW) skips labour cert. EB-3: skilled workers, professionals, other workers. EB-4: special immigrants (religious workers, certain abused immigrants). EB-5: investor immigrants ($800K-$1.05M minimum, job creation). Backlogs: severe for Indian and Chinese nationals in EB-2 and EB-3 (often 5-15+ years).

snappy: A structure (fence, eaves, driveway) extending over onto a neighbour's property without permission.
detail: Remedies: action to compel removal, damages, or sometimes an easement-by-prescription if the encroachment has persisted long enough. Survey-based discovery often arises at sale: buyer's surveyor finds a fence two feet onto the next lot, and the deal hinges on whether neighbours will sign a boundary-line agreement. Minor encroachments are sometimes accepted via 'encroachment agreements' that licence the use without conveying property.

snappy: Federal statute protecting threatened and endangered plant and animal species.
detail: 16 U.S.C. §§ 1531 et seq. (1973). Section 7 requires federal agencies to consult with US Fish and Wildlife Service or NOAA Fisheries on actions that may affect listed species. Section 9 prohibits 'taking' (including habitat modification — Sweet Home, 1995). Section 10 incidental-take permits via Habitat Conservation Plans. Tennessee Valley Authority v. Hill (1978) — snail darter halts dam project — was the foundational case. Listing decisions and critical-habitat designations chronically litigated.

snappy: A formal proceeding by an agency or prosecutor to enforce the law against a specific actor.
detail: SEC files civil enforcement actions in federal court or its own administrative forum. DOJ brings criminal cases. State AGs bring state actions. Targets respond with motions to dismiss, settlement offers, or full litigation. Most actions settle — typical settlement includes fines, injunctive relief, and required compliance improvements. High-profile recent: FTC v. Microsoft (Activision deal), FTC v. Meta (Instagram/WhatsApp divestiture).

snappy: DOJ component running the immigration courts — separate from USCIS (benefits adjudication).
detail: Houses Immigration Courts (trial-level) and Board of Immigration Appeals (administrative appeals). Immigration Judges are DOJ employees, not Article III judges — limited independence. Backlogs reaching 3 million cases (2024) drive long wait times for hearings. Process: detention hearings, master calendar, merits hearings, BIA appeals, federal court review. Calls for independent immigration courts (Article I) recurring; no major legislative action through 2024.

snappy: Federal statute requiring equal pay for equal work — regardless of sex.
detail: 29 U.S.C. § 206(d) (1963). Requires equal pay for jobs requiring substantially equal skill, effort, and responsibility under similar conditions. Permissible pay differentials: seniority, merit, quantity/quality of production, any factor other than sex. Title VII covers broader employment discrimination but Equal Pay Act doesn't require proof of discriminatory intent. Lilly Ledbetter Fair Pay Act (2009) reset statute-of-limitations clock with each discriminatory paycheck. Many states have expanded equal-pay statutes.

snappy: Constitutional guarantee against arbitrary discrimination by government.
detail: Fourteenth Amendment. Three levels of scrutiny: strict (race, national origin, fundamental rights — government must show compelling interest, narrowly tailored), intermediate (sex, illegitimacy — important interest, substantially related), and rational basis (everything else — legitimate interest, rationally related). Modern equal-protection cases: Bostock (2020, sex discrimination includes sexual orientation), Students for Fair Admissions (2023, affirmative action).
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Divorce property division based on fairness — used in 41 US states.
detail: Court divides marital property fairly, considering factors: length of marriage, contributions, economic circumstances, future needs, fault (in some states). 'Equitable' doesn't mean 50-50 — courts can award disproportionate shares. Contrast with community-property states (9 states) where presumption is 50-50. Significant judicial discretion. Major battles over treatment of: businesses, professional licences, stock options, retirement accounts, gifts and inheritances during marriage.

snappy: A restriction on property enforceable in equity (injunction) rather than only at law (damages).
detail: Originated in Tulk v. Moxhay (1848). Allows enforcement against successors with notice — including remote successors with constructive notice from the recorded deed. Modern statutes and Restatement (Third) of Property have largely merged equitable servitudes, real covenants, and equitable easements into a unified 'servitude' doctrine. Practical effect: subdivision restrictions enforceable both by injunction and damages.

snappy: A defaulting borrower's right to reclaim the property by paying off the full debt before foreclosure sale.
detail: Common-law equitable doctrine — borrowers historically could redeem at any time, frustrating lenders. Modern statutes constrain it to a fixed period before sale. Distinct from statutory redemption (post-sale right in some states). Foreclosing requires extinguishing the equity of redemption — which is what foreclosure sale accomplishes. 'Free equity' (the value above the debt) belongs to the borrower; lenders can't keep it.

snappy: Federal statute regulating private-sector employee benefit plans — pensions, 401(k)s, health insurance.
detail: 29 U.S.C. §§ 1001 et seq. (1974). Two main parts: pension regulation (vesting, funding, fiduciary duty) and welfare-benefits-plan regulation (health, disability). Preempts inconsistent state laws (Aetna Health v. Davila, 2004). Fiduciary duties: prudent person standard, exclusive purpose, diversification. Federal jurisdiction. Major recent litigation: 401(k) excessive-fee class actions (Tibble v. Edison, 2015), retiree healthcare disputes.

snappy: Money or documents held by a neutral third party until conditions are met.
detail: In real-estate closings, the escrow agent holds buyer's funds and seller's signed deed, releasing both when closing conditions clear (title, financing, inspection contingencies). Outside real estate, escrow is used in M&A deals (purchase-price holdbacks for indemnity claims), payment platforms, and online marketplaces. Escrow.com handles small-scale online transactions; major banks and title companies handle real-estate closings.

snappy: First Amendment ban on government establishing religion — separation of church and state.
detail: Tests have evolved: Lemon v. Kurtzman (1971) three-part test; replaced by Kennedy v. Bremerton (2022) which favoured 'historical practices and understandings'. Forbids government endorsement, prayer in public schools (Engel v. Vitale, 1962), and most direct religious-instruction funding. Permits ceremonial deism, neutral funding extended to religious organisations (Espinoza v. Montana, 2020). Ongoing battleground over religious displays, school funding, and chaplains.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Federal tax on the transfer of assets at death — applies only to very large estates.
detail: 2024 federal exemption: $13.61 million per individual ($27.22M for married couples with portability). Top rate: 40%. Sunset provision: exemption drops to ~$7M in 2026 absent congressional action. State estate taxes in roughly a dozen states (and DC) with lower thresholds (Massachusetts $2M, Oregon $1M). Charitable bequests and qualified-spouse transfers fully deductible. Sophisticated planning: irrevocable trusts, life-insurance trusts, family limited partnerships, valuation discounts.

snappy: The contract you agree to when installing software.
detail: Grants limited license to use the software, reserving title with the publisher. Typical terms: scope of use (number of devices, commercial/personal), restrictions (no reverse engineering, no redistribution), disclaimer of warranties, limitation of liability, termination on breach. Enforceability depends on adequate notice and opportunity to read; click-through EULAs generally pass; browse-wrap is more contested.

snappy: Evidence obtained through an illegal search or arrest can't be used at trial.
detail: Created by Weeks v. United States (1914) for federal cases, extended to states by Mapp v. Ohio (1961). The rationale is deterring police misconduct, not vindicating defendant's rights. Has been steadily narrowed: good-faith exception (Leon, 1984), inevitable-discovery exception (Nix, 1984), independent-source exception, attenuation doctrine. Hudson v. Michigan (2006) held knock-and-announce violations don't trigger exclusion. Critics say the rule frees the guilty without changing police behaviour.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: President's right to keep certain conversations and documents confidential — to protect candid advice.
detail: United States v. Nixon (1974) recognised the privilege but found it must yield to criminal-investigation needs. Cheney v. United States District Court (2004) gave Vice-President task force discussions some protection. Trump v. Mazars (2020) and Trump v. Vance (2020) addressed congressional subpoenas and state grand juries. Privilege is qualified, not absolute; weighed against the requesting authority's specific need.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: An employee not entitled to FLSA overtime pay — typically white-collar workers meeting specific tests.
detail: Main exemptions: executive (manages a department, supervises 2+ employees), administrative (office or non-manual work, discretion on significant matters), professional (advanced knowledge in field of science or learning), outside sales, computer professional, highly compensated employee. Must meet both duties test and salary test ($35,568/year federal threshold; pending DOL update). Improper exemption classification triggers massive back-wages and liquidated-damages exposure.

snappy: Litigants generally must complete agency processes before going to court.
detail: Lets agencies correct errors, develop record, and apply expertise before judicial intervention. Sometimes statutory (must file claim with agency before suing); sometimes prudential (court discretion). Exceptions: futile remedies, irreparable harm, constitutional challenges, lack of agency authority. ERISA, IDEA, Title VII, Social Security disability — all require exhaustion. Failure usually bars judicial review entirely.

snappy: Emergency situations — hot pursuit, imminent destruction of evidence, threat to life — let police enter without a warrant.
detail: Categories include hot pursuit of a fleeing suspect, evidence destruction (often drugs being flushed), emergency aid, and protection of officers or public. Kentucky v. King (2011) said police-created exigencies don't disqualify so long as the police actions causing the exigency weren't unlawful. Lange v. California (2021) held that the categorical hot-pursuit doctrine doesn't always justify warrantless home entry for misdemeanors — case-by-case analysis.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: The default remedy — money that would put the plaintiff in the position of full performance.
detail: Captures the 'benefit of the bargain' — profit plus reliance, minus what's been saved by not performing. Includes consequential damages if foreseeable (Hadley v. Baxendale). Hard to prove with precision in new-business cases ('lost profits' are speculative). Courts award them when the plaintiff can establish damages with 'reasonable certainty'.

snappy: A witness qualified by knowledge, skill, experience, training, or education to give opinion testimony.
detail: Federal Rule of Evidence 702 governs admissibility — reliable methods, sufficient facts, reliable application. Daubert gatekeeping applies. Common types: economists for damages, doctors for medical causation, forensic experts for DNA/ballistics, engineers for accident reconstruction. Rule 26(a)(2) requires written reports and disclosure deadlines. Battle of the experts often decides modern complex litigation.

snappy: Student visa for full-time academic studies in the US.
detail: Issued for course of study (variable duration). Permits limited on-campus work (20 hours/week during term). Off-campus work generally barred except for Curricular Practical Training (CPT, integrated with curriculum) and Optional Practical Training (OPT, 12 months post-graduation with 24-month STEM extension). SEVP (Student and Exchange Visitor Program) oversight. Spouse and dependent children F-2 visa (no work authorisation). Common transition to H-1B specialty worker visa via OPT period.

snappy: A product is defective if dangers weren't adequately disclosed — warnings can make safe an otherwise dangerous product.
detail: 'Open and obvious' dangers usually don't require warnings (kitchen knife is sharp). Sophisticated-user doctrine excuses warnings to experts. 'Learned intermediary' doctrine routes drug warnings through prescribing physicians, not patients (though direct-to-consumer advertising has eroded this). Adequate warning analysis: form, content, location, conspicuousness. Tobacco litigation rebooted this doctrine despite extensive labelling.

snappy: Limited use of copyrighted material without permission — for criticism, news, teaching, parody.
detail: Four-factor test: purpose of use (commercial vs nonprofit, transformative?), nature of the original, amount used, and effect on the market. Transformative use is the heavyweight factor since Campbell v. Acuff-Rose (1994). The Andy Warhol Foundation v. Goldsmith (2023) clarified that purpose matters as a separate matter from artistic transformation. Generative AI training data cases (NYT v. OpenAI) are the next major fair-use frontier.

snappy: Unlawfully restraining someone's freedom of movement.
detail: Requires intentional confinement to a bounded area, awareness or harm to the plaintiff, and no privilege or consent. Shop owners face the 'shopkeeper's privilege' — limited authority to detain suspected shoplifters with reasonable cause and reasonable duration. Schools, hospitals, and police have specific authority that can override otherwise actionable confinement. Often paired with claims for negligent infliction of emotional distress.

snappy: Publishing material that puts someone in a false but not necessarily defamatory light — close cousin of defamation.
detail: Restatement § 652E. Differences from defamation: requires publicity (broad dissemination), no need for damaging-reputation showing, but actual malice required for public-figure plaintiffs (Time v. Hill, 1967). Some states reject the tort as redundant with defamation. Heavily criticised for confusing overlap. Often pleaded alongside defamation as alternative theory; rarely the sole successful claim.

snappy: Specialised state courts handling divorce, custody, child support, adoption, juvenile delinquency, and domestic violence.
detail: Structure varies by state: dedicated family-court division, family-court department within general civil court, or general jurisdiction handling family matters. Increasingly therapeutic-oriented: mediation, parenting education, custody evaluations, mental-health resources. High emotional stakes, often pro-se litigants. Self-help centres widespread to assist unrepresented parties. Differential modern judges' education and skill in domestic-violence dynamics, child development, and cultural competence.

snappy: A neutral third party helping divorcing or separating couples negotiate agreements on property, support, custody.
detail: Court-required in many states before contested hearings. Mediator (often retired judge or specialised attorney) doesn't decide — facilitates parties' agreement. Advantages: cheaper, faster, more flexible than litigation; preserves relationships for ongoing co-parenting. Some states have free or reduced-fee mediation services. Domestic-violence cases often excluded due to power imbalances. Mediation agreements typically formalised in court order.

snappy: Permanent residency through US citizen or LPR family member sponsorship.
detail: Two main types: immediate relatives (US-citizen spouses, parents, unmarried minor children — no annual cap) and family-preference categories (F1: adult unmarried citizen children; F2: spouses and unmarried children of LPRs; F3: married adult citizen children; F4: siblings of adult US citizens). Family-preference caps create multi-year (often decade-plus) backlogs in some categories. Spouse cases routinely scrutinised for fraud — conditional residence for 2 years before full LPR.

snappy: An employer's affirmative defence to supervisor-harassment claims when there's no tangible adverse employment action.
detail: Faragher v. City of Boca Raton (1998) and Burlington Industries v. Ellerth (1998). Two elements: (1) employer exercised reasonable care to prevent and correct harassment (anti-harassment policy, complaint procedure, training); (2) plaintiff unreasonably failed to take advantage of preventive or corrective opportunities. Drives modern HR practices: policy implementation, training, prompt investigation. Doesn't apply when tangible employment action results from harassment.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

full: Foreign Bank Account Report / FinCEN Form 114
snappy: Required US filing for individuals with $10,000+ aggregate in foreign financial accounts.
detail: Bank Secrecy Act enforcement. Due April 15 with automatic extension to October 15. Penalties extreme: $10,000 per violation for non-wilful; greater of $100K or 50% of account balance for wilful. Bittner v. United States (2023) limited non-wilful penalties to per-report (not per-account). Companion: Foreign Account Tax Compliance Act (FATCA) Form 8938 for tax-return-based foreign-asset reporting. Aggressive enforcement post-UBS prosecution in 2009.

snappy: Federal Advisory Committee Act regulates outside-expert groups advising the executive branch.
detail: 5 U.S.C. App. 2. Requires charter, fair-balance membership, open meetings, public records. Triggered when a committee is 'established or utilized' by federal agency. Cheney v. United States District Court (2004) addressed application to internal task forces with industry consultants. Cumbersome procedures sometimes drive agencies to use industry-funded studies or other workarounds rather than form FACA committees.

snappy: The federal tax on individual and business earnings — funds most of the federal budget.
detail: Progressive structure for individuals: brackets ranging from 10% to 37% (post-TCJA). Corporate flat rate 21%. Calculation: gross income → adjusted gross income → taxable income → tentative tax → credits → final liability. Returns due April 15 (with extension to October 15). Withholding from wages and estimated quarterly payments avoid year-end lump-sum due. Audit risk based on income level, return type, claims of certain deductions/credits.

snappy: Valid federal law overrides conflicting state law under the Supremacy Clause.
detail: Three types: express preemption (federal statute says so), conflict preemption (compliance with both impossible, or state law obstructs federal purpose), field preemption (Congress occupies an entire regulatory field). Major battlegrounds: drug labelling (Wyeth v. Levine, 2009), motor-vehicle safety, agricultural-chemical regulation, banking (national-bank preemption), employment (ERISA preemption). Presumption against preemption in fields historically state-regulated.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Created the FTC and prohibited 'unfair methods of competition' and 'unfair or deceptive acts or practices'.
detail: 15 U.S.C. §§ 41–58 (1914). Section 5 is the workhorse: 'unfair methods of competition' covers antitrust-adjacent conduct; 'unfair or deceptive acts' covers consumer protection. FTC has rulemaking, adjudicative, and civil-penalty authority. AMG Capital Management v. FTC (2021) eliminated § 13(b) restitution authority; pending legislation through 2024 sought to restore. Khan-era FTC has issued more rulemaking (non-compete ban among others) facing pre-enforcement challenges.

snappy: Federal courts can hear any case 'arising under' federal law — the Constitution, federal statutes, or treaties.
detail: 28 U.S.C. § 1331. The 'well-pleaded complaint' rule means federal jurisdiction must appear from the plaintiff's claim, not a federal defence. Some statutes have exclusive federal jurisdiction (patent, copyright, antitrust); others are concurrent with state courts (FELA, FLSA). Federal-question is the workhorse jurisdiction — most non-diversity federal cases land here.

snappy: Division of power between federal and state governments.
detail: Federal government has enumerated powers (Constitution Article I); states have everything else (Tenth Amendment). Supremacy Clause means federal law overrides conflicting state law. Modern federalism fights: cannabis (illegal federally, legal in many states), abortion post-Dobbs, immigration enforcement, ERISA preemption of state benefits regulation. Commerce Clause cases (Lopez 1995, Sebelius 2012) test the outer limits of federal power.

snappy: The strongest form of property ownership — full rights to use, sell, lease, or pass by will.
detail: The default modern estate when property is conveyed without limitations. Distinguishable from lesser estates like life estates or defeasible fees. 'Fee simple absolute' is the unconditional version; 'defeasible' fee simples (determinable, subject to condition subsequent) can revert on specified events. Almost all residential property in the US is held in fee simple. Subject to state regulation, taxation, eminent domain, and lawful zoning.

snappy: A serious crime — generally punishable by more than a year in prison.
detail: Includes murder, manslaughter, rape, robbery, burglary, fraud above certain thresholds, drug-trafficking offences. Felony convictions trigger collateral consequences: loss of voting rights (varies by state), firearms restrictions, immigration consequences, professional licensing barriers, housing eligibility. Recent reform movement has narrowed mandatory-minimums (First Step Act, 2018) and broadened expungement.

snappy: If someone dies during certain dangerous felonies, every participant can be charged with murder — even if no one meant to kill.
detail: The robber whose getaway driver runs over a pedestrian faces murder charges. Triggering felonies usually listed by statute: robbery, burglary, arson, kidnapping, sexual assault. Critics argue the doctrine punishes results rather than intent and produces life sentences for accomplices who never wielded a weapon.
