England abolished the rule in 1957; most US states retain it in some form. Tison v. Arizona (1987) upheld death-penalty application to major participants showing reckless indifference.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: A legal obligation to act in someone else's best interests — owed by directors to corporation, lawyers to clients, trustees to beneficiaries.
detail: The hallmark of trust-relationship law. Two main components: duty of care (informed, attentive decisions) and duty of loyalty (avoiding self-dealing and conflicts). Cardozo's famous formulation in Meinhard v. Salmon (1928): 'Not honesty alone, but the punctilio of an honor the most sensitive.' Foundation of corporate governance, trust administration, and professional responsibility. Breach exposes fiduciary to personal liability and equitable remedies.

snappy: Self-incrimination, grand jury, double jeopardy, due process, takings.
detail: 'I plead the Fifth' invokes the privilege against self-incrimination — applies in any proceeding where testimony could expose to criminal liability. Includes due-process protections, double-jeopardy bar, requirement of grand-jury indictment for federal felonies, and the just-compensation requirement for eminent domain. Often paired with Miranda warnings during interrogation.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Personally provocative face-to-face insults likely to incite immediate violence — limited First Amendment protection.
detail: Chaplinsky v. New Hampshire (1942) defined the doctrine. Modern Supreme Court has narrowed it sharply — virtually no convictions upheld since 1942. Distinguishable from incitement (Brandenburg, 1969 — requires intent and likelihood of imminent lawless action). State courts often overturn fighting-words convictions on overbreadth or vagueness grounds. Largely a doctrinal relic with continuing residual influence on hate-speech debates.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Only completed agency actions are reviewable — interim or preliminary actions usually aren't.
detail: APA § 704. Test from Bennett v. Spear (1997): action consummates the agency's decision-making and is one from which legal consequences flow. Important threshold for litigation: regulated entities often want to challenge pre-enforcement actions; courts often require waiting for final agency position. Sackett v. EPA (2012) treated EPA compliance orders as final agency actions despite EPA's contrary position.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Free speech, free press, religion, assembly, and petition.
detail: Content-based restrictions face strict scrutiny; content-neutral restrictions get intermediate scrutiny. Categories outside protection: incitement to imminent lawless action (Brandenburg, 1969), true threats, defamation, obscenity, fighting words, child sexual abuse material. Free Exercise vs Establishment Clause tension drives religion cases. Recent battlegrounds: social-media regulation (NetChoice 2024), school speech (Mahanoy, 2021).
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Killing someone with intent and premeditation — the most serious criminal charge.
detail: Distinguished from second-degree murder by 'premeditation and deliberation' or by being committed during certain felonies (felony-murder rule). Eligible for the harshest sentences: life without parole or, in death-penalty jurisdictions, capital punishment. Most states subdivide murder into degrees by statute; federal first-degree murder (18 U.S.C. § 1111) covers killings in special maritime/territorial jurisdiction.

snappy: Once a copy is lawfully sold, the buyer can resell or lend it without further permission from the copyright owner.
detail: 17 U.S.C. § 109. Foundation of used-book stores, libraries, video rental, and resale markets. Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons (2013) extended to copies lawfully made abroad. Software's licence-not-sold structure narrows the doctrine — Vernor v. Autodesk (9th Cir. 2010) gave software licensors enforceable resale restrictions. Digital first-sale debate remains unresolved (Capitol Records v. ReDigi rejected digital first sale). Right to repair efforts increasingly engage similar themes.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Copyright protection attaches the moment a work is recorded in some tangible medium — paper, file, video tape, server disk.
detail: 17 U.S.C. § 102(a) requires fixation 'in a tangible medium of expression'. Live, unrecorded performances don't qualify (unless simultaneously recorded with authorisation). Digital storage clearly counts. The 'transitory duration' question: are RAM copies fixed? MAI Systems v. Peak (9th Cir. 1993) said yes; Cartoon Network v. CSC Holdings (2d Cir. 2008) said brief buffering is not.

snappy: Personal property attached to real estate so firmly it becomes part of the land.
detail: Built-in dishwashers, fireplaces, light fixtures, plumbing — these transfer with a house sale. Free-standing appliances, area rugs, and personal furniture don't. The legal test: physical attachment, adaptation to the property, and intent. Disputes are common at closing — buyers expect chandeliers; sellers think they're keeping them. Best practice: specify in the contract what stays and what goes.

snappy: Federal statute setting minimum wage, overtime, recordkeeping, and child-labour standards.
detail: 29 U.S.C. §§ 201 et seq. (1938). Covers most US employees. Minimum wage $7.25/hour federal (states often higher). Overtime: 1.5x regular rate for hours over 40 per week, unless exempt. Exemption categories: executive, administrative, professional, outside sales, computer professional, highly compensated. Salary threshold $35,568 (DOL 2024 rule attempted to raise to $58,656; litigation pending). Tip credit allows lower base for tipped workers if tips bring total to minimum. State laws often more protective.

snappy: Federal statute giving eligible employees up to 12 weeks of unpaid leave per year for family or medical reasons.
detail: 29 U.S.C. §§ 2601 et seq. (1993). Eligible: 12+ months with employer, 1,250+ hours worked, employer with 50+ employees within 75 miles. Qualifying reasons: birth/adoption, family member's serious health condition, employee's serious health condition, military exigency. Job-protected — must return to same or equivalent position. State paid-leave laws supplement (California, New York, Washington, others). Many employers provide enhanced paid leave benefits.

snappy: A clause excusing performance when 'acts of God' or named disruptive events occur.
detail: From the French 'superior force'. Triggers vary by clause: wars, strikes, pandemics, government action, supply-chain failure. COVID-19 spawned a wave of force-majeure disputes; results turned on whether the clause specifically listed pandemics or just 'unforeseeable events'. Best-practice modern clauses list named events and add a catch-all, with notice obligations and a duty to mitigate.

snappy: The legal process letting a mortgage lender force the sale of property after default.
detail: Two regimes: judicial foreclosure (court-supervised, the rule in about half of US states) and non-judicial foreclosure (out-of-court, faster, the rest). Borrowers have rights to notice, cure (catch up on payments), and sometimes redemption (buy back after sale). Federal CARES Act (2020) and CFPB rules added moratoriums during COVID. Foreclosure can leave the borrower with a deficiency judgment if proceeds don't cover the debt — depends on the state.

snappy: Federal statute requiring agents of foreign principals to register with the Department of Justice.
detail: 22 U.S.C. §§ 611 et seq. (1938). Originally enacted to counter Nazi propaganda. Modern enforcement revived dramatically under Mueller investigation (2017-2019) — Paul Manafort and Rick Gates among prosecuted figures. Disclosure: identity of foreign principal, activities, compensation. Exemptions for diplomats, lawyers in adversarial proceedings, religious-academic activities. Civil and criminal penalties; willful failure to register can be charged as a felony.

snappy: A defendant is only liable for harms a reasonable person could have foreseen — limits liability scope.
detail: Threads through duty, breach, and proximate cause analysis. Palsgraf v. Long Island R.R. (1928) — fireworks exploded; harm to distant plaintiff not foreseeable. Modern criticism: 'foreseeable' is a conclusion masquerading as analysis. Type-of-harm and manner-of-harm distinctions: foreseeable type usually suffices even if manner is bizarre (Wagon Mound No. 2, 1967).

snappy: The comprehensive annual disclosure document public companies file with the SEC.
detail: Major sections: business description, risk factors, management discussion and analysis (MD&A), audited financial statements, executive compensation, related-party transactions. Filing deadlines: 60 days after fiscal year-end for large accelerated filers, 90 days for non-accelerated. Subject to CEO/CFO certifications under SOX. Foundation of US public-company disclosure. Increasingly contains ESG and cybersecurity disclosure.

snappy: Quarterly disclosure document filed with the SEC — unaudited financial statements plus updates to risks and operations.
detail: Required for first three quarters; the fourth quarter folds into the 10-K. Filing deadlines: 40 days after quarter-end for large accelerated filers; 45 days for non-accelerated. CEO/CFO certifications required. Forward-looking statements protected by safe harbour under Private Securities Litigation Reform Act. Frequent quarterly cycle has been challenged — some advocate moving to semi-annual reporting like UK; opponents stress investor information needs.

snappy: The main US individual income tax return form.
detail: Annual filing required for most US citizens and residents with income above thresholds. Two-page core form with multiple supporting schedules: Schedule A (itemised deductions), Schedule B (interest/dividends), Schedule C (sole-proprietor business), Schedule D (capital gains), Schedule E (rental/royalty), Schedule SE (self-employment tax). Substantial overhaul in 2018 (TCJA), simplified versions (1040-SR for seniors). Filing deadlines and extension procedures standard.

snappy: Form for reporting material corporate events between periodic filings — earnings releases, executive changes, M&A, defaults.
detail: Triggering events listed in 23 enumerated items. Filing within 4 business days. Items include: entry into material agreement, asset acquisition/disposition, bankruptcy, officer/director changes, regulatory enforcement, cybersecurity incidents (added 2023). Real-time disclosure mechanism — supplements but doesn't replace 10-K/10-Q. Selective-disclosure violations addressed through Regulation FD compliance.

snappy: The standard registration statement for an IPO — comprehensive disclosure about the company, offering, and risks.
detail: Required sections: business description, risk factors, use of proceeds, dividend policy, capitalisation, dilution, MD&A, audited financial statements, principal stockholders. SEC review process: initial comments, responses, multiple amendments. JOBS Act (2012) allowed emerging-growth companies to file confidentially. 'Quiet period' restrictions on publicity before and after filing. Cousin Form S-3 used for follow-on offerings by seasoned issuers.

snappy: Agency rulemaking requiring trial-type proceedings — rare due to procedural burden.
detail: Triggered only when authorising statute requires rules to be made 'on the record after opportunity for an agency hearing' (APA §§ 556–557). Includes sworn testimony, cross-examination, transcript, on-the-record decision. Famous example: FDA peanut-butter rulemaking (took 12 years for definition). Most agencies have abandoned formal rulemaking in favour of informal notice-and-comment. United States v. Florida East Coast Railway (1973) read the trigger narrowly.

snappy: Even if a court has jurisdiction, it can dismiss the case when another forum is dramatically more appropriate.
detail: Gulf Oil v. Gilbert (1947) set the federal framework: weighed private factors (witnesses, evidence, costs) and public factors (court congestion, local interest, governing law). Most commonly invoked when foreign plaintiffs sue in US courts for events abroad. Piper Aircraft v. Reyno (1981) made it harder for foreign plaintiffs to keep their case in the US. In federal court, internal transfer under 28 U.S.C. § 1404 has largely supplanted the doctrine domestically.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Clause picking the court or arbitration forum where disputes must be filed.
detail: Carnival Cruise v. Shute (1991) upheld a forum clause in a passenger ticket — even one printed on the back. Courts generally enforce them unless 'unreasonable and unjust' or product of fraud. Different from choice-of-law clauses (which pick the substantive law, not the courthouse). Most boilerplate pairs both: 'governed by Delaware law, courts of Delaware'.

snappy: Temporary placement of children whose parents can't safely care for them — state-administered.
detail: Federal Child Abuse Prevention and Treatment Act funds. State child-welfare agencies (CPS/DCFS) make placement decisions, subject to court oversight. Goal typically reunification with biological parents; if not feasible, termination of parental rights and adoption. Foster parents licensed by state, receive monthly stipend for child's basic expenses. Fostered children eligible for Medicaid, special-education protections, college tuition assistance in some states.

snappy: Post-Civil War amendment guaranteeing due process, equal protection, and privileges of citizenship.
detail: Section 1's three clauses: Citizenship (overruling Dred Scott), Privileges or Immunities (Slaughter-House-narrowed), Due Process and Equal Protection. Section 5 gives Congress enforcement power. Heart of modern individual-rights doctrine — the vehicle for applying most Bill of Rights protections against states (incorporation doctrine). Equal Protection drove Brown v. Board (1954) school desegregation; Loving v. Virginia (1967) struck miscegenation bans; Obergefell (2015) recognised same-sex marriage.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Protection from unreasonable searches and seizures.
detail: Warrant generally required for searches; exceptions include consent, plain view, exigent circumstances, automobile, search incident to arrest, border. Modern frontiers: cell-site data (Carpenter, 2018), GPS tracking (Jones, 2012), aerial surveillance, third-party doctrine (information shared with banks/phone companies traditionally outside Fourth Amendment protection). Exclusionary rule (Mapp, 1961) is the main remedy: suppress evidence illegally obtained.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: A contract where a franchisor licences trademark and operating system to a franchisee in exchange for fees.
detail: FTC Franchise Rule (16 C.F.R. § 436) requires Franchise Disclosure Document (FDD) before sale — extensive financial, structural, and operational disclosure. State franchise laws (about 15 states) impose registration and regulatory requirements. Termination, renewal, and transfer rights heavily regulated in many states. Recent reform: California's franchise non-compete restrictions, fair-franchise-relationship debates. Mature regulatory area.

snappy: Lying to convince someone to sign a contract whose terms they understand — voidable but not void.
detail: Distinguished from fraud in the execution (lying about what the document is). Contract enforceable until rescinded; victim may also sue for tort damages. Securities fraud, M&A misrepresentation, and consumer-protection cases routinely invoke this theory. The 'reliance' element draws scrutiny when sophisticated parties had access to truth. Integration clauses and disclaimer provisions try to shield against fraud-in-the-inducement claims with mixed enforcement.

snappy: First Amendment protection for individuals' practice of religion.
detail: Employment Division v. Smith (1990) held that neutral laws of general applicability don't violate Free Exercise even if they burden religion. Fulton v. Philadelphia (2021) narrowed Smith — laws with secular exemptions must extend religious exemptions or pass strict scrutiny. Religious Freedom Restoration Act (1993) restored strict scrutiny for federal action. Recent expansion: religious schools, religious vaccination exemptions, religious-employer accommodation.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: First Amendment protection against government restrictions on what people can say.
detail: Heavily protective: most regulation of speech content is presumptively unconstitutional. Categorical exclusions: obscenity, incitement to imminent lawless action (Brandenburg v. Ohio, 1969), true threats, fighting words, child pornography. Government speech doctrine (Walker v. Texas Division, 2015) lets government control its own messages. Robust modern protection of corporate speech (Citizens United, 2010) and offensive speech (Snyder v. Phelps, 2011).
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Federal statute creating a right of public access to government records.
detail: 5 U.S.C. § 552 (1966). Default presumption of disclosure with nine exemptions (national security, deliberative process, trade secrets, personal privacy, law enforcement, etc.). State-level analogues vary widely. Requesters: anyone — citizens, journalists, NGOs, foreign nationals, businesses. Response deadlines (20 working days) often substantially missed; litigation routinely required for production. Glomar response: agency refuses to confirm or deny existence of records.

snappy: Evidence derived from an illegal search is also excluded — the poisoned tree's fruit is also poisoned.
detail: Silverthorne Lumber v. United States (1920); name from Wong Sun v. United States (1963). Extends the exclusionary rule beyond direct fruits to derivative evidence: a confession after an illegal arrest, witness testimony obtained from illegally seized address books, leads followed up from a tainted source. Exceptions: independent source, inevitable discovery, attenuation (the connection became sufficiently weak). A core doctrine in suppression motions.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Discharge of a contract because the reason for entering it has been destroyed.
detail: Both parties can still perform, but the underlying purpose has vanished. Krell v. Henry (1903) — a flat rented to view King Edward VII's coronation; the king got sick, the parade cancelled. The flat was still available, but the purpose was gone. US courts apply it narrowly. COVID-era restaurant-lease cases have refined the doctrine: lockdowns frustrating the purpose vs merely making performance harder.

snappy: Older state-court test: expert testimony admissible only if the underlying technique is 'generally accepted' in the relevant field.
detail: Frye v. United States (D.C. Cir. 1923) — originally about a polygraph predecessor. Most states have moved to Daubert, but New York, California (modified), Pennsylvania, and several others still apply Frye. The standards diverge most on novel science: a methodology can be reliable yet not 'generally accepted' yet. Choice-of-law issues in toxic-tort multidistrict litigation often hinge on which test governs.

snappy: An ownership interest that doesn't take possession until later — after a life estate ends, a condition occurs, or a term expires.
detail: Five types: reversions, remainders (vested or contingent), possibilities of reverter, rights of entry, and executory interests. Created mostly through deeds and trusts. The Rule Against Perpetuities historically restricted contingent future interests to those vesting within a life-plus-21-years; many states have abolished or relaxed the rule. Future interests show up most in estate planning and long-term leases.

snappy: Paying an employee to stay home during their notice period — to keep them away from competitors.
detail: Originated in UK financial services; spreading in US tech and finance. Distinguishable from non-compete (which restricts post-termination activity) — garden leave maintains employment with pay while restricting work. Often more enforceable than non-competes because employee remains compensated. Tail-period substitute as state laws restrict traditional non-competes (FTC's 2024 non-compete ban litigation, California's expansive non-compete prohibition).

snappy: EU privacy law with global reach over companies handling EU residents' data.
detail: Effective 2018. Requires lawful basis for processing personal data, gives users rights (access, deletion, portability), and mandates breach notification within 72 hours. Fines up to 4% of global annual revenue or €20M. Has driven privacy programs at global companies and inspired similar laws — California CCPA, Brazil LGPD, India DPDPA. US companies serving EU customers must comply or face enforcement by EU data-protection authorities.

snappy: Two or more people doing business together for profit — formed by default without filing.
detail: Uniform Partnership Act / Revised Uniform Partnership Act adopted in most states. Each partner has unlimited personal liability for partnership debts. Each partner is an agent of the partnership. Profits and losses pass through to partners' personal taxes. Partnership formation can be inadvertent — sharing profits and management of a business creates one. Common business structure for professionals (lawyers, accountants) historically; mostly replaced by LLPs.

snappy: Additional tax on transfers to grandchildren or more remote descendants — to prevent skipping the middle generation's estate tax.
detail: 40% rate; exemption matches estate-tax exemption (~$13.61M 2024). Lifetime allocations to dynasty trusts powerful estate-planning vehicle. Predeceased parent exception. Direct skips (gift to grandchild), taxable distributions (from trust to skip person), taxable terminations (trust interest passing to skip person). Major focus of ultra-high-net-worth planning. Sophisticated 'dynasty trusts' can hold assets across multiple generations.

snappy: When a trademark becomes the common name for a category — Aspirin, Escalator, Cellophane, Thermos.
detail: The trademark dies because it no longer identifies a single source. Bayer lost 'Aspirin'; Otis lost 'Escalator'; DuPont lost 'Cellophane'. Modern fighters: Xerox ('photocopy, don't Xerox'), Google ('search, don't google' — though the brand survives), Velcro (its musical video 'Don't Say Velcro'). Trademark owners invest heavily in advertising correcting public usage. Primary-significance test: what does the relevant public understand the term to mean?

snappy: Surrogacy where the surrogate isn't genetically related to the child — embryos transferred via IVF.
detail: Now the dominant surrogacy form (95%+ of arrangements). Genetic parents may be intended parents, donors, or combinations. Intended parents' legal connection clearer than traditional surrogacy: most surrogacy-friendly states permit pre-birth parentage orders. Distinguished from traditional surrogacy (surrogate's egg fertilised, often more legally complex). International gestational surrogacy in Ukraine, Georgia, and other lower-cost destinations raises additional jurisdictional and immigration issues.

snappy: Federal tax on transfers of property during life — paired with estate tax under unified credit.
detail: Annual exclusion: $18,000 per recipient per year (2024); unlimited number of recipients. Excess gifts use lifetime exemption (unified with estate tax — same ~$13.61M). Spousal gifts unlimited (US-citizen spouses). Tuition and medical payments made directly to providers exempt. Generation-skipping gifts (to grandchildren/younger) subject to additional GST tax. Used strategically for wealth transfer to reduce estate tax exposure.

snappy: Federal statute banning genetic-information discrimination in employment and health insurance.
detail: Public Law 110-233 (2008). Title I covers health insurance; Title II covers employment. Prohibits employers from collecting genetic information, basing decisions on it, or requiring genetic testing. Family medical history counts as genetic information. Limited 'voluntary wellness program' exception. EEOC enforcement. Increasingly relevant as direct-to-consumer DNA testing (23andMe, Ancestry) has spread and employers contemplate wellness incentives tied to family history.

snappy: A public company is acquired (often by management or sponsor-led group) and ceases public reporting.
detail: Triggers heightened disclosure under SEC Rule 13e-3. Fairness opinions, special committee of disinterested directors, majority-of-minority vote often used to defend transaction. Going-private freezes intensely scrutinised in Delaware Chancery: entire-fairness review unless 'MFW' procedural protections used (Kahn v. M&F Worldwide). Examples include Dell go-private (2013), Twitter/Musk acquisition (2022).

snappy: Evidence obtained from a defective warrant is still admissible if officers reasonably relied on it.
detail: United States v. Leon (1984). Applies when officers act in 'objectively reasonable reliance' on a warrant later found defective. Doesn't apply if the affidavit was deliberately false, the magistrate abandoned judicial role, the warrant was facially deficient, or the supporting affidavit lacked indicia of probable cause. Davis v. United States (2011) extended good faith to reliance on then-binding appellate precedent. A major dilution of the exclusionary rule.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Clause specifying which state's or country's law applies to interpret the contract.
detail: Standard in any contract between parties from different jurisdictions. Courts generally enforce the chosen law if it has a 'reasonable relationship' to the parties or transaction. Some areas (consumer protection, employment) limit choice-of-law freedom. New York and Delaware law are popular for commercial deals because their case law is deep, sophisticated, and businesses-friendly.

snappy: Federal statute requiring multimember agency meetings to be open to the public.
detail: 5 U.S.C. § 552b (1976). Applies to agencies headed by a collegial body (FCC, SEC, FTC, etc.). Ten exemptions parallel to FOIA's. Routine effect on agency conduct: closed meetings require advance announcement and majority vote of members; transcripts of closed sessions kept on file. Many agencies avoid Sunshine by using two-commissioner subcommittees or written 'circulation' procedures.

snappy: A group of citizens that decides whether prosecutors have enough evidence to charge someone with a serious crime.
detail: Constitutionally required for federal felonies (Fifth Amendment); about half the states still use grand juries, the rest use 'informations' filed by prosecutors. Operates in secret; only prosecutors present evidence and witnesses; no judge, no defence counsel, no cross-examination. Notoriously prosecutor-friendly — Chief Judge Sol Wachtler quipped a grand jury would 'indict a ham sandwich'. Federal Rule of Criminal Procedure 6 governs operation.

snappy: California's mid-strength deed — implied warranty that the grantor hasn't already conveyed and that no encumbrances were created by grantor.
detail: California Civil Code § 1113 implies two limited warranties: grantor hasn't already conveyed, and grantor hasn't encumbered the property except as disclosed. Less protection than a warranty deed (no warranty against pre-grantor defects) but more than a quitclaim. Standard form in California residential sales, paired with title insurance for the gap. Other Western states use similar 'limited warranty' or 'special warranty' deed forms.

snappy: Documentation of lawful permanent resident status — allowing US residence and work without restriction.
detail: Two-year (conditional, marriage/investor) or 10-year (renewable). Paths to obtain: family-based, employment-based, asylum/refugee, lottery (Diversity Visa), special programs. Confers most rights of citizens except: voting in federal elections, serving on federal juries, certain federal employment. Triggers tax residency. Abandonment risk: extended absences (over 6 months requiring re-entry permits, over a year potentially abandoning status). Pathway to citizenship after 3-5 years.

snappy: Court-appointed authority to make decisions for someone unable to do so for themselves — typically minors or incapacitated adults.
detail: Two parts: guardian of the person (personal/medical decisions) and guardian of the estate (financial decisions). Procedure: petition, capacity evaluation, court hearing, ongoing court supervision. For minors: typically arises after death of parents or when parents can't fulfill responsibilities. For incapacitated adults: extensive due-process protections required (capacity, less-restrictive-alternatives analysis). Modern trend: supported decision-making as alternative to plenary guardianship.

snappy: Non-immigrant work visa for 'specialty occupations' requiring a bachelor's degree or equivalent.
detail: Annual cap: 65,000 regular plus 20,000 advanced-degree (US master's). Lottery system since registrations exceed cap. Initial 3 years, renewable to 6. Specialty-occupation requirements: theoretical or technical expertise, US bachelor's degree or equivalent in specific field. Major employers: tech companies, consulting firms, healthcare systems. Heavy debate around displacement effects vs critical-skills needs. Spouse H-4 can get work authorisation in limited cases. Major route to employment-based green cards via subsequent EB filing.

snappy: Court order requiring the government to justify someone's detention.
detail: Constitutionally protected (Article I, Section 9 — Congress may suspend only during invasion or rebellion). Federal habeas under 28 USC § 2254 is the main vehicle for state prisoners to challenge their convictions in federal court after exhausting state remedies. AEDPA (1996) sharply restricted the scope. Guantanamo detainees won habeas rights in Boumediene v. Bush (2008). One of the oldest legal protections in common law.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Premerger notification requirement — large mergers must be reported to antitrust agencies before closing.
detail: Clayton Act § 7A (1976). Reporting thresholds (currently around $111.4M deal value with various tests). 30-day waiting period (15 days for cash tenders). Second-request investigations dive deep into industry and competitive effects. Many deals are restructured to win clearance. Filing fees substantial; non-compliance penalties severe. The structural gating mechanism for US merger control.

snappy: Federal statute restricting political activities by federal civil servants.
detail: 5 U.S.C. §§ 7321–7326. Prohibits running for partisan elective office, using official authority to influence elections, soliciting political contributions in federal workplace. Originally 1939; modernised 2012. Office of Special Counsel investigates violations; Merit Systems Protection Board hears appeals. Sanctions range from reprimand to removal. Recent flashpoints: federal employees' political social-media posts, attendance at political rallies.

snappy: A criminal offence motivated by bias against the victim's race, religion, sexual orientation, gender, or disability.
detail: Federal Hate Crimes Prevention Act (Matthew Shepard Act, 2009) expanded coverage from race/religion/ethnicity to sexual orientation, gender, gender identity, and disability. Hate crimes are not standalone offences in most cases — they're sentencing enhancements added to underlying crimes (assault, vandalism, murder). Wisconsin v. Mitchell (1993) upheld penalty enhancements against First Amendment challenge. 50 states plus DC have hate-crime laws, with widely varying coverage.

snappy: An out-of-court statement offered for the truth of what it asserts — generally not admissible.
detail: Federal Rule of Evidence 801. The rule reflects mistrust of statements not made under oath and not subject to cross-examination. Rules 803 and 804 carve dozens of exceptions — business records, excited utterances, statements against interest, dying declarations. Crawford v. Washington (2004) overlaid a Confrontation Clause analysis on hearsay in criminal cases: 'testimonial' out-of-court statements need cross-examination opportunity.

full: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act
snappy: Federal law protecting US patient health information.
detail: Privacy Rule limits disclosure of protected health information (PHI). Security Rule requires technical and administrative safeguards. Breach Notification Rule requires notice within 60 days. Covered entities: healthcare providers, health plans, healthcare clearinghouses. Business associates (cloud vendors, EHR systems) must sign HIPAA business-associate agreements (BAAs). Enforced by HHS Office for Civil Rights with penalties up to $1.9M per violation category per year.

snappy: A tenant who stays past the end of the lease without landlord agreement on new terms.
detail: Landlord's options: treat as trespasser and evict, accept rent and create a new tenancy (typically periodic), or hold to a new fixed term under common law. Some leases include holdover provisions specifying double rent during overstay. Eviction process required — self-help (changing locks, removing belongings) is forbidden in essentially all states. Commercial leases routinely include heightened holdover-rent provisions.

snappy: A revolving credit line secured by the equity in your home — borrow as needed up to a limit.
detail: Junior to the first mortgage in most cases. Interest is variable, tied to prime rate plus margin. Draw period typically 10 years, repayment period 20. Tax-deductibility of interest is limited after the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (2017) — only deductible if used to buy, build, or substantially improve the home. Default can lead to foreclosure. Lenders can reduce or freeze HELOC availability when property values drop.

snappy: A workplace permeated with severe or pervasive harassment that alters working conditions.
detail: Standard from Harris v. Forklift Systems (1993): conduct must be both objectively and subjectively hostile. Factors: frequency, severity, physical threats or humiliation, interference with work performance. Single severe incident can suffice (especially physical assault, racial slur from supervisor). Employer liability framework: Faragher v. City of Boca Raton (1998) and Burlington Industries v. Ellerth (1998) — strict liability for supervisor harassment with tangible adverse action; affirmative defence available for non-tangible-action supervisor harassment.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Limited common-law protection against free-riding on time-sensitive news — created by International News Service v. Associated Press (1918).
detail: Largely preempted by federal copyright law. NBA v. Motorola (2d Cir. 1997) preserved a narrow zone for non-copyrightable hot news: (1) time-sensitive information, (2) substantial cost to gather, (3) directly competing use, (4) free-riding that would destroy the incentive to gather. Increasingly invoked unsuccessfully by news organisations against aggregators. Often replaced by contract-based controls (paywall-bypass restrictions, API terms).

snappy: A jury unable to reach a unanimous (or near-unanimous) verdict, forcing a mistrial.
detail: Triggers a mistrial under Federal Rule of Criminal Procedure 31. Prosecution can retry once or sometimes multiple times — retrying isn't double jeopardy when the first trial ended without a verdict. Allen charges (judge urging continued deliberation) try to break impasses but are constrained by coercion concerns. Civil cases: most jurisdictions require unanimity though some allow non-unanimous civil verdicts.

snappy: A contract to do something illegal is generally unenforceable — the law won't help either party.
detail: Different from contracts merely violating statutes (which may be enforceable depending on the statute's purpose) and from contracts violating public policy. Examples: gambling debts in states without legal gambling, contracts to commit crimes, agreements for usurious interest. In pari delicto: where both parties at fault, courts often leave parties as they found them. Restitution sometimes available when one party is significantly less culpable.

snappy: The primary US immigration statute — codified at 8 U.S.C. §§ 1101 et seq.
detail: Original 1952; major amendments 1965 (eliminated national-origin quotas), 1986 (IRCA — employer sanctions plus amnesty), 1990 (visa categories), 1996 (IIRIRA — restricted relief). Created framework still in use: visa categories, admission grounds, deportation grounds, naturalisation. Administered by DHS components (USCIS, ICE, CBP), DOJ EOIR, State Department, and DOL. Regular legislative-reform proposals rarely pass; executive-action programs (DACA, DAPA, parole policies) fill gaps.

snappy: DOJ trial-level court hearing removal cases — staffed by Immigration Judges.
detail: 63 immigration courts across the US. Cases initiated by DHS Notice to Appear. Immigration Judges decide: charges of removability, eligibility for relief (asylum, cancellation, adjustment), discretionary grants. No juries, no right to government-provided counsel for respondent. Children, including unaccompanied minors, must appear in immigration court. Backlog crisis: case wait times 3-7 years average. Detained docket move faster. Decisions appealable to Board of Immigration Appeals.

snappy: Congressional process to remove federal officers for treason, bribery, or 'high crimes and misdemeanors'.
detail: House impeaches by majority; Senate convicts by two-thirds. Four presidential impeachments: Andrew Johnson (1868), Bill Clinton (1998), Donald Trump (twice, 2019 and 2021). None convicted in the Senate. Federal judges have been removed by impeachment 8 times in history. Impeachment also reaches cabinet officers and other 'civil officers'. Walter Nixon v. United States (1993) held impeachment trial procedures are nonjusticiable political questions.

full: Implied warranty of fitness for particular purpose
snappy: When buyer relies on seller's expertise to recommend goods for buyer's specific use — seller implicitly warrants the goods will work.
detail: UCC § 2-315. Requires seller's reason to know of buyer's particular purpose and reliance on seller's skill or judgment. Disclaimer requires conspicuous writing. Distinguishable from merchantability warranty (ordinary use). Classic example: contractor recommends specific paint for marine environment; if the paint fails in saltwater, breach. Often disclaimed in B2B sales; nearly impossible to disclaim against consumers under state law.

snappy: Landlord must provide rental housing that's fit to live in — heat, water, electricity, no health hazards.
detail: Common-law innovation (Javins v. First National Realty, D.C. Cir. 1970) overruling caveat emptor. Now codified in most state landlord-tenant statutes. Covers essential services (plumbing, heating, electricity), structural integrity, and freedom from infestation. Tenant remedies: repair-and-deduct, rent escrow, withholding rent, lease termination, damages. Cannot be waived in residential leases in nearly all states.

snappy: Discharge of a contract because performance has become physically or legally impossible.
detail: Traditional examples: subject matter destroyed (Taylor v. Caldwell, 1863 — music hall burned down); death of the personal-services obligor; subsequent illegality. Courts apply impossibility strictly — mere difficulty or expense isn't enough. The modern, more flexible doctrine is 'commercial impracticability' (UCC § 2-615), which extends the relief to unforeseen events that make performance unreasonably burdensome.

snappy: Treating a parent as earning what they could be earning — even when actual income is lower, to set realistic support.
detail: Used when a parent is voluntarily underemployed or unemployed without good reason. Court considers: education, work history, vocational potential, local job market. Common applications: parent quits high-paying job after order entered, parent under-reports cash business income, parent claims inability to find work despite history. Bad-faith intent often required but not always. Heavy litigation issue — distinguishes legitimate career choices from support-evasion.

snappy: Reasons a non-citizen can be denied admission or visa — health, criminal history, security, public charge, fraud, prior immigration violations.
detail: INA § 212(a). Major categories: health-related (communicable diseases), criminal (any conviction for crimes involving moral turpitude, controlled-substance offenses, multiple convictions, prostitution), security (terrorism, espionage, totalitarian-party membership), public charge (likely to become primarily dependent on government), fraud (false statements in visa applications), prior immigration violations (unlawful presence, prior deportation). Waivers available in narrow circumstances — discretionary.

snappy: Out-of-pocket costs reasonably incurred by the non-breaching party — handling rejected goods, finding substitutes.
detail: UCC § 2-715(1) for buyer, § 2-710 for seller. Costs of inspecting rejected goods, transportation, storage, custody, commissions for cover purchases. Distinguishable from consequential damages (downstream losses). Generally recoverable absent express disclaimer; usually small relative to other categories. Often grouped with direct damages even where consequential are excluded.

snappy: Child-support formula based on both parents' combined income, with each contributing proportionally.
detail: Used in most US states. Logic: child support should approximate what would have been spent on the child in an intact household. Combine both parents' gross income, apply guideline percentage for number of children, allocate by income ratio. Adjustments for childcare costs, health insurance, extraordinary expenses. Time-sharing adjustments for shared physical custody. Most consistent with public policy of treating children as both parents' responsibility.

snappy: Most Bill of Rights protections apply to state governments through the Fourteenth Amendment.
detail: Largely a Twentieth-century development. Initially through 'fundamental fairness' (Palko v. Connecticut, 1937), later through 'fundamental to the American scheme of justice' (Duncan v. Louisiana, 1968). Not all rights incorporated — Seventh Amendment civil-jury, Fifth Amendment grand-jury, and Third Amendment haven't been. McDonald v. Chicago (2010) incorporated the Second Amendment; Timbs v. Indiana (2019) incorporated the Excessive Fines Clause.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: One party agrees to cover the other's losses from defined risks.
detail: Common in commercial contracts: vendor indemnifies customer for IP-infringement claims arising from the vendor's product. Indemnity clauses fight over scope (which claims), procedure (control of defence, consent to settle), and caps (liability ceilings). Insurance is the financial backing; many indemnity obligations are uninsurable if not crafted carefully. Mutual indemnity is common between commercial peers.

snappy: A federal agency whose leaders can only be removed for cause — insulated from direct presidential control.
detail: Examples: SEC, FTC, FCC, NLRB, Federal Reserve, FDIC. Originally upheld in Humphrey's Executor v. United States (1935). Seila Law v. CFPB (2020) and Collins v. Yellen (2021) restricted single-director independent agencies. Free Enterprise Fund v. PCAOB (2010) limited double-layered for-cause protection. Continuing tension between unitary-executive theory and Congress's institutional independence preferences.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: A worker classified as a business owner rather than employee — different tax, benefits, and protection regime.
detail: Test varies by purpose: IRS uses 'common law' test (behavioral, financial, type of relationship factors); DOL uses 'economic reality' test (2024 final rule, post-2021 rule revision); states use ABC test (California's AB5, Massachusetts) or hybrid. Misclassification consequences: back wages, overtime, employer payroll taxes, benefit-plan liability, workers' compensation, unemployment-insurance contributions, plus penalties. Major battleground in gig-economy contexts.

snappy: A board member without significant ties to management or the company — relied on for unconflicted judgment.
detail: Stock exchange listing rules (NYSE, Nasdaq) require majority of independent directors and independent audit, compensation, and nominating committees for listed companies. Sarbanes-Oxley reinforced independence requirements. Definition varies but generally excludes recent employees, family of management, significant business relationships. Delaware case law gives weight to bright-line tests plus context-specific 'material relationship' analysis.

snappy: Formal felony charge issued by a grand jury after reviewing prosecution evidence.
detail: Required in federal felony cases by the Fifth Amendment; some states use indictment, others use prosecutor's 'information'. Grand juries operate in secret, hear only the prosecution's side, and indict on probable cause — a low standard, hence Sol Wachtler's famous quip that a grand jury would 'indict a ham sandwich'. The federal grand jury also issues subpoenas, making it a powerful investigative tool.

snappy: A constitutional claim that your lawyer's performance was so deficient it violated your right to counsel.
detail: Two-prong test from Strickland v. Washington (1984): (1) deficient performance and (2) prejudice (reasonable probability of different outcome). The standard is highly deferential to lawyers — strategic decisions even if questionable rarely qualify. Common claims: failure to investigate, failure to call witnesses, failure to challenge evidence, sleeping or intoxicated counsel. Successful claims often involve capital cases or post-conviction DNA exoneration.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Doctrine letting employers prevent a former employee from working for a competitor — even without a non-compete.
detail: PepsiCo v. Redmond (7th Cir. 1995) endorsed the theory: the employee's new role would 'inevitably' lead to disclosure of trade secrets. Adoption is split: some states embrace it, California rejects it (Schlage Lock v. Whyte, 2002), New York applies narrowly. Effectively a judicially imposed non-compete. Some employee-mobility advocates push for state-law abolition. Even where recognised, courts require strong evidence of overlap and bad faith.

snappy: The standard process for federal agency rulemaking — propose, take comments, finalise.
detail: APA § 553. Procedure: agency publishes proposed rule in Federal Register, accepts public comments (typically 30-60 days), publishes final rule with response to significant comments. Effective workhorse of modern regulation. Hard-look judicial review under Motor Vehicle Manufacturers v. State Farm (1983) requires agencies to engage substantively with comments. Distinguished from formal rulemaking and adjudication; covers most environmental, financial, and consumer-protection regulations.

snappy: A prosecutor's charging document filed directly with the court — an alternative to grand-jury indictment.
detail: Used in states without grand-jury requirements and in federal misdemeanor cases. Followed by preliminary hearing where a judge (not jurors) decides if probable cause exists. Many states use informations for the vast majority of felony prosecutions — California and Connecticut among others. Filing an information typically requires defendant's waiver of grand-jury rights for federal felonies. Faster and more transparent than grand-jury proceedings.

snappy: Unauthorised use of someone else's IP — making, selling, or copying without permission.
detail: Patent infringement is judged element-by-element against the claims. Trademark infringement asks whether consumers are likely to be confused about source. Copyright infringement requires copying (often inferred from access plus substantial similarity). Remedies: injunctions, monetary damages, sometimes treble damages for willful conduct. Defences include licensure, fair use (copyright), and non-infringement on the merits.

snappy: Trading securities based on material, non-public information you got through a position of trust.
detail: Built on SEC Rule 10b-5 plus the misappropriation theory (United States v. O'Hagan, 1997). 'Tippees' (recipients of tips) also liable if the tipper breached duty for personal benefit. Famous prosecutions: Martha Stewart (2004), Raj Rajaratnam (2011), congressional pre-COVID-19 stock sales (largely unprosecuted). SEC's Office of Whistleblower has paid hundreds of millions for insider-trading tips. The STOCK Act (2012) explicitly bars congressional insider trading.

snappy: Federal statute creating Inspectors General — independent watchdogs in major federal agencies.
detail: 5 U.S.C. §§ 401–424 (1978). Dual reporting to agency head and Congress. Investigate fraud, waste, abuse, and misconduct. Issue audit reports and management recommendations. Generally protected from removal without congressional notification. The Watergate-era response to perceived executive-branch corruption. CIGIE (Council of the Inspectors General on Integrity and Efficiency) coordinates IG community. High-profile IG-led investigations regularly drive policy and personnel actions.

snappy: A contract calling for deliveries (or payments) in separate lots — each instalment treated separately for breach analysis.
detail: UCC § 2-612 governs. Non-conformity in one instalment doesn't automatically allow cancellation of the whole contract unless it substantially impairs the value of the entire contract. Allows cure of instalment defects. Common in long-term supply agreements and commercial real-estate sales with phased closings. Distinguishable from divisible contracts and contracts requiring single performance.

snappy: Statement in a contract that it represents the entire agreement between the parties.
detail: Also called a 'merger clause' or 'entire agreement clause'. Strengthens parol evidence-rule protection — outside agreements, oral promises, and prior drafts can't be used to vary the terms. Courts give significant weight but not automatic deference; a contract still has to be free of fraud, ambiguity, or mistake. Standard in nearly every commercial agreement.

snappy: Extreme and outrageous conduct that causes severe emotional distress — the bar is intentionally high.
detail: Restatement (Second) of Torts § 46. Requires 'extreme and outrageous' conduct beyond all bounds of decency, intent or recklessness, causation, and severe distress. Insults, threats, and indignities usually fall short. Successful claims often involve abuse of power (employer harassment campaigns), bereavement targeting (Westboro Baptist Church, before Snyder v. Phelps, 2011), or sustained stalking. Bystander recovery permitted in narrow circumstances.

snappy: A USPTO administrative trial to challenge an issued patent's validity — created by the America Invents Act (2011).
detail: Conducted by the Patent Trial and Appeal Board. Grounds limited to patents and printed publications (§§ 102, 103). Faster and cheaper than district court (typically 12-18 months vs 2-4 years), broader claim construction. Heavily used by defendants in parallel litigation. Cuozzo Speed Technologies v. Lee (2016) upheld the broadest-reasonable-interpretation standard; SAS Institute (2018) required all-claim review. Stronger evidence standard than initial examination.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Mid-tier review: government must show 'substantial relationship to important interest' — used for sex classifications and content-neutral speech regulations.
detail: Craig v. Boren (1976) established for sex classifications. United States v. Virginia (1996) required 'exceedingly persuasive justification' — strict-scrutiny-adjacent. Modern intermediate-scrutiny battlegrounds: gender-based military registration, abortion-clinic buffer zones, commercial speech (Central Hudson, 1980). Gives courts moderate latitude to evaluate government justifications without the near-fatality of strict scrutiny.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: The federal tax statute — over 4,000 pages and growing.
detail: 26 U.S.C. Codifies federal income, estate, gift, payroll, and excise taxes. Frequently amended — major rewrites in 1939, 1954, 1986, 2017 (Tax Cuts and Jobs Act). IRS implements via Treasury Regulations (highly authoritative) and Revenue Rulings (less authoritative). Tax Court, district courts, Court of Federal Claims, and Court of Appeals all hear tax disputes. Tax-policy debates focus on rates, base broadening, equity, complexity, and international competitiveness.

snappy: Adopting a child from another country — heavily regulated by both US and source-country laws.
detail: Hague Adoption Convention governs adoptions between member countries. US Intercountry Adoption Act implements. Process: USCIS approval, immigrant visa for child (IH-3/IH-4 or IR-3/IR-4), country-specific procedures. Numbers have declined dramatically from 2004 peak (~22,884) to recent ~1,500-2,500 annually — driven by source-country restrictions, China's policy changes, Russia's ban. Adoption-tax-credit available for qualified expenses.

snappy: A stakeholder (e.g., insurance company, bank) sues multiple claimants to a single fund and asks the court to decide who gets it.
detail: Federal Rule 22 and the Federal Interpleader Act (28 U.S.C. § 1335). Lets the stakeholder deposit the disputed money or property and step out of the dispute. Common with insurance proceeds, escrow funds, and contested estates. Statutory interpleader has expansive jurisdiction — minimal diversity and nationwide service of process — ideal when claimants are scattered across states.

snappy: Written questions one side sends the other in a lawsuit — must answer under oath.
detail: Federal Rule 33 caps these at 25 per side without leave of court. Cheaper than depositions but less probing; lawyers usually craft answers carefully to minimise admissions. Common subjects: identification of witnesses, factual contentions, computation of damages, and which documents support each claim. Objections (overbroad, vague, privileged) often outnumber actual answers in big commercial cases.

snappy: An outsider inserts themselves into a pending lawsuit because they have a stake in the outcome.
detail: Federal Rule 24. 'As of right' when the intervenor has a protectible interest the existing parties won't adequately represent. 'Permissive' when there's a common question of law or fact. Common in environmental cases (NGOs intervening on agency suits), civil-rights challenges (states intervening to defend statutes), and bankruptcy. Government's right to intervene whenever a federal statute's constitutionality is challenged is codified by 28 U.S.C. § 2403.

snappy: Intentionally intruding on someone's private affairs in a way that would be highly offensive to a reasonable person.
detail: Restatement § 652B. No publication required — the intrusion itself is the tort. Classic examples: eavesdropping, peeping, unauthorised wiretapping, covert recording, GPS tracking of spouses. Increasingly applied to data breaches, computer hacking, and unauthorised surveillance. Coverage stops at the public sphere — photographing someone in a public place generally doesn't qualify.

snappy: Four distinct torts protecting different aspects of privacy — intrusion, disclosure, false light, appropriation.
detail: Cataloged by William Prosser in his influential 1960 California Law Review article. Restatement (Second) of Torts §§ 652A–E adopted the framework. Not all states recognise all four; some don't recognise the tort at all. Distinct from constitutional privacy (Griswold, Roe) and state-statutory privacy (consumer data laws). Modern data-breach litigation increasingly invokes these torts as alternatives to limited statutory remedies.

snappy: When police lawfully impound property, they can catalog its contents without a warrant.
detail: South Dakota v. Opperman (1976). Justifications: protect owner's property, protect officers from liability claims, protect officers from danger. Must follow standardised procedures — discretion-driven searches are pretexts. Common application: arrested driver's car towed and inventoried; contraband found is admissible. Florida v. Wells (1990) emphasised that procedures must be 'sufficiently regulated' to avoid being a pretext for investigation.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: When the government takes or damages your property without formal eminent domain proceedings — you sue to get paid.
detail: The flip side of eminent domain: instead of government bringing the action, the property owner does. Trigger events: physical occupation, regulatory taking, denial of all economic use. Loretto v. Teleprompter (1982) recognised even minor permanent physical invasion as a taking. Penn Central (1978) and Lucas v. South Carolina Coastal Council (1992) frame regulatory-takings analysis. Just-compensation remedy required by the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Federal statute regulating investment advisers — including hedge funds, private equity, and traditional wealth managers.
detail: 15 U.S.C. §§ 80b. Registration with SEC required for advisers managing more than $110M (state registration for smaller). Imposes fiduciary duties, recordkeeping, custody rules, advertising restrictions. Reg Best Interest expanded for broker-dealer advice in 2019. The Dodd-Frank Act (2010) brought hedge fund advisers under the regime. Foreign advisers face elaborate registration provisions; private-funds advisers comply with detailed disclosure.

snappy: First sale of a company's stock to the general public — registered with the SEC.
detail: Process: hire underwriters, draft S-1 registration statement, SEC review, road show, pricing, allocation, trading begins. Underwriting fees typically 6-7% of proceeds. Lock-up periods (commonly 180 days) restrict insiders from selling. Direct listings (Slack, Spotify, Coinbase) bypass underwriters. SPACs offered alternative through 2020-2022; activity has declined. JOBS Act (2012) created 'emerging growth company' on-ramp with reduced disclosure.

snappy: Personal retirement account with tax advantages — separate from employer-sponsored plans.
detail: Two main types: Traditional (deductible contributions, taxable withdrawals) and Roth (after-tax contributions, tax-free qualified withdrawals). 2024 contribution limit: $7,000 ($8,000 if 50+). Income limits for Roth contributions ($161K single phase-out, $240K joint). 'Backdoor Roth' strategy for high-income taxpayers. SEP-IRA and SIMPLE-IRA for small business/self-employed. Early withdrawal 10% penalty before 59½ (limited exceptions).

snappy: Specific deductions taxpayers can claim instead of the standard deduction — listed on Schedule A.
detail: Major categories: state and local taxes (SALT, capped at $10,000 post-TCJA), home mortgage interest ($750K loan-amount limit), charitable contributions, medical expenses (over 7.5% of AGI), gambling losses (limited). Post-TCJA, itemising only beneficial if total exceeds standard deduction — far less common now. 'Bunching' strategy: time charitable contributions and other discretionary deductions to alternate years.

snappy: Bringing additional claims or parties into a lawsuit.
detail: Federal Rules 18 (claims), 19 (compulsory party joinder), 20 (permissive party joinder), 22 (interpleader). Rule 19 forces inclusion of a party 'necessary' to complete relief or to protect their interest. Failure to join an 'indispensable' party can require dismissal. Rule 20 lets plaintiffs sue multiple defendants together when claims share a question and arise from the same transaction — a frequent feature in employment-discrimination cases.

snappy: Multiple defendants are each liable for the entire harm — plaintiff can collect the full amount from any one.
detail: Lets plaintiff recover even if some defendants are insolvent. Traditionally applied to all multiple-defendant negligence; tort reform has dramatically narrowed it in many states. 'Several only' rules limit each defendant to their percentage of fault. 'Hybrid' rules in many states impose joint liability above a fault threshold (often 50% or more). Driver of many tort-reform debates around 'deep pocket' defendants.

snappy: Both parents share custody — usually both legal decision-making (joint legal) and physical care (joint physical or shared parenting).
detail: Joint legal custody now default in many states absent contraindications. Joint physical custody requires substantially equal time-sharing — typically 35-50% with each parent. Schedule logistics: alternating weeks, 2-2-3 split, 2-2-5-5, biweekly. Increasingly common, especially after equal-parenting-time legislation in Kentucky, Arizona, and several other states. Requires parental cooperation; high-conflict cases often unsuitable.

snappy: Two or more entities jointly responsible for the same worker — both liable for employment obligations.
detail: Common scenarios: franchisor-franchisee, staffing-agency-client, parent-subsidiary. NLRB 'joint employer' rule has flipped repeatedly: Browning-Ferris (2015) liberal; Trump Board (2020) restrictive; Biden Board (2023) liberal again — but 2023 rule struck by court in 2024. DOL has parallel test for FLSA. Implications: union organising, collective bargaining duties, wage-hour liability, anti-discrimination liability. Major McDonald's settlement (2023) over joint-employer claims highlights stakes.

snappy: Co-ownership where if one owner dies, the others inherit automatically — no probate.
detail: Requires the 'four unities': time, title, interest, possession (all owners get the same interest at the same time via the same deed, sharing the whole). Right of survivorship is the key feature, used by spouses and family co-owners to avoid probate. Compare 'tenancy in common', the default for unrelated co-owners — each has a transferable share that passes by will or intestate succession. Many states have an intermediate 'tenancy by the entirety' for married couples.

snappy: A business arrangement where two or more parties combine resources for a specific project or purpose.
detail: Can take various legal forms: contract-only, LLC, LP, or corporation. Joint ventures usually involve shared management, shared profits, and a defined scope or duration. Common in international expansion (foreign-partner JVs), large infrastructure projects, and R&D collaborations. Antitrust scrutiny when competitors form JVs; HSR filing often required. Differs from partnership in being typically project-bound rather than ongoing-enterprise-bound.

snappy: Contract setting up a shared business between two or more companies for a limited purpose.
detail: Distinct from a merger — each party keeps its separate existence and contributes specific assets, capital, or expertise. Governs ownership splits, capital contributions, governance, profit allocation, and exit mechanics. International JVs are heavily used in jurisdictions requiring local partners (China historically, Saudi Arabia, India). Common in pharma R&D, oil-and-gas exploration, infrastructure projects, and large defence contracts.

snappy: Post-trial ruling overturning the jury's verdict because no reasonable jury could have reached it.
detail: Federal Rule 50(b) calls this 'renewed motion for judgment as a matter of law' — the old 'JNOV' label survives in practice. Must be preceded by a Rule 50(a) motion at trial's close. Standard is highly deferential: court takes evidence in the light most favourable to the verdict winner. Granted only when the evidence so overwhelmingly favours the moving party that no rational jury could decide otherwise. Often paired with a Rule 59 motion for new trial as alternative relief.

snappy: Court power to strike down laws and executive actions that violate the Constitution.
detail: Established in Marbury v. Madison (1803), Chief Justice Marshall: 'It is emphatically the province and duty of the judicial department to say what the law is.' Not explicit in the Constitution. The Supreme Court is final on federal law. State courts can also review state laws under state constitutions, often providing broader protections than federal counterparts. Major exercises: Brown v. Board (1954), Citizens United (2010), Dobbs (2022).
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: A lien (mortgage, judgment, tax) that comes after an earlier-recorded senior lien in priority.
detail: Foreclosure of a senior lien extinguishes junior liens — junior lienholders get paid only from any sale surplus. Junior-lien holders therefore often pay off seniors to preserve their security. Sophisticated lenders demand subordination agreements when permitted by primary loan documents. Common scenario: home equity loan junior to a first mortgage. Tax liens can leapfrog earlier private liens under federal and state priority rules.

snappy: Whether a court has the legal power to hear a particular case.
detail: Two questions: subject-matter jurisdiction (does this court handle this type of case — federal vs state, civil vs criminal) and personal jurisdiction (does the court have authority over this specific defendant). Personal jurisdiction over out-of-state defendants is the modern battleground, especially for online conduct and product liability. International Shoe v. Washington (1945) is the founding case.

snappy: The judge's directive to the jury on the law to apply when deciding the case.
detail: Read at trial's end, often in writing too. Each side submits proposed instructions; the judge selects, modifies, or rewrites. Pattern instructions (e.g., Ninth Circuit Model Civil Jury Instructions) provide starting points. Erroneous instructions are a leading ground for reversal on appeal — misstating the burden of proof, omitting an element, or misdefining a key term draws the harshest scrutiny.

snappy: 2005 Supreme Court case allowing eminent domain for private economic development.
detail: 5–4 decision permitting New London, Connecticut, to seize private homes (including Susette Kelo's iconic pink house) to make way for a Pfizer-anchored redevelopment plan. The Court read 'public use' to include 'public purpose' — and economic development counted. Massive popular backlash followed; over 40 states passed laws restricting takings for private benefit. The redevelopment itself never happened — the site sat empty for years.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: A secret payment in exchange for steering business — usually illegal.
detail: Federal Anti-Kickback Statute makes paying or receiving anything of value for referrals in federally funded healthcare (Medicare, Medicaid) a felony — up to $100,000 per violation plus exclusion. Banking, defence procurement, and construction industries have their own anti-kickback regimes. Civil False Claims Act allows whistleblowers ('qui tam') to share in recoveries — driving billions in pharma settlements over the past decade.

snappy: Unlawfully seizing and moving a person against their will — usually with ransom, sexual assault, or another crime in mind.
detail: Federal Kidnapping Act (the 'Lindbergh Law', 18 U.S.C. § 1201) covers victims transported across state lines, with presumption after 24 hours. State laws vary on movement requirements: some require substantial transportation, others just confinement. Aggravated kidnapping (weapon use, child victim, sexual purpose) carries life sentences. Parental kidnapping has separate federal statutes governing custody-violation cases.

snappy: Fourth Amendment requirement that police identify themselves before forcing entry.
detail: Officers serving a warrant must knock, announce ('Police! Search warrant!'), and wait a reasonable time before breaking in. Exceptions: risk to officers, destruction of evidence, futility. Hudson v. Michigan (2006) controversially held that violations don't trigger the exclusionary rule — evidence still admissible. State courts can provide stricter protection under state constitutions. Botched no-knock raids (Breonna Taylor, 2020) drove reform efforts in several jurisdictions.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Intracompany transferee visa — for executives, managers, or specialised-knowledge workers transferring from foreign affiliate.
detail: Two subcategories: L-1A (executives and managers, 7-year maximum) and L-1B (specialised knowledge, 5-year). Requirements: continuous employment with multinational employer for at least 1 of last 3 years abroad. No annual cap, no lottery — popular alternative when H-1B unavailable. 'New office' L-1 for setting up US operation (1-year initial, renewals based on performance). Family L-2 visa with automatic work authorisation for spouses.

snappy: The owner of property leased to a tenant.
detail: Owes duties of habitability (implied in most states for residential rentals — heat, water, structural safety), quiet enjoyment, and disclosure of known hazards (lead paint, radon, prior deaths in some states). Cannot retaliate against tenants who report code violations. Eviction must follow statutory process. Commercial landlords get more contractual freedom than residential ones, where consumer-protection rules dominate.

snappy: The federal trademark statute — also covers false advertising and unfair competition.
detail: 15 U.S.C. §§ 1051–1141. Enacted 1946. Provides registration mechanism, infringement causes of action (§ 1114), and false-designation-of-origin claims (§ 1125(a), '§ 43(a)' in practice). Section 43(a) reaches false advertising even without a registered mark — staple of competitor lawsuits. Section 43(c) protects famous marks against dilution. Major Supreme Court cases include Two Pesos v. Taco Cabana (trade dress, 1992) and Matal v. Tam (registration of disparaging marks, 2017).

snappy: Common-law theft: taking and carrying away someone else's property with intent to keep it permanently.
detail: Modern statutes have largely consolidated larceny, embezzlement, and false pretenses into a unified 'theft' offence (Model Penal Code § 223). Distinctions still matter where statutes retain them. 'Grand larceny' vs 'petit larceny' turn on the property's value — typically $1,000-$2,500 is the dividing line, varying by state. Asportation requirement: even slight movement of the property can suffice.

snappy: Even a contributorily negligent plaintiff can recover if the defendant had the final chance to avoid the harm.
detail: Doctrine mitigates the harsh all-or-nothing rule in contributory-negligence states. Davies v. Mann (1842) — donkey hobbled in road, defendant could have stopped — established the principle. Largely subsumed into comparative-fault apportionment in modified-comparative states. Survives mostly in pure contributory-negligence jurisdictions as the main escape hatch.

snappy: A contract granting a tenant possession of property for a specified time and rent.
detail: Residential leases face heavy regulation: implied warranty of habitability, security-deposit caps, anti-discrimination rules (federal Fair Housing Act, state protected classes). Commercial leases are far more negotiable — gross vs net rent, common-area maintenance, exclusivity clauses, percentage rent for retail. Length matters: most jurisdictions require leases over a year to be in writing under the statute of frauds.

snappy: The right to make major decisions about a child's upbringing — education, healthcare, religion.
detail: Distinguishable from physical custody (where child lives). Joint legal custody now standard in most states — requires consultation on major decisions; daily decisions made by parent with current physical custody. Disputes when parents can't agree typically resolved by tie-breaker provision in custody order or by going back to court. Increasingly common: detailed parenting plans specifying decision categories and procedures.

snappy: Three-part test from Lemon v. Kurtzman (1971): government action must (1) have secular purpose, (2) not advance/inhibit religion, (3) not foster entanglement.
detail: Dominant Establishment Clause framework for half a century. Effectively overruled by Kennedy v. Bremerton School District (2022) in favour of 'history and tradition' analysis. Survives nominally — lower courts still cite — but the Court has stopped applying. The shift has substantially eased restrictions on government accommodation of religion and religious expression by public employees.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Preliminary document outlining a contemplated deal — usually non-binding except on specified terms.
detail: Common in M&A, real-estate, and major commercial negotiations. Lays out the basic deal (price, structure, key conditions) before the parties spend money on diligence and definitive documents. Most LOIs are non-binding overall but contain binding provisions for exclusivity (no-shop), confidentiality, expense allocation, and break-up fees. Bad drafting can create unintended binding obligations — Texaco v. Pennzoil (1987) being the cautionary example.

snappy: Written or printed defamation — distinct from spoken (slander).
detail: Includes social media posts, emails, blog comments, photographs, and broadcast media (which counts as libel even though spoken because it reaches a fixed audience). Libel per se categories — accusations of crime, professional incompetence, loathsome disease, sexual misconduct — let plaintiffs collect without proving specific economic loss. Online libel raises tricky jurisdictional questions: where you can sue depends on where the statement was 'aimed'.

snappy: Permission to use IP that the owner still controls.
detail: Software, music, patents, trademarks all licensed daily. Exclusive licenses transfer the practical right to exclude (the licensee can sue infringers). Non-exclusive licenses allow multiple licensees. Terms cover scope (territory, field of use, duration), royalties, sublicensing rights, and termination. Open-source licenses (MIT, GPL, Apache) are still licenses — the copyright holder is just generous about the terms.

snappy: A contract granting permission to use IP, software, or property — without transferring ownership.
detail: Distinct from sale (which transfers title). Covers software licences (EULA), patent licences, trademark licences, real-property licences. Heavily contested whether software licences are 'sales' for first-sale doctrine purposes — Vernor v. Autodesk (9th Cir. 2010) treated as licence. Major commercial terms: scope of use, exclusivity, royalty, term, audit rights, change-of-control provisions, termination triggers.

snappy: A legal claim against property securing a debt.
detail: Mortgages are voluntary liens. Mechanic's liens (unpaid contractors), tax liens (unpaid taxes), and judgment liens (court judgments) are involuntary. A lien must be discharged before clean title can transfer — the proceeds of sale typically pay off recorded liens at closing. Priority follows recording order in most states (first to record, first paid), with tax liens and some statutory liens taking priority regardless.

snappy: Ownership of property only for the duration of a person's life — passes to a 'remainder' after death.
detail: Common in estate planning: 'To my wife for life, then to my children'. The life tenant can use the property and collect rents but can't waste it (commit harm to remainder interest). Often used to give a surviving spouse use of the home while preserving inheritance for children of a prior marriage. Life estates 'pur autre vie' measure life by someone other than the holder. Largely replaced by trusts in sophisticated planning.

snappy: The test for trademark infringement: would the average consumer be confused about the source of the goods?
detail: Circuit-specific multi-factor tests (Polaroid, Sleekcraft, DuPont) consider: similarity of marks, similarity of goods, channels of trade, consumer sophistication, defendant's intent, actual confusion evidence, and strength of plaintiff's mark. Survey evidence is common but contested. Internet-era complications (initial-interest confusion, keyword advertising) added new factors. The trademark equivalent of patent-infringement claim construction in its centrality.

snappy: A partnership where partners aren't personally liable for other partners' malpractice or negligence.
detail: Originated in Texas (1991) after savings-and-loan crisis exposed law firm partners to massive joint liability. Now available in all states. Common form for law firms, accounting firms, and other professional services. Partners remain liable for their own malpractice. Some states' LLPs offer full shield (corporate-like protection); others limit shield to professional malpractice. Pass-through taxation. Registration with state required, unlike general partnerships.

snappy: A partnership with at least one general partner (unlimited liability) and one or more limited partners (liability limited to investment).
detail: Uniform Limited Partnership Act / Revised version. Limited partners can't participate in management without losing liability protection. Common in private equity, hedge funds, venture capital (LPs invest, GP manages). Family limited partnerships are common estate-planning vehicles. Real-estate syndications historically used LP structure; increasingly displaced by LLCs for operational businesses. Pass-through taxation.

snappy: Someone who has voluntarily entered a specific public controversy — must prove actual malice but only for that topic.
detail: Gertz v. Welch (1974). Test: voluntary thrust into a particular public controversy. Defamation claim about an unrelated topic gets ordinary negligence standard. Examples: scientist publicly commenting on a controversial study, business leader in a public dispute. Modern social media complicates analysis: does posting controversial views thrust ordinary people into public-figure status? Courts split.

snappy: A pre-set dollar amount the breaching party must pay — written into the contract.
detail: Enforceable if (a) actual damages would be hard to quantify at signing, and (b) the amount is a reasonable forecast of likely harm. Courts strike them as 'penalty clauses' if they look punitive — e.g., a $1,000-per-day fee bearing no relation to actual delay costs. Common in construction (delay damages), software (SLA credits), and real-estate purchase agreements (forfeiture of earnest money).

snappy: The contract between a property seller and a real-estate broker authorising the broker to market the property.
detail: Three main types: exclusive right to sell (broker gets commission no matter who finds buyer), exclusive agency (broker gets commission unless seller finds buyer alone), and open listing (commission only if that specific broker brings the buyer). Typically 3-6 months. Includes asking price, broker-services description, commission rate, and listing-period dates. Cancellation usually permitted with notice; commission protection sometimes survives termination for short period.
