snappy: Theory that the Constitution's meaning evolves with changing social understanding.
detail: Associated with progressive constitutional thought; sometimes called 'common law constitutionalism' (David Strauss). Justice William Brennan articulated influential versions in the 1980s. Rooted in evolving 'standards of decency' (Eighth Amendment) and dynamic Equal Protection (Brown v. Board, 1954). In retreat at current Supreme Court; persists more strongly in legal academia and lower courts. Originalists call living constitutionalism unconstrained judicial discretion.

snappy: A trust created during the grantor's lifetime — typically used to avoid probate.
detail: Grantor usually serves as initial trustee, retaining full control during life. Assets retitled into the trust avoid probate at death. Becomes irrevocable at grantor's death; successor trustee distributes per trust terms. Doesn't reduce estate tax (assets still in taxable estate). Privacy advantage: trust terms not public (unlike wills probated). Most beneficial in probate-heavy states (California, Florida) or for cross-state property. Pour-over will catches assets not retitled.

snappy: A document specifying medical treatment preferences if you become unable to communicate.
detail: Distinct from a regular 'will' (which addresses post-death matters). Common provisions: life-sustaining treatment preferences (mechanical ventilation, feeding tube, dialysis), pain management, organ donation, designated decision-maker (healthcare power of attorney). Five Wishes form widely used. POLST (Physician Orders for Life-Sustaining Treatment) provides medical-order version. Schiavo case (2005) drove broad adoption — most US adults still don't have one. Hospital advance-directive policies routinely solicit at admission.

snappy: A hybrid entity giving limited liability like a corporation plus tax flexibility of a partnership.
detail: Created by filing articles of organisation with a state. Members own the LLC; managers run it (member-managed or manager-managed). Default tax treatment: pass-through (partnership for multi-member, sole-proprietor for single-member). Can elect corporate taxation. Maximum flexibility in operating agreement. Most popular form for small businesses; commonly used for real-estate holdings, investment vehicles, and joint ventures. Delaware LLC Act influential model.

snappy: Employer locking out workers — refusing to allow them to work to pressure them in bargaining.
detail: Mirror image of a strike. Lawful as economic weapon in good-faith bargaining (American Ship Building v. NLRB, 1965). Often used during contract expirations when employer wants to apply pressure. Locked-out workers typically ineligible for unemployment benefits (varies by state). NHL (2004-05, 2012-13), NBA (2011), and various corporate disputes have used lockouts. Replacement workers permissible in defensive lockouts under specific conditions.

snappy: A state law authorising its courts to reach out and assert jurisdiction over out-of-state defendants.
detail: Specifies the kinds of contacts that subject a defendant to suit: doing business in the state, causing injury there, owning property, contracting with a resident, etc. Some long-arms extend to the constitutional maximum (California); others enumerate specific bases (New York's CPLR § 302). Federal courts in diversity follow the long-arm of the state they sit in, subject to constitutional due-process limits.

snappy: Profits from selling appreciated assets held for over a year — taxed at favourable rates.
detail: 2024 brackets: 0% (single income up to $47,025), 15% (up to $518,900), 20% (above). Plus 3.8% Net Investment Income Tax for high earners. Holding period starts the day after acquisition. Wash-sale rule (30-day repurchase) doesn't apply to LTCGs but affects loss harvesting. Step-up in basis at death typically eliminates LTCG for inherited assets — a major estate-planning consideration.

snappy: 2024 Supreme Court decision overruling Chevron deference — courts now interpret statutes without binding deference to agencies.
detail: 6-3 decision. Held the APA's command that courts 'decide all relevant questions of law' is inconsistent with Chevron's binding deference. Courts may still respect agency expertise (Skidmore) but not be bound by reasonable agency interpretations. Companion to Relentless v. Department of Commerce. Practical effect: shift of regulatory power from agencies to federal courts. Reshapes administrative law dramatically — pending and future challenges to thousands of regulations face new judicial environment.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: A spouse's claim for loss of companionship, affection, sexual relations, and household services after the other's injury or death.
detail: Common-law action available to spouses; some states extend to children (loss of parental consortium) and parents (loss of child consortium). Recovery typically tied to the underlying injury claim. Same-sex spouses now eligible post-Obergefell (2015). Damages often capped under state medical-malpractice reforms. The claim's name reflects archaic 'consortium' terminology that has migrated from intimate companionship to broader relational losses.

snappy: A seller with unlimited supply can claim full lost profit from a buyer's breach — even after reselling the goods.
detail: UCC § 2-708(2). Standard damages formula (resale - contract price) understates harm for sellers with effectively unlimited inventory. Such sellers would have made both sales — resale doesn't replace lost first sale. Courts require seller to prove unlimited supply and likely additional sale (Neri v. Retail Marine, 1972). Common application: car dealers, mass-produced consumer goods, software licences. Hot topic in B2B SaaS subscription cancellation disputes.

snappy: A one-time alimony payment instead of ongoing monthly payments.
detail: Advantages: clean break, no ongoing connection between exes, no modification risk for payor, no collection risk for payee. Tax treatment uncertain post-TCJA — characterisation as property settlement vs alimony has implications. Common in higher-asset divorces with sufficient liquid assets. Often paid from retirement-account QDRO transfers or refinanced home equity. Less common in cases requiring ongoing support due to disability or limited earning capacity.

full: Material adverse change / material adverse effect
snappy: Contract provision letting buyer walk if target suffers a material adverse change between signing and closing.
detail: Three common 'flavours': MAC (impact on target's business), MAE (broader adverse effect), prospects-language (forward-looking). Highly negotiated carve-outs: industry-wide changes, regulatory changes, war/terrorism, sometimes pandemics. Akorn v. Fresenius (Del. Ch. 2018) was a landmark MAC ruling — first finding of a MAC in Delaware. COVID-19 era saw widespread MAC litigation (most settled). Modern drafting increasingly addresses geopolitical and supply-chain triggers.

snappy: International treaty system letting trademark owners file one application designating multiple countries through WIPO.
detail: US joined in 2003. A single 'international application' based on a home-country mark designates up to 100+ member countries. Each designated country examines under its own law and can refuse. Centralised renewal and maintenance. 'Central attack' risk: if the home registration falls in the first 5 years, all dependent designations fall too. Common alternative: direct national filings in target countries.

snappy: Using the postal service or private interstate carriers in a scheme to defraud.
detail: 18 U.S.C. § 1341. The original federal fraud statute, dating to 1872. Even a routine bank statement mailed in furtherance of a scheme can trigger it. Often charged alongside wire fraud — the two statutes are interpreted identically. The 'honest services' theory under § 1346 covers public corruption and breach-of-fiduciary-duty cases, though Skilling v. United States (2010) restricted it to bribery and kickbacks.

snappy: Acceptance is effective when sent — not when received.
detail: Default common-law rule: a properly addressed, properly posted acceptance binds the offeror the moment it leaves the offeree's hands. Matters when an offer is revoked in the gap between sending and receipt. The rule doesn't apply to option contracts (where acceptance is effective on receipt) or to email/text — most courts treat instant messaging as received when sent anyway.

snappy: When an agency claims authority over a question of vast economic or political significance, courts demand clear congressional authorisation.
detail: West Virginia v. EPA (2022) crystallised the doctrine — struck down EPA's Clean Power Plan. NFIB v. OSHA (2022) struck down COVID-19 vaccine-or-test mandate for large employers. Biden v. Nebraska (2023) struck down student-loan forgiveness program. Cuts both directions: targets executive-branch overreach, but critics call it ad hoc, anti-majoritarian, and amorphous in defining 'major'. Operates as a clear-statement rule that disfavours agency claims to broad authority.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Filing a lawsuit (or pressing criminal charges) without probable cause and with malicious intent — when the underlying case ends in your favour.
detail: Required elements: institution of prior proceedings against plaintiff, defendant lacked probable cause, malice, prior proceedings terminated favourably for plaintiff, damages. The 'favourable termination' element is critical and often defeats claims. Civil malicious-prosecution claims are disfavoured in many jurisdictions (concerns about chilling legitimate litigation). Special damages often required in civil cases.

snappy: A statutory floor: judges can't sentence below the minimum specified by Congress (or state legislature).
detail: Most common in federal drug offences (21 U.S.C. § 841) and firearm offences (18 U.S.C. § 924(c)). 'Safety valve' provisions (18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)) provide narrow relief for low-level offenders meeting strict criteria — broadened by First Step Act (2018). Widely criticised for transferring sentencing power from judges to prosecutors (who pick the charge). 'Stacking' multiple § 924(c) counts produced extreme sentences before First Step Act limited it.

snappy: Killing without the malice that makes murder — either provoked into passion or grossly negligent.
detail: Voluntary manslaughter: heat-of-passion killings after adequate provocation (classic example: catching a spouse in adultery). Involuntary manslaughter: deaths from criminal negligence or unlawful acts not amounting to felonies — drunk-driving fatalities often charged here. Carries shorter sentences than murder. The Casey Anthony (2011) acquittal hinged on jurors finding insufficient evidence even for manslaughter.

snappy: A specific product unit deviates from its intended design — the lemon, not the product line.
detail: Most cleanly proven type of defect: just show the product differed from specifications. Doesn't require alternative-design analysis. Examples: missing rivet, contaminated batch of pharmaceutical, wiring error in specific car. Strict liability without need to prove how the defect occurred — manufacturer is liable even with rigorous quality control if a defective unit reaches the market. Recall systems address this category.

snappy: Property acquired during the marriage — generally subject to division upon divorce.
detail: Includes income earned during marriage, real estate purchased during marriage, businesses built during marriage, retirement accounts accrued during marriage, debts incurred during marriage. Excludes separate property in most regimes. Tracing rules (commingling, transmutation) handle property that started separate but mixed. Pre-marital businesses appreciating during marriage often present sophisticated tracing and valuation issues. Forensic accountants commonly engaged.

snappy: The signed contract resolving all property, support, and custody issues in a divorce.
detail: Drafted by attorneys; signed by both spouses; incorporated into divorce judgment. Sections: identification, property division, alimony, child custody and visitation, child support, attorneys' fees, miscellaneous (taxes, life insurance, retirement). Court reviews for fairness — particularly for unrepresented parties and child-support compliance with guidelines. Once incorporated, breach is contempt of court rather than just contract breach. Major source of post-divorce enforcement litigation.

snappy: Title good enough that a reasonable buyer would accept it — free from significant doubts or unreasonable risks.
detail: The implied standard in real-estate sales contracts unless otherwise specified. Minor defects (curable easements, modest encumbrances) usually OK; serious defects (clouded chain, unreleased mortgages, undisclosed liens) excuse the buyer's performance. Marketable Title Acts in many states automatically extinguish stale claims after a fixed period (often 30-40 years) to clean up old records.

snappy: A legally recognised union between two people — state-licensed, with rights to inheritance, taxes, benefits, and decision-making.
detail: Each state regulates marriage requirements: licensing, blood tests (now rare), age limits, waiting periods. Obergefell v. Hodges (2015) constitutionalised same-sex marriage nationwide. Respect for Marriage Act (2022) codified federal recognition. Marriage carries hundreds of legal consequences: spousal privilege, tax filing status, immigration sponsorship, intestate inheritance, health-care decision-making, FMLA leave, Social Security survivor benefits.

snappy: Government-issued authorisation to marry — required before a wedding ceremony.
detail: Each state regulates: minimum age (now 18 in most states; underage marriages largely banned post-2018 movements), waiting periods (24-72 hours typical), expiration (30-90 days), officiant qualifications. Solemnisation typically by judge, religious official, or in some states a notary or 'self-uniting' procedure. Once filed with state vital-records office, marriage is legally established. Some states require pre-marriage education or counselling for waiver of waiting period.

snappy: Umbrella contract setting overall terms — specific deals layered on as separate 'statements of work'.
detail: Standard in consulting, IT services, marketing agencies, and outsourcing. The MSA covers stable terms (payment terms, IP ownership, confidentiality, liability caps, governing law, indemnification), while each SOW handles deliverables, schedule, and price for a specific engagement. Lets sophisticated customers negotiate the legal terms once and run dozens of projects against them.

snappy: A breach serious enough to excuse the other side from continuing to perform.
detail: Restatement § 241 factors: extent of benefit deprived, adequacy of damages, forfeiture by breaching party, likelihood of cure, good faith. Material breach lets the non-breacher walk away and sue for total damages. Minor breach (substantial performance) entitles only damages, not termination. Hard line to draw; turns on facts, often litigated.

snappy: Three-step burden-shifting test for circumstantial-evidence employment discrimination claims.
detail: McDonnell Douglas v. Green (1973). Plaintiff establishes prima facie case (membership in protected class, qualification, adverse action, more-favourable treatment of similarly situated). Burden shifts to employer to articulate legitimate non-discriminatory reason. Plaintiff then must show that reason is pretext for discrimination. Workhorse framework for individual disparate-treatment claims. Mixed-motive cases under 1991 Civil Rights Act use different framework.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: A formula determining whether a consumer can file Chapter 7 — meant to channel above-median-income debtors into Chapter 13.
detail: BAPCPA (2005) addition. Two parts: (1) household income vs state median; (2) if above median, calculation of 'disposable income' allowing payment to unsecured creditors. Allowable expenses include IRS National and Local Standards for food, housing, transportation. Result: about 95% of Chapter 7 filers pass the means test (after BAPCPA's initial impact). Above-median filers needing Chapter 7 can sometimes qualify via expense calculations.

snappy: A contractor, subcontractor, or supplier can put a lien on real property they improved if not paid.
detail: Created by state statute; rules vary widely. Notice and timing requirements are strict — missed deadlines forfeit the lien. Priority often relates back to project commencement, jumping later mortgages. Owners protect themselves by requiring lien waivers from each tier of contractors before final payment. Properly perfected mechanic's liens can force foreclosure sale of the entire property. Trade-creditor protection in a security-interest-poor industry.

snappy: A required conversation between lawyers to try resolving a discovery dispute before going to the judge.
detail: Federal Rule 37(a)(1) requires a good-faith attempt to confer before filing a motion to compel. Local rules in many districts require certification that meet-and-confer occurred. Often a phone call or video conference; written summaries follow. Judges hate hearing disputes that lawyers could have worked out themselves — failure to meet-and-confer is a fast track to losing on procedural grounds.

snappy: The mental state required for a crime — knowing, intentional, reckless, or negligent.
detail: Most serious crimes require some level of culpable mental state. The Model Penal Code defines four: purposeful (acting with conscious goal), knowing (aware act will cause result), reckless (conscious disregard of risk), negligent (should have known of risk). Strict-liability crimes (statutory rape in some states, most traffic infractions) require no mens rea at all — controversial because they punish without moral fault.

snappy: Combining two companies into one — surviving entity absorbs the other.
detail: Two flavours: forward (target merges into acquirer or subsidiary) and reverse (acquirer's subsidiary merges into target). Approvals required: typically board, sometimes shareholders (depending on size and structure). State merger statutes (DGCL § 251 etc.) govern. Appraisal rights for dissenting shareholders. Antitrust review (HSR notification). Securities-law disclosure: proxy statement for shareholder vote, 8-K disclosures. Closing conditions include regulatory approval, no MAC, accuracy of representations.

snappy: Post-2017 wave of state and federal laws addressing workplace sexual harassment.
detail: Key federal: Forced Arbitration of Sexual Assault Act and Speak Out Act (2022) limited mandatory arbitration and NDAs for sexual harassment claims. State laws: New York's prohibition on NDAs for harassment without complainant consent; California's SB 1300 limiting separation agreement releases; many states banning forced arbitration. Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (2017) eliminated tax deduction for sexual harassment settlements with NDAs. Continues to expand annually.

snappy: Ownership of subsurface minerals — oil, gas, coal, hard-rock minerals — can be separated from surface ownership.
detail: 'Severance' transactions split mineral and surface estates, sometimes by centuries-old deeds still controlling modern transactions. Mineral-rights owners have implied right to reasonable surface access. Common in oil-and-gas country (Texas, Oklahoma, Pennsylvania), coal regions, and federal lands. Federal General Mining Law of 1872 still governs hard-rock minerals on federal land — a frequent reform target. Mineral leases generate royalty income for owners.

snappy: The constitutional floor for personal jurisdiction — a defendant must have purposeful connections to the forum state.
detail: From International Shoe v. Washington (1945): jurisdiction is fair only if maintaining the suit there doesn't 'offend traditional notions of fair play and substantial justice'. Internet and mail contacts complicate the analysis. Calder v. Jones (1984) added the 'effects test' for intentional torts aimed at a state. Walden v. Fiore (2014) restricted the effects test — contacts must be defendant's, not just plaintiff's.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: The lowest hourly rate an employer can pay — federal floor with state and city overlays.
detail: Federal: $7.25/hour since 2009. Many states higher: California, Washington, Massachusetts all over $15. Major cities even higher: Seattle, San Francisco, NYC at $16-19+. Tipped employees: federal credit allows $2.13 base if tips bring total to minimum (state laws often eliminate tip credit). Subminimum certificates for workers with disabilities phasing out. Minimum-wage debates have driven ongoing state-by-state expansion despite federal stasis.

snappy: The 'You have the right to remain silent…' warning given before custodial interrogation.
detail: From Miranda v. Arizona (1966). Required before police can question a suspect 'in custody'. Statements obtained in violation are inadmissible in the prosecution's case-in-chief (though can sometimes be used for impeachment). Police can ask 'routine booking questions' without Miranda. The warnings vary somewhat by jurisdiction; the core four: right to remain silent, anything said can be used, right to attorney, attorney provided if can't afford.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Acceptance must match the offer exactly — any deviation is a counter-offer.
detail: Strict common-law doctrine still applied to service contracts and other non-goods deals. Modern commercial reality (purchase orders crossing acknowledgments) led the UCC drafters to soften it for goods via § 2-207. Lawyers exchanging redlines in heavy negotiations effectively make a series of counter-offers until both sides sign the same document.

snappy: Acquiring, disclosing, or using someone's trade secret through improper means or breach of duty.
detail: Defined by DTSA and Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Three classic forms: (1) acquisition by improper means (theft, bribery, espionage), (2) disclosure or use by someone who acquired it through improper means or duty, (3) disclosure or use with reason to know it was a trade secret. Reverse engineering and independent development don't qualify. Damages: actual losses plus unjust enrichment, sometimes royalty, often plus injunctive relief.

snappy: Wrongly treating workers as independent contractors or exempt employees — major liability source.
detail: Sources of liability: minimum wage, overtime, employer payroll taxes (15.3% of wages for FICA), workers' compensation premiums, unemployment insurance, employee benefits (especially ERISA-governed plans). Multi-state employers face conflicting tests. Statute of limitations: 2 years for non-willful FLSA violations, 3 years willful, longer for tax. Civil settlements regularly exceed $10M-$100M+ in class actions; criminal exposure for egregious misclassification.

snappy: A less serious crime — generally punishable by a year or less in jail, or a fine.
detail: Examples: petty theft, simple assault, public intoxication, disorderly conduct, first-offence DUI in some states. Despite the lesser sentence, misdemeanour convictions can still affect employment, immigration, and housing. The 'misdemeanour-felony' line varies by state — same conduct (theft, drug possession) can be either depending on amount and prior record. Most criminal cases in US courts are misdemeanours.

snappy: A false statement that induces someone to enter a contract — voidable for fraud, sometimes for innocent misstatement.
detail: Restatement (Second) §§ 159-173. Three flavours: fraudulent (knowingly false, made to induce reliance), negligent (carelessly made), innocent (made in good faith but factually wrong). Remedies: rescission (cancellation) plus restitution; fraudulent misrepresentation also supports tort damages. Element of justifiable reliance often hotly contested. Modern commercial contracts include anti-reliance clauses to disclaim reliance on extra-contractual statements.

snappy: A trial terminated before verdict because of a serious procedural defect.
detail: Triggers: hung jury, prosecutorial misconduct, juror misconduct, inadmissible evidence reaching the jury, prejudicial errors that can't be cured by instruction. Defence-requested mistrials usually allow retrial without double jeopardy bar; sua sponte mistrials require 'manifest necessity' to permit retrial (Arizona v. Washington, 1978). Strategic incentive: defence sometimes prefers mistrial when the existing trial seems lost.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: A circumstance that argues for a lighter sentence — youth, abuse history, mental illness, minor role.
detail: Federal Sentencing Guidelines list mitigators including acceptance of responsibility (§ 3E1.1) and minor role (§ 3B1.2). Constitutional rule requires sentencer in capital cases to consider any mitigating evidence (Lockett v. Ohio, 1978; Eddings v. Oklahoma, 1982). Roper v. Simmons (2005) categorically barred death penalty for juvenile crimes; Miller v. Alabama (2012) barred mandatory life-without-parole for juveniles. Mitigation specialists are now standard in capital defence.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Duty of the non-breaching party to take reasonable steps to limit their losses.
detail: A fired employee must look for comparable work; a wronged landlord must try to re-let. Failure to mitigate doesn't bar recovery, but it reduces damages by the amount that reasonable effort would have saved. Standard is reasonableness — the plaintiff doesn't have to take heroic measures or accept inferior substitutes. Sometimes called 'the doctrine of avoidable consequences'.

snappy: Changing existing custody or visitation order — generally requires showing material change in circumstances.
detail: Standards: substantial change in circumstances since last order, plus modification serving child's best interest. Examples of material change: parental relocation, change in job or schedule, child's changing needs, custodial parent's substance abuse or mental-health deterioration. Some states impose waiting periods after final orders (typically 2 years). Frequent re-litigation discouraged by 'changed circumstances' requirement — but heavily contested. Final orders rarely truly final in family law.

snappy: Disguising the proceeds of crime to make them look legitimate — turning dirty money into clean money.
detail: 18 U.S.C. §§ 1956–1957. Three classic stages: placement (depositing cash), layering (moving through multiple accounts), integration (spending as if legitimate). Bank Secrecy Act requires banks to file Suspicious Activity Reports and Currency Transaction Reports over $10,000. Cryptocurrency tumblers and shell-company chains are modern laundering tools. Drug-trafficking proceeds and corruption are the most common predicates. Forfeiture proceedings often accompany prosecution.

snappy: A case becomes moot if the controversy resolves itself before judicial decision — courts won't decide moot cases.
detail: Article III 'case or controversy' requirement. Exceptions: capable of repetition yet evading review (Roe v. Wade pre-Dobbs, election cases); voluntary cessation (defendant must show 'absolutely clear' wrongful conduct can't recur); class-action representative claims (mootness doesn't end class claims). Modern frequent issues: government policy changes mid-litigation, mooted by political transitions, vaccine-mandate cases through pandemic.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Visual artists have limited rights of attribution and integrity for their works — even after sale.
detail: Visual Artists Rights Act (1990), 17 U.S.C. § 106A. Covers paintings, drawings, prints, sculptures, and limited-edition photos. Rights: attribution and prevention of destruction or modification harming reputation. Castillo v. G&M Realty (2d Cir. 2020) upheld $6.7 million award for whitewashed graffiti art. Limited reach compared to broad EU and Latin American moral-rights regimes. Right can be waived in writing, often demanded by buyers.

snappy: A loan secured by real estate — if you don't pay, the lender can take the property.
detail: Two-part instrument: the promissory note (the IOU) and the mortgage itself (the security interest). Standard 30-year fixed-rate mortgages dominate the US market thanks to the secondary market run by Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac. After the 2008 crisis, the Dodd-Frank ability-to-repay rules tightened underwriting. The lender records the mortgage in county land records to perfect its priority against later claims.

snappy: If you give a better deal to anyone else, I get the same — extending the best terms to the MFN-protected party.
detail: Common in long-term supply, distribution, licensing, and franchise agreements. Antitrust scrutiny: MFN clauses can facilitate price coordination among competitors (US v. Apple, 2013 — eBook MFN as price-fixing facilitator). Recent state attorneys-general action against pharmaceutical MFN provisions tied to drug-pricing disputes. Common in hotel-OTA (online travel agency) contracts before regulators pushed back.

snappy: A formal written request asking the judge to do something — dismiss the case, suppress evidence, grant summary judgment.
detail: Motions drive most of pretrial litigation. The motion to dismiss tests whether the complaint, even if true, states a legal claim. The motion for summary judgment asks the judge to decide without a trial because no factual dispute matters. Motions in limine handle evidence rulings before trial begins. Each is briefed by both sides, often with oral argument.

snappy: Post-verdict motion asking the judge to wipe the trial result and start over.
detail: Federal Rule 59. Grounds include jury misconduct, newly discovered evidence, excessive damages, errors of law, or verdict 'against the weight of the evidence'. Filed within 28 days of judgment. Often paired with a Rule 50(b) motion for judgment as a matter of law as an alternative. Standards are deferential to the jury — motions for new trial are usually denied.

snappy: A motion asking the judge to decide the case without trial because no fact is genuinely disputed.
detail: Federal Rule 56. Filed after discovery. Movant must show 'no genuine dispute as to any material fact' and entitlement to judgment as a matter of law. Celotex v. Catrett (1986) lowered the burden on movants. The court takes evidence in the light most favourable to the non-movant. Partial summary judgment can narrow trial to specific issues. Most commercial cases end here, not at trial.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: A pre-trial motion to exclude (or permit) specific evidence before the jury hears it.
detail: Lets the judge rule on evidentiary disputes in advance, so jurors aren't tainted by inadmissible material. Common targets: prior bad acts, prejudicial photos, expert testimony failing Daubert, settlement discussions, insurance coverage. Rulings can be tentative — the judge can revisit at trial. A favourable in limine ruling often drives settlement because it telegraphs how the jury will see the case.

snappy: A motion asking the judge to force the other side to comply with a discovery request.
detail: Federal Rule 37(a). Filed when interrogatories go unanswered, document productions are deficient, or a deponent refuses to answer. Requires prior meet-and-confer. Courts can shift fees to the losing side. Repeat motions to compel signal abusive discovery tactics and can escalate to terminating sanctions — default judgment or dismissal in extreme cases.

snappy: A pre-answer motion asking the judge to throw out a case for legal defects in the complaint.
detail: Federal Rule 12(b) lists seven grounds: lack of jurisdiction, improper venue, insufficient process, and the most-used (b)(6) — 'failure to state a claim'. Twombly (2007) and Iqbal (2009) raised the pleading bar: complaints need 'plausible' factual allegations, not just labels. Granting dismissal usually ends the case (sometimes with leave to amend); denial means the case proceeds to discovery.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: A motion to remove improper material from a pleading or the record — redundant, immaterial, impertinent, or scandalous matter.
detail: Federal Rule 12(f) for pleadings; Rule 16 and local rules cover other filings. Used against vexatious allegations meant for press coverage rather than legal effect, or affirmative defences that fail as a matter of law. Disfavoured but granted where prejudice is clear. Often paired with a motion for sanctions when opposing counsel files clearly improper material.

snappy: Both sides have to mean the same thing — the 'meeting of the minds'.
detail: Assessed objectively: what would a reasonable person have understood from the words and conduct, not what the parties privately thought. Mistakes about identity, subject matter, or terms can negate assent. Raffles v. Wichelhaus (1864) — the famous 'Peerless' ship case — held no contract when two ships of the same name caused genuine confusion. Modern courts rarely rescue parties from their own carelessness.

snappy: Both parties shared a wrong belief about a basic fact at contracting — voidable by the disadvantaged party.
detail: Restatement (Second) § 152. Requires (1) mistake by both parties, (2) about a basic assumption, (3) material effect on performance, (4) party seeking relief didn't bear the risk. Sherwood v. Walker (Mich. 1887) — cow thought barren, turned out fertile — paradigm case. Distinct from unilateral mistake (much narrower relief) and mutual misunderstanding (no contract formed). Modern commercial settings: undisclosed liabilities in M&A, mistaken cost assumptions in construction.

snappy: Federal statute requiring environmental analysis of major federal actions — informational, not substantive.
detail: 42 U.S.C. § 4321 et seq. (1970). Triggers: federal funding, permits, or direct federal action. Three tiers of analysis: categorical exclusion, environmental assessment, environmental impact statement. Council on Environmental Quality coordinates implementation. Fiscal Responsibility Act (2023) imposed page limits and time deadlines; many projects still face years of analysis. Most-litigated environmental statute — courts probe sufficiency of analysis but rarely review substance.

snappy: The process by which a lawful permanent resident becomes a US citizen.
detail: Standard requirements: 5 years as LPR (3 for spouses of citizens), continuous residence, physical presence, good moral character, English language and US civics knowledge, attachment to US Constitution. Application: Form N-400, biometrics, interview, oath ceremony. Annual naturalisations: 700,000-1,000,000. Process typically takes 12-18 months. Children naturalise automatically if parent naturalises (subject to conditions under Child Citizenship Act 2000). Dual citizenship permitted in the US.

snappy: A contract to keep specified information confidential.
detail: Unilateral (one side discloses) or mutual (both do). Key terms: scope of confidential information, exclusions (already public, independently developed), duration of obligation, permitted uses, return-or-destroy at termination. Overbroad NDAs run into trade-secret defences and, in some states, anti-NDA statutes for sexual harassment. Federal Defend Trade Secrets Act notice should be included to preserve whistleblower-immunity protections.

snappy: Lets Congress pass laws 'necessary and proper' to carry out its enumerated powers — the elastic clause.
detail: McCulloch v. Maryland (1819) — Marshall: 'Let the end be legitimate... and all means appropriate, which are not prohibited.' Foundation of broad federal authority. NFIB v. Sebelius (2012) declined to use the Necessary and Proper Clause to save the ACA's individual mandate from Commerce Clause infirmity. Constitutional textualists debate its scope — particularly whether it can authorise novel powers absent specific enumerated power foundation.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Failing to use the care a reasonable person would — the workhorse tort.
detail: Four elements: duty of care, breach, causation (factual + proximate), and damages. The reasonable-person standard is objective: what would a reasonable person have done? Comparative negligence in most states reduces damages by the plaintiff's share of fault. Famous duty case: Palsgraf v. Long Island Railroad (1928) — Justice Cardozo limiting duty to the foreseeable plaintiff. The vast majority of personal-injury cases are negligence claims.

snappy: Violating a safety statute counts automatically as negligence — the statutory violation is the breach.
detail: Restatement (Second) of Torts § 286. Requires (1) defendant violated statute, (2) injury type the statute was meant to prevent, (3) plaintiff in the protected class. Doesn't excuse the plaintiff from proving causation and damages. Common applications: traffic-code violations, building-code violations, food-safety violations. Some states treat as conclusive negligence; others as evidentiary presumption rebuttable by reasonable-care showing.

snappy: Recovering for emotional harm caused by negligence — historically restricted but expanded over the 20th century.
detail: Older rule: required physical impact or 'zone of danger' (Dillon v. Legg, Cal. 1968, broke this). Modern factors: closeness to incident, observation of event, relationship to victim. Restatement (Third) recognises NIED with specific limits. Bystander recovery limited to close family members witnessing serious injury. Direct-victim claims arise in mishandling of dead bodies, false telegrams of death, mishandling of medical results.

snappy: The principle that internet service providers should treat all internet traffic equally — no blocking, throttling, or paid prioritisation.
detail: FCC has flip-flopped between Title II classification (utility-like regulation) and Title I classification (lighter touch) across administrations. Verizon v. FCC (D.C. Cir. 2014) struck FCC's first net-neutrality rule. 2015 Open Internet Order under Title II survived in USTA v. FCC (2016) but was repealed in 2017. Restored in 2024 then re-challenged. State-level net-neutrality laws (California, others) operate independently. Persistent partisan-policy oscillation.

snappy: The Constitution's enumeration of rights doesn't deny others 'retained by the people'.
detail: Rarely the operative basis of decision — more a rule of construction. Cited supportingly in Griswold v. Connecticut (1965) on privacy rights. Some scholars (Randy Barnett) advocate for it as an independent rights source. Courts generally treat it as confirming unenumerated rights exist without prescribing specific ones. The cousin clause to the Tenth Amendment on the federalism side.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

full: National Labor Relations Act / Wagner Act
snappy: Federal statute guaranteeing private-sector workers' right to organise, bargain collectively, and engage in concerted activity.
detail: 29 U.S.C. §§ 151 et seq. (1935). Administered by National Labor Relations Board (NLRB). Section 7 rights: organising, bargaining, concerted activity, even without union. Section 8 unfair labour practices for employers (firing for union activity, refusal to bargain) and unions. Taft-Hartley Act (1947) added union ULPs. State 'right-to-work' laws ban mandatory dues. Modern issues: gig workers' coverage, social media activity protection, joint-employer questions.

snappy: A divorce that doesn't require proving misconduct — irreconcilable differences are enough.
detail: All 50 states now offer no-fault divorce (last holdout was New York, in 2010). Reduces hostility and litigation cost. Common grounds: irreconcilable differences, irretrievable breakdown, incompatibility, living apart for a statutory period (often 1 year). Some states retain fault grounds as option — relevant in some states for alimony or property division. Critics argue no-fault has weakened marriage; defenders cite reduced false-allegation incentives and lower-conflict resolution.

snappy: A warrant authorising police to enter without first knocking and announcing.
detail: Generally requires reasonable suspicion that knock-and-announce would endanger officers, allow evidence destruction, or be futile (Richards v. Wisconsin, 1997). Application has been controversial: the 2020 Breonna Taylor shooting in Louisville happened during a no-knock raid (though officers said they did announce). Many cities and states banned or restricted no-knock warrants following the killing. Federal law-enforcement agencies imposed substantial restrictions through 2023.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Clause barring an employee from working for competitors after leaving — within limits.
detail: Enforceability varies dramatically by state. California has banned them since 1872; FTC's 2024 federal ban was blocked by a Texas federal court before taking effect, currently on appeal. Where enforceable (most states), restrictions must be 'reasonable' in scope, geography, and duration — typically capped at 1–2 years. Heavy enforcement against high-paid executives and salespeople; broad use against low-wage workers is the policy battleground.

snappy: Congress can't transfer its legislative power to administrative agencies without an 'intelligible principle'.
detail: Almost dormant for decades after 1935 — Schechter Poultry was the last successful non-delegation challenge. Gundy v. United States (2019) plurality declined to revive; Justice Gorsuch's dissent signaled future battles. Increasingly cited in challenges to broad agency authority. Tension with the major-questions doctrine — both reflect skepticism of administrative power. Justice Thomas has argued for stricter non-delegation enforcement.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Debts that survive bankruptcy — debtor remains personally liable.
detail: 11 U.S.C. § 523. Major categories: most student loans (without 'undue hardship' showing), federal taxes within 3 years, alimony and child support, debts from fraud, debts from wilful and malicious injury, criminal fines and restitution, DUI-injury debts. Bankruptcy litigation often addresses whether specific debts are non-dischargeable — fraud and breach-of-fiduciary-duty cases especially. Student-loan discharge standard somewhat loosened by 2022 DOJ/DOE policy changes.

snappy: An employee entitled to overtime pay under the FLSA — usually hourly workers in non-managerial roles.
detail: Default classification — exempt status requires affirmative qualification. Major implications: hours must be tracked, overtime required for hours over 40 (or over 8 daily in some states), no required meal/rest period under FLSA but most states require. Misclassification suits and DOL audits regularly produce major recoveries — back-pay plus liquidated damages (essentially doubling) plus attorneys' fees. Modern wage-theft prevention statutes in many states add even higher penalties.

snappy: Temporary visa for specific purposes — work, study, tourism, investment, transit.
detail: Categories alphabetised: A (diplomats), B (tourism/business), C (transit), D (crew), E (treaty trader/investor/Australian E-3), F (students), H (workers — H-1B specialty occupation famous), J (exchange visitor), L (intracompany transfer), M (vocational student), O (extraordinary ability), P (performers), TN (NAFTA/USMCA), U (crime victim). Each has specific eligibility, duration, work authorisation rules. Maintenance of status required to avoid violations.

snappy: Bar on a departing employee from poaching customers or colleagues for a specified period.
detail: More widely enforceable than non-competes — even California enforces customer non-solicits in some employment contexts. Two flavours: customer non-solicits (don't approach the company's customers) and employee non-solicits (don't recruit former colleagues). Modern challenges focus on what counts as 'solicitation' on social media — LinkedIn-era case law is still developing.

snappy: A use that was lawful when started but doesn't comply with newer zoning rules — grandfathered.
detail: Created when zoning changes around a pre-existing use (the corner grocery still operating in a now-residential zone). Allowed to continue, but expansion, intensification, and rebuilding after destruction are typically restricted. Amortisation schedules in some jurisdictions phase out nonconforming uses over a period of years. Loss of nonconforming status often follows abandonment, change of use, or destruction beyond a certain threshold.

snappy: The DMCA procedure for getting infringing content removed from online platforms.
detail: Copyright owner sends a DMCA notice (17 U.S.C. § 512(c)(3)) identifying the work, location of infringement, and good-faith belief of infringement. Service provider removes content to keep safe-harbor protection. User can counter-notify; if they do, content goes back up unless owner sues within 10-14 days. Lenz v. Universal Music (9th Cir. 2015) requires consideration of fair use before sending. Routine on YouTube, Twitch, Reddit, Twitter/X.

snappy: The federal standard: a complaint needs only enough detail to put the defendant on fair notice of the claim.
detail: Adopted by the Federal Rules in 1938 to replace older 'fact pleading' regimes. Conley v. Gibson (1957) summarised: 'no set of facts' standard. Twombly (2007) and Iqbal (2009) introduced 'plausibility' — critics called it pleading-standard inflation. Some states (California, New York) follow more demanding fact-pleading regimes. Heightened pleading required in fraud and securities claims (Rule 9(b), PSLRA).
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Required rulemaking process — agency proposes a rule, takes public comments, then finalises.
detail: Under § 553 of the APA. Agency publishes notice of proposed rulemaking in the Federal Register, sets a comment period (usually 60+ days), reviews comments, and publishes final rule with explanation of major changes. Skipping the process — without a valid exception — gets the rule struck down. Courts review whether the agency adequately responded to substantial comments. Major rules can attract hundreds of thousands of comments.

snappy: Substituting a new party or new obligation for an old one — with all parties' consent.
detail: Releases the original obligor from the original obligation. Used in M&A (target's contracts assigned to acquirer), corporate restructurings, and assignment situations where the assignee actually takes over the duty (not just the right). Distinct from assignment (which transfers rights but typically keeps the assignor on the hook) and accord-and-satisfaction (which replaces the obligation but not the parties).

snappy: A patent claim must cover something not previously disclosed to the public — the heart of patentability.
detail: 35 U.S.C. § 102 (post-AIA): no prior public disclosure, use, sale, or patent more than one year before filing (grace period). 'On-sale bar' invalidates patents for inventions offered commercially before the critical date. Helsinn Healthcare v. Teva (2019) confirmed the on-sale bar applies to secret commercial sales. First-to-file rule (post-2013) replaced first-to-invent — earlier filer wins when both invented independently.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Interfering with a pending or anticipated proceeding — by destroying evidence, intimidating witnesses, or lying to investigators.
detail: Sprawling federal coverage in 18 U.S.C. §§ 1501–1521. Specific provisions cover witness tampering (§ 1512), evidence destruction (§ 1519, expanded after Enron), and judicial proceedings (§ 1503). Sarbanes-Oxley added the powerful 'omnibus' provision criminalising any 'corrupt' act interfering with official proceedings. Famous prosecutions: Martha Stewart (the obstruction count, not insider trading itself), Mike Flynn, Roger Stone.

snappy: A patent fails if the invention would have been obvious to a 'person having ordinary skill in the art' at the time.
detail: 35 U.S.C. § 103. KSR v. Teleflex (2007) loosened the rigid 'teaching, suggestion, or motivation' test, allowing more flexible common-sense analysis of whether prior-art combinations were predictable. Graham v. John Deere (1966) sets the four-factor framework: scope of prior art, differences from claim, level of ordinary skill, secondary considerations (commercial success, long-felt need). Bread-and-butter rejection in patent prosecution.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: A clear proposal that, if accepted, creates a contract.
detail: An offer must be definite enough that a reasonable person would understand they could close the deal by accepting. Advertisements are usually not offers — they're invitations to deal. The Lefkowitz fur-coat case (1957) is the classic counterexample: a clear quantity and price plus 'first come, first served' made the ad itself an offer. An offer can be revoked any time before acceptance, with narrow exceptions.

snappy: An adoption where the birth parents and adoptive family maintain some ongoing contact.
detail: Most US adoptions are now at least semi-open. Spectrum: letters and photos exchanged, periodic visits, ongoing communication. Post-adoption contact agreements enforceable in some states; not in others. Considered beneficial for child's identity development, knowledge of biological/genetic information, and emotional adjustment. Birth parents often value ongoing connection. Closed adoption preference of mid-20th century has substantially reversed.

snappy: The internal governance contract for an LLC.
detail: Sets management structure (member-managed or manager-managed), capital contributions, distributions, voting, transfers of interests, and dissolution. Delaware LLC law is famously permissive — operating agreements can override most defaults. Multi-member LLCs without one fall back on default state rules, which often don't match what the members actually wanted. Single-member LLCs increasingly still draft them for piercing-the-veil protection.

snappy: A separate, paid-for promise to keep an offer open — can't be revoked during the option period.
detail: Real-estate option agreements (buyer pays $5,000 for the right to purchase within 90 days) are classic examples. Stock options use the same structure. Option contracts require their own consideration; the underlying offer is supported by the option payment. The Restatement § 87 and the UCC's 'firm offer' rule (§ 2-205) create exceptions where some offers can't be revoked even without consideration.

snappy: A court order barring an alleged abuser from contact with a victim — civil remedy distinct from criminal charges.
detail: Available in all states. Procedure: emergency ex parte order (often same-day), followed by full hearing within 7-14 days. Standard: preponderance of evidence (lower than criminal). Provisions: stay-away orders, no-contact (including electronic), surrender of firearms, custody temporary orders, residence exclusion. Violation triggers criminal contempt or specific statutory criminal offence. Federal Violence Against Women Act provides funding and full-faith-and-credit interstate recognition.

snappy: Interpreting the Constitution according to its original public meaning at adoption.
detail: Modern revival led by Antonin Scalia and Robert Bork in the 1980s. Two main strands: original intent (framers' purposes) and original public meaning (how a reasonable contemporary would have read the text). Now the dominant theory on the Supreme Court — Bruen (2022), Dobbs (2022), Kennedy v. Bremerton (2022) all rest on history-and-tradition analysis. Critics argue history is often indeterminate, manipulable, or unattainable.

snappy: Copyright protects only works that show some minimal spark of creativity — not mere sweat-of-the-brow.
detail: Feist Publications v. Rural Telephone Service (1991) held that alphabetical white-pages listings lacked sufficient originality despite the labour involved in compilation. The constitutional 'authorship' requirement (Art. I, § 8) sets the floor. Routine database compilations, simple recipes, and standard forms typically don't qualify. Generative AI raises new questions: the Copyright Office requires human authorship for registration.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Federal workplace-safety statute administered by OSHA (Occupational Safety and Health Administration).
detail: 29 U.S.C. §§ 651 et seq. (1970). General Duty Clause (§ 5(a)(1)): employers must furnish workplace 'free from recognised hazards'. Specific standards for industries (construction, agriculture, general industry). OSHRC adjudicates citations. NFIB v. OSHA (2022) struck COVID-19 vaccine-or-test mandate — major-questions doctrine. State plans (about half the states) operate parallel programs subject to federal approval.

snappy: A contract to buy all of seller's production — quantity determined by what the seller produces.
detail: UCC § 2-306. Enforceable despite quantity indefiniteness — actual output is the quantity. Good-faith requirement: seller can't ramp up output to take advantage of favourable price, or cut output to escape unfavorable price. Modern applications: agricultural cooperatives' arrangements with processors, renewable-energy power-purchase agreements. Combined with requirements contracts in the same UCC section, output contracts illustrate the UCC's flexibility about quantity.

snappy: Premium pay (typically 1.5x regular rate) for hours worked beyond 40 in a workweek — required for non-exempt workers.
detail: FLSA § 7. Calculation: 1.5x the 'regular rate' which includes most non-discretionary compensation (commissions, non-discretionary bonuses, shift differentials). 'Daily overtime' (8+ hours per day) required in California and a few other states. Comp time generally not allowed in private sector under federal law. Improper exemption classification is a major source of class-action litigation. State laws often more protective.

snappy: Federal statute requiring OMB approval before agencies collect information from the public.
detail: 44 U.S.C. §§ 3501 et seq. (1980). Agencies must demonstrate necessity, minimum burden, and clear utility before approval. Each information collection gets a control number displayed on forms. Designed to reduce burden on regulated parties; routinely criticised as inadequate to its goal. OMB's Office of Information and Regulatory Affairs (OIRA) is the gatekeeper. Civil society organisations regularly comment on burden estimates.

snappy: President's Article II power to grant 'reprieves and pardons for offenses against the United States'.
detail: Reaches federal offenses only; no state crimes. Can be granted before or after conviction. Famous controversial pardons: Nixon (by Ford, 1974), Marc Rich (Clinton, 2001), various end-of-term Trump pardons (2021), Hunter Biden (2024). Doesn't reach impeachment. Burdick v. United States (1915) treated acceptance of a pardon as carrying implicit admission of guilt — though never decisive in practice. Pardons can be conditional.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: A pattern where one parent turns the child against the other parent — sometimes considered emotional abuse.
detail: Controversial concept: clinical 'parental alienation syndrome' (PAS) coined in the 1980s but not recognised in DSM-5. Family courts in many states recognise alienating behaviour as relevant to custody. Indicators: persistent denigration, blocking communication, false abuse allegations, encouraging child's rejection of other parent. Remedies: custody modification, reunification therapy, sometimes transfer of primary custody to alienated parent. Hot-button issue in high-conflict divorce.

snappy: When a custodial parent wants to move with the child far enough to disrupt the existing custody schedule — requires court approval.
detail: Standards vary by state: California requires showing move serves child's best interest; New York and other states use 'detriment to noncustodial parent' analysis; some states presume against relocation. Distance triggers vary: 100 miles, 150 miles, out-of-state. Notice requirements common: 60-90 days before move. High-conflict cases — major source of family-court litigation. Considerations: job opportunities, support network, child's school, ongoing relationship with both parents.

snappy: A detailed written agreement or court order setting out custody, visitation, decision-making, and dispute resolution.
detail: Modern divorce courts in most states require detailed parenting plans rather than vague custody orders. Common elements: weekday/weekend schedule, holiday rotation, summer schedule, birthdays, travel, transportation, communication during other parent's time, dispute-resolution process. Court-approved becomes enforceable; failure to follow can be contempt. Plans typically revised periodically as children grow.

snappy: Bars evidence of prior agreements that contradict a final written contract.
detail: Protects integrated writings: if the parties intended their writing to be the complete deal, earlier negotiations and verbal side-deals can't vary the terms. Exceptions allow parol evidence to show fraud, ambiguity, or collateral agreements on separate subjects. Merger clauses ('this is the entire agreement') strengthen the presumption of integration. Misnamed — applies to oral and written prior agreements alike.

snappy: Conditional release from prison before the full sentence is served, with supervision.
detail: Abolished in the federal system (Sentencing Reform Act of 1984) for crimes committed after 1987 — replaced by determinate sentencing and supervised release. Still common in state systems. Parole boards typically consider rehabilitation, risk to public, and conduct in prison. Violation of conditions can revoke parole — formal hearing required (Morrissey v. Brewer, 1972). 'Life with parole' contrasts with the harsher 'life without parole'.

snappy: Contract setting up the terms of a partnership.
detail: Default rules under the Uniform Partnership Act (or state variants) cover gap-fillers, but a written agreement is standard for any serious business. Key terms: capital contributions, profit/loss allocation, management rights, withdrawal mechanics, dispute resolution. General partnerships make each partner jointly and severally liable; limited partnerships and LLPs limit liability for non-managing partners. Many family businesses operate without one and pay the price in succession.

snappy: Government-granted monopoly on a new invention for ~20 years.
detail: Issued by the USPTO after examination for novelty, non-obviousness, and utility. Three types: utility (new functional inventions), design (ornamental designs), and plant (new plant varieties). The patent owner can exclude others from making, using, or selling — but doesn't necessarily have the right to practise their own invention if it infringes someone else's patent. Big pharma, biotech, and chip-design industries are patent-thick; software patents face hostile case law since Alice v. CLS Bank (2014).

snappy: The numbered sentences at the end of a patent that define the legal boundaries of the invention.
detail: 'Claims define the invention' — patentees often paraphrase. Independent claims stand alone; dependent claims add limitations to earlier ones. Claim construction (judge interpreting claim terms) under Markman v. Westview Instruments (1996) is the central event in patent litigation. Markman hearings often dispositive. Phillips v. AWH (Fed. Cir. 2005) emphasised intrinsic evidence (specification, prosecution history) over external dictionaries.

snappy: An international filing system letting one application establish priority in 150+ member countries.
detail: Administered by WIPO. Single PCT application + 30-month 'national phase' deadline preserves rights to pursue patents in most major economies. International search and preliminary examination provide early indicators of patentability. PCT doesn't grant patents itself — applicants must enter national phase in each desired country. Strategic foundation for global patent portfolios; expensive — typically $10,000-50,000+ for global filing.

snappy: Whether an invention falls within the four statutory categories: process, machine, manufacture, composition of matter.
detail: 35 U.S.C. § 101 also excludes 'abstract ideas', 'laws of nature', and 'natural phenomena'. Alice v. CLS Bank (2014) established a two-step test: (1) is the claim directed to a judicial exception? (2) if yes, does it contain an 'inventive concept' transforming it into a patent-eligible application? Has invalidated thousands of software and business-method patents. Heavily criticised as unworkable; Congress repeatedly proposed legislative fixes through 2024.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Once a patent owner sells a patented product, they lose patent rights to control further use or resale of that item.
detail: Quanta Computer v. LG Electronics (2008) restated the doctrine; Impression Products v. Lexmark International (2017) held that even sales with contractual post-sale restrictions exhaust patent rights. International sales also trigger exhaustion (Kirtsaeng for copyright was extended to patents). Major limit on patent-holders' downstream control. Service-and-maintenance restrictions and licensing structures often replace direct sale to avoid full exhaustion.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: The back-and-forth between an inventor's lawyer and the USPTO examiner to get a patent issued.
detail: Begins with filing the patent application; typically takes 2-4 years. Office actions raise rejections (lack of novelty under § 102, obviousness under § 103, indefiniteness under § 112). Applicant responds with claim amendments, arguments, declarations. Rejections can be appealed to the Patent Trial and Appeal Board. Prosecution history later constrains claim interpretation in litigation under the doctrine of prosecution-history estoppel.

snappy: A company that acquires patents and licences or sues but doesn't make products — controversial business model.
detail: Pejorative term coined in the early 2000s; the patent owner usually prefers 'NPE' (non-practising entity) or 'patent assertion entity'. Mass-licensing campaigns and forum-shopping (especially in the Eastern District of Texas pre-TC Heartland, 2017) drew complaints. America Invents Act (2011), Alice (2014), and IPR proceedings created tools to push back. Critics say trolls drag innovation; defenders argue they monetise legitimate inventions.

snappy: What you can patent at all — limits set by 35 USC § 101.
detail: Statutory categories: processes, machines, manufactures, compositions of matter. Excluded: laws of nature, natural phenomena, abstract ideas. The Supreme Court trilogy of Mayo (2012), Myriad (2013), and Alice (2014) significantly narrowed software and biotech patent eligibility, especially around diagnostic methods and isolated DNA. Software patents now face a two-step Alice/Mayo analysis; many issued patents fall on motion practice.

snappy: Legally establishing a man as the father of a child.
detail: Three main paths: presumption (married to mother at birth or within 300 days of birth), acknowledgment of paternity (voluntary signed declaration), court order. Genetic testing routine in disputed cases. Establishment triggers: child-support obligation, custody/visitation rights, inheritance rights, eligibility for father's benefits. 'De facto' paternity doctrines (Marvin v. Marvin in California's parental context) recognise non-biological parental relationships in some contexts.

snappy: Antitrust conduct so harmful it's automatically illegal — no need to prove anti-competitive effect.
detail: Categories: horizontal price-fixing, market division between competitors, bid-rigging, group boycotts among horizontal competitors. Strict liability — pro-competitive justifications irrelevant. Criminal prosecution available for hardcore cartel conduct. Categories have shrunk over time: maximum-RPM and minimum-RPM both moved to rule of reason (State Oil v. Khan, 1997; Leegin v. PSKS, 2007). Modern enforcement: international cartels in auto parts, capacitors, generic drugs, foreign-exchange trading.

snappy: A strike to remove a prospective juror without giving any reason — limited number per side.
detail: Each side has a fixed allotment under Federal Rule 24 (criminal: 6–20 depending on charge) and 47 (civil: 3 per side). Batson v. Kentucky (1986) and progeny forbid using peremptories on race grounds; J.E.B. v. Alabama (1994) extended to sex. The challenged party must articulate a race-neutral reason — a standard widely criticised as too easy to game. Some scholars and judges have proposed eliminating peremptories.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Under UCC § 2-601, the buyer can reject goods that fail in any respect to conform to the contract.
detail: Buyer not limited to material non-conformity (as under common-law substantial performance). Even minor defects allow rejection. Counterbalances: cure rights (§ 2-508), installment-contract exception, and good-faith requirement (§ 1-304). Practical consequence: sellers must deliver exactly as ordered. Doctrine criticised as harsh but defended as protective of buyer expectations in goods sales.

snappy: Collectives (ASCAP, BMI, SESAC, GMR) that licence music for public performance — radio, TV, restaurants, venues.
detail: PROs negotiate blanket licences with users, collect fees, and distribute royalties to songwriters and publishers. ASCAP and BMI are subject to longstanding antitrust consent decrees governing their licensing practices. SESAC and GMR aren't. The Music Modernization Act (2018) and related litigation reshaped digital royalty flows. Rate setting for radio and TV uses occurs in dedicated 'rate court' proceedings before federal district judges.

snappy: A rental that automatically renews period-by-period (month-to-month, year-to-year) until properly terminated.
detail: Default form when a tenant remains after a fixed-term lease expires with landlord consent. Termination requires notice equal to the period (one month's notice for month-to-month). Distinguished from term tenancies (fixed end date) and tenancies at will. Most state landlord-tenant codes set minimum notice periods that can exceed common-law defaults. Common arrangement for residential rentals after the first year.

snappy: Lying under oath about something material to the proceeding — a federal felony.
detail: 18 U.S.C. § 1621. Requires wilful false testimony about a material fact. 'Literal truth' is a defence even if misleading (Bronston v. United States, 1973). Difficult to prosecute: must prove the speaker knew the statement was false at the time, and materiality is often contestable. Famous perjury prosecutions: Bill Clinton (settled with admonishment), Scooter Libby (2007), Roger Clemens (acquitted, 2012). Suborning perjury (18 U.S.C. § 1622) targets those who induce the lie.

snappy: A non-citizen authorised to live and work permanently in the US — also called green card holder.
detail: Acquired through immigrant visa or adjustment of status. Number issued annually capped at about 1 million (variable). Family preferences: spouse, parents, children (with subcategories). Employment preferences: EB-1 (priority workers), EB-2 (advanced degree), EB-3 (skilled), EB-4 (special), EB-5 (investor). Backlogs in some categories run decades for Indian and Chinese nationals. Abandonment, criminal convictions, fraud, or extended absence can lead to loss of status.

snappy: Whether a court has power over a specific defendant — typically based on their connections to the state.
detail: Two flavours: general (defendant is 'at home' in the state) and specific (the suit arises from defendant's contacts there). International Shoe v. Washington (1945) launched the 'minimum contacts' analysis. Recent restrictions: Bristol-Myers Squibb (2017) limited mass-tort jurisdiction; Ford v. Montana (2021) reframed specific-jurisdiction analysis. Internet contacts and stream-of-commerce theories still hotly debated.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Where the child lives — can be sole (with one parent) or shared (split between parents).
detail: Determines who has the child on which days. Visitation orders specify schedule when not joint. Modern shared-parenting orders detail: weekday schedule, weekend schedule, summer/holiday/birthday arrangements, exchange procedures, transportation responsibility. Conflicts often around: holiday rotation, summer vacation length, religious observance, travel and relocation. Mediation often used to develop and update parenting plans.

snappy: Officers lawfully in a place can seize evidence in plain view without a separate warrant.
detail: Requires (1) lawful presence, (2) lawful access to the item, and (3) immediately apparent criminal character. Horton v. California (1990) eliminated the older 'inadvertent discovery' requirement. Common application: officer responding to a domestic call sees drugs on the table. The doctrine extends to plain smell (drug odour) and plain hearing (incriminating statements). Doesn't authorise prolonged search to find more evidence.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: The party who files a civil lawsuit — the side asking the court for relief.
detail: Plaintiffs bear the burden of proof in civil cases: typically a 'preponderance of the evidence', which means more likely than not. The plaintiff drafts the complaint, names the defendants, and chooses the venue (subject to jurisdiction rules). In criminal cases the equivalent role is filled by the state or federal prosecutor, not a private plaintiff.

snappy: The Chapter 11 debtor's plan to repay creditors and restructure operations.
detail: Sections typically: classification of claims, treatment of each class, means of execution (operating cash flow, asset sales, new financing), retention of management, post-confirmation operations. Disclosure statement accompanies plan with information for creditor voting. Plan confirmation requires majority approval per class (in numbers and at least 2/3 in dollars) or cramdown over dissenting classes. 'Absolute priority rule' requires senior classes paid in full before junior receive value (unless senior consents).

snappy: A specialty patent for new varieties of asexually reproduced plants.
detail: 35 U.S.C. § 161. 20-year term. Limited to plants reproduced by cutting, grafting, or other asexual means — not by seed. Most modern plant innovations are protected by utility patents (covering more, including sexually reproduced plants) or the Plant Variety Protection Act. Granted to plant breeders and nurseries; common for ornamental varieties, fruit trees, and proprietary cultivars.

snappy: An agreement where the defendant pleads guilty in exchange for reduced charges or sentence.
detail: Resolves 90%+ of criminal cases in the US — only a small fraction go to trial. Variants: charge bargaining (plead to a lesser offence), sentence bargaining (plead in exchange for a sentence recommendation), fact bargaining (stipulate to specific facts). Critics argue plea bargaining coerces guilty pleas from innocent defendants facing severe trial penalties; defenders cite efficiency and finality.

snappy: A defensive mechanism allowing existing shareholders to buy shares at a discount if a hostile bidder crosses an ownership threshold.
detail: Triggered by acquirer reaching specified ownership (typically 10-15%). Massive dilution makes acquisition prohibitively expensive without board cooperation. Moran v. Household International (Del. 1985) upheld; Unocal (Del. 1985) and Revlon (Del. 1986) shaped doctrine. Modern variations: 'NOL pills' to protect tax assets, 'rights plans' triggered against activists, pandemic-era pills used to defend against opportunistic accumulation.

snappy: Some constitutional questions are committed to the political branches — not for courts to decide.
detail: Baker v. Carr (1962) listed factors: textual commitment to other branch, lack of judicial standards, need for non-judicial discretion, impossibility of resolving without offending other branches. Modern applications: foreign affairs (Zivotofsky v. Clinton, 2012 — passport designation), gerrymandering (Rucho v. Common Cause, 2019 — partisan gerrymanders nonjusticiable). Tensions with judicial review broadly; doctrine has narrowed over time.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Holding drugs in quantities or with packaging suggesting plans to sell — much more serious than simple possession.
detail: 21 U.S.C. § 841(a)(1). Intent often inferred from quantity, packaging materials, scales, large cash holdings, or text messages. Federal mandatory minimums kick in at threshold amounts that vary by drug (heroin, cocaine, methamphetamine, fentanyl). The Fair Sentencing Act (2010) and First Step Act (2018) reduced crack-cocaine disparities. Sentencing guidelines drive most outcomes — quantities can produce life sentences for repeat offenders.

snappy: A contract signed after marriage addressing property and support rights upon divorce or death.
detail: Less established than prenups; enforceability varies more by state. Requirements typically: voluntary, full disclosure, fair terms, sometimes independent counsel. Common triggers: reconciliation after near-divorce, business creation during marriage, inheritance receipt, infidelity. Recognised broadly but scrutinised heavily — courts give less deference than to prenups because of the marital fiduciary relationship.

snappy: A legal document letting one person act on another's behalf — financial decisions, healthcare, legal matters.
detail: Two main types: general (broad authority — financial transactions, real estate, banking) and limited (specific purpose — sell specific property, sign tax return). Durable POA: continues if principal becomes incapacitated. Springing POA: takes effect only on specified event (typically incapacity). Healthcare POA (or proxy) handles medical decisions. Routine in elder-care planning, business operations during absence, and capacity-incidence preparation. Revocable any time while competent.

snappy: Trustee's recovery of payments debtor made to creditors shortly before bankruptcy — restoring them to the estate.
detail: 11 U.S.C. § 547. Recovers transfers within 90 days of filing (or 1 year for insiders) made to creditors holding antecedent debt, while debtor was insolvent. Defences: contemporaneous exchange for new value, ordinary course of business, new value provided after transfer. Major issue for vendors who collected on past-due invoices from struggling customers. Substantial litigation source — preference recoveries can be material in larger cases.

snappy: A class of corporate stock with priority over common stock in dividends and liquidation — but usually limited voting rights.
detail: Modern preferred stock highly customised: dividend rate (fixed, floating, participating), conversion rights, liquidation preferences (1x, 2x, participating, capped), redemption rights, anti-dilution adjustments, voting rights (typically only on adverse changes). Venture capital uses preferred stock universally — series A/B/C/D preferred with increasing seniority. Public preferred typically used by banks and other financial institutions for regulatory capital.

snappy: 1978 amendment to Title VII making it illegal to discriminate based on pregnancy, childbirth, or related medical conditions.
detail: 42 U.S.C. § 2000e(k). Treats pregnancy like any other temporary medical condition for accommodation purposes. Young v. UPS (2015) clarified pregnant employees can claim discrimination if their employer accommodates other similarly limited workers. Pregnant Workers Fairness Act (2022) expanded — requires reasonable accommodations for known pregnancy-related limitations unless undue hardship. PUMP Act (2022) expanded breastfeeding accommodations. Active growing area of enforcement and litigation.

snappy: A pretrial proceeding where a judge decides whether probable cause exists to hold the defendant for trial.
detail: Held within days or weeks of arrest. Prosecutor presents enough evidence to show probable cause; defendant can cross-examine but rarely puts on full defence. Lower standard than trial: evidence in light most favourable to prosecution. Federal Rule of Criminal Procedure 5.1. Dismissal at preliminary hearing doesn't bar later refiling. Often called 'prelim' or 'PX'. Many defendants waive to focus on plea negotiations.

snappy: Property owner's duty to keep their property reasonably safe for visitors — duty depends on visitor's status.
detail: Three traditional categories: invitees (business visitors, customers — highest duty: discover and warn of hazards), licensees (social guests — moderate duty: warn of known hazards), trespassers (limited duty: only refrain from wilful injury). Many states have merged into a unified reasonable-care standard. Big retailers face frequent slip-and-fall litigation; comparative fault commonly mitigates exposure.

snappy: A contract signed before marriage setting out property and support rights upon divorce or death.
detail: Enforceable in most states with proper formalities: voluntary, full financial disclosure, fair terms, independent counsel for each party (recommended), execution well before wedding (avoid duress). Uniform Premarital Agreement Act adopted in roughly half of states. Common provisions: property division, alimony waiver, treatment of separate property, inheritance rights. Can be voided for unconscionability or duress. Increasingly common across income levels, not just for high-net-worth marriages.

snappy: Everything publicly known before a patent's filing date — used to attack novelty.
detail: Includes earlier patents, journal articles, conference papers, product launches, and even oral disclosures. A patent must be novel over the prior art and non-obvious to a person of ordinary skill. Patent prosecution is largely a fight over what counts as prior art and what it teaches. Inter partes review (IPR) at the USPTO has become a popular forum for accused infringers to challenge patents on prior-art grounds.

snappy: Government action that bans speech before it occurs — presumptively unconstitutional under the First Amendment.
detail: Near v. Minnesota (1931) established the doctrine; New York Times v. United States (1971, the Pentagon Papers case) confirmed strong presumption against. Exceptions are narrow: troop movements in wartime, obscene material in court orders, child pornography. Modern variations: gag orders in high-profile cases, sealed grand-jury proceedings, national-security classified information. Government licensing schemes for protests typically must include precise standards and prompt judicial review.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Certain unsecured claims paid before others — domestic support, employee wages (limited), taxes (limited).
detail: 11 U.S.C. § 507 lists priority levels: domestic support obligations first, then administrative expenses, then employee wages (up to limit), then employee benefit-plan contributions, then certain taxes.
