Must be paid in full before lower-priority claims receive anything in Chapter 7 distribution. Must be paid in full over plan term in Chapter 11 confirmable plan (subject to limited exceptions). Often determines whether plan is feasible.

snappy: A securities offering to a limited number of sophisticated investors — exempt from full SEC registration.
detail: Regulation D safe harbours (Rules 504, 506(b), 506(c)) the most common path. Limits on number of non-accredited investors. General solicitation allowed under Rule 506(c) with investor-accreditation verification. Form D filing required. Restricted securities — typically locked up under Rule 144 for one year. PIPE (private investment in public equity) transactions are major liquidity tool for already-public companies.

snappy: An itemised list of documents withheld from discovery on privilege grounds.
detail: Required by Federal Rule 26(b)(5) whenever a party refuses to produce documents claiming attorney-client privilege or work product. Each entry shows date, author, recipients, subject, and privilege basis — without revealing the protected content. Sloppy logs (vague descriptions, missing entries) routinely lead to privilege waivers. Tens of thousands of log entries are common in major commercial cases.

snappy: Fourteenth Amendment clause protecting rights of national citizenship — gutted by the Slaughter-House Cases (1873).
detail: Originally intended (by some) to be a major vehicle for incorporating the Bill of Rights against states. Slaughter-House Cases narrowly construed it almost out of existence. Briefly revived in McDonald v. Chicago (2010) — Justice Thomas concurrence relied on it for Second Amendment incorporation. Scholars across the political spectrum debate its proper scope. Largely dormant despite occasional revival proposals.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Reasonable belief based on facts that a crime occurred or that specific evidence will be found.
detail: Lower standard than 'beyond a reasonable doubt' but more than mere suspicion. Required for arrests, search warrants, and indictments. Probable-cause hearings (preliminary hearings) screen out weak cases between charging and trial in some jurisdictions. Police can also act on 'reasonable suspicion' for brief Terry stops — even lower threshold than probable cause.

snappy: The court process for administering a deceased person's estate — paying debts, distributing assets per will or intestacy law.
detail: Steps: filing will and death certificate, appointing executor/administrator, inventory of assets, paying debts and taxes, distributing remainder. Time and cost vary enormously: simple estates 6-12 months, $500-3,000; complex contested probates can take years and cost tens of thousands. Probate avoidance strategies: revocable living trust, joint tenancy with right of survivorship, beneficiary designations (POD/TOD accounts, life insurance, retirement accounts). State-specific small-estate procedures for low-asset cases.

snappy: Court-imposed supervision in the community instead of jail or prison.
detail: Conditions typically include reporting to a probation officer, maintaining employment, no contact with co-defendants/victims, no new offences, drug testing. Violation can result in revocation and incarceration. Distinct from parole, which is supervised release after serving prison time. Roughly 3.5 million Americans are on probation at any given moment — outnumbering those in prison.

snappy: Government must use fair procedures before depriving someone of life, liberty, or property.
detail: Mathews v. Eldridge (1976) three-factor balancing: private interest affected, risk of erroneous deprivation, government's interest. Required procedures vary widely: full hearings for criminal proceedings, modest pre-deprivation procedures for benefits, post-deprivation hearings for some property interests. Goldberg v. Kelly (1970) required pre-termination hearings for welfare. Cleveland Board of Education v. Loudermill (1985) extended to public employment.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: The bargaining process was unfair — high-pressure tactics, hidden terms, take-it-or-leave-it adhesion.
detail: First prong of unconscionability analysis (with substantive unconscionability). Factors: relative bargaining power, sophistication, information asymmetries, time pressure, language barriers, surprise terms. Adhesion contracts inherently raise procedural concerns. Most jurisdictions require some procedural unconscionability before invalidating terms — but the showing can be modest if substantive unconscionability is severe (sliding-scale approach).

snappy: A promise can be enforced even without consideration, if the other side reasonably relied on it.
detail: Restatement § 90: a promise the promisor should reasonably expect to induce reliance, that does induce reliance, is binding if injustice can be avoided only by enforcement. Famous case: Hoffman v. Red Owl Stores (1965) — a grocery franchisor strung a prospective franchisee along, who sold his bakery and moved towns. No final contract existed, but the franchisor's preliminary promises were enforced. Reliance damages, not expectation, are the usual remedy.

snappy: The borrower's written promise to repay a debt — the actual IOU in real-estate finance.
detail: Article 3 of the UCC governs negotiable promissory notes. In a mortgage transaction, the note is the debt obligation; the mortgage (or deed of trust) is the security for that debt. The two are separately enforceable but typically transferred together. Standardised forms (Fannie Mae / Freddie Mac) dominate residential mortgages. Holder-in-due-course doctrine gives downstream note holders enhanced rights.

snappy: A written statement by a creditor of how much the debtor owes them — filed in the bankruptcy case.
detail: Official Form 410. Includes: claim amount, basis (loan, lease, services), priority status (secured, priority unsecured, general unsecured), supporting documentation. Bar date: deadline to file (typically 70 days from § 341 meeting in Chapter 7, court-set in Chapter 11). Late-filed claims often disallowed. Objection process: debtor or trustee challenges claim amount or status; creditor may respond. Allowed claims share in distribution per priority rules.

snappy: A court order restricting what can be done with discovery materials — e.g., kept confidential, used only for this case.
detail: Federal Rule 26(c). Essential in commercial cases involving trade secrets, customer data, or sensitive financials. Common provisions: 'attorneys' eyes only' tiers, sealed filings, return or destruction at case end. Also used in family law and harassment cases to shield sensitive personal information. Courts balance public's right of access against parties' legitimate confidentiality interests — In re Search Warrant (D.C. Cir.) framework.

snappy: A simpler placeholder filing that secures a US filing date but never matures into a patent on its own.
detail: 12-month pendency — the applicant must file a non-provisional (or PCT) application within a year claiming priority back to the provisional. Often used to lock in priority before completing the full specification. No examination, no claims required, lower fees. Common before a launch or investor pitch. Foreign priority benefits under the Paris Convention also tied to provisional filing date.

snappy: Whether the harm was a foreseeable consequence of the defendant's act — limits liability to nearby effects.
detail: Distinct from cause-in-fact (the 'but-for' test): defendant's act actually caused harm. Proximate cause asks the next question: is it fair to hold defendant responsible for this particular result? Wagon Mound (1961) made foreseeability the dominant test. Bridge cases like Palsgraf (1928) and the Polemis line wrestle with how unforeseeable a consequence has to be before liability cuts off.

snappy: Disclosure document sent to shareholders before a vote — board nominees, executive compensation, governance matters.
detail: Schedule 14A filed with SEC. Annual proxy statement major sections: director nominees, executive compensation (CD&A), audit committee report, related-party transactions. Say-on-pay vote required by Dodd-Frank. Shareholder proposals under Rule 14a-8 must be included if procedural and substantive thresholds met. Proxy contests (activist nominations) follow special procedures. Universal proxy card (effective 2022) lets shareholders pick from both slates.

snappy: A government-funded lawyer who represents defendants who can't afford counsel.
detail: Required after Gideon v. Wainwright (1963). Funded by state and county budgets; chronically underfunded. The American Bar Association recommends maximum annual caseloads (e.g., 150 felonies per attorney) routinely exceeded by 2-3x. Federal public defenders operate under the Criminal Justice Act of 1964. The 'meet 'em and plead 'em' criticism reflects systemic resource shortages — defendants often see counsel for the first time at arraignment.

snappy: Publicising truthful information about someone's private life in a way highly offensive and not of legitimate public concern.
detail: Restatement § 652D. The truth defence doesn't help defendants — that's exactly what this tort targets. Tension with First Amendment: legitimate public interest defeats the claim. Florida Star v. B.J.F. (1989) limited liability for publication of lawfully obtained public-record information. Revenge porn statutes increasingly fill the gap. Famous Hulk Hogan v. Gawker case (2016) yielded $140M verdict; settled in bankruptcy.

snappy: Works free for anyone to use — no copyright owner, no permission needed.
detail: Works enter the public domain when copyright expires, when no rights ever attached (US government works), or when the author dedicates them. As of 2024, US works published before 1929 are public-domain; Sonny Bono Term Extension Act (1998) froze the clock for two decades. Steamboat Willie's Mickey Mouse joined the public domain January 1, 2024.

snappy: A person who has achieved general fame or notoriety — must prove actual malice in defamation suits.
detail: Gertz v. Welch (1974) recognised two categories: general-purpose (Hollywood star, U.S. senator) and limited-purpose (well-known in a specific controversy). The category determines whether actual malice applies. Pervasive-power-and-influence figures (politicians, A-list celebrities) clearly qualify; intermittently famous people are case-specific. Streisand-effect dynamics make defamation cases especially fraught for public-figure plaintiffs.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Different categories of government property get different levels of speech protection — traditional, designated, limited, and non-public forums.
detail: Traditional public forums (streets, parks, sidewalks): content-based restrictions get strict scrutiny. Designated public forums: similar protection for the period government has opened the property. Limited public forums (school buildings opened to outside groups): content discrimination must be reasonable. Non-public forums (military bases, jails): only reasonableness review. Public-employee workplaces and online government-controlled forums raise modern complications.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Contracts violating fundamental social policy are unenforceable — even when not strictly illegal.
detail: Restatement (Second) §§ 178-179. Common applications: agreements to commit torts, perjury, marriage-restraint clauses, contracts impairing family-relations duties, restrictive covenants exceeding reasonable scope. Courts weigh contractual interest against public policy. The doctrine grows and shrinks with social attitudes — once enforced surrogate-mother contracts and segregation covenants are now void. Modern flashpoints: non-competes, mandatory arbitration in consumer contexts.

snappy: Money awarded to punish defendants and deter future misconduct, on top of actual losses.
detail: Available only for conduct beyond ordinary negligence — typically intentional wrongs, fraud, or recklessness. The Supreme Court limits punitives constitutionally: usually no more than 9× compensatory damages (BMW v. Gore, State Farm v. Campbell). Some states cap them by statute. Famously high awards (the McDonald's coffee case, 1994) are often reduced on appeal — the McDonald's punitives were cut from $2.7M to $480K.

snappy: The signed contract between buyer and seller setting the terms of a real-estate sale.
detail: Includes price, deposit (earnest money), financing contingency, inspection contingency, closing date, and seller representations. State association forms dominate residential transactions. Commercial transactions use heavily negotiated custom agreements covering due diligence, title, environmental, lease assignments, etc. Statute of Frauds requires real-estate contracts to be in writing. Termination during contingency periods typically returns the deposit.

snappy: Generally, you can't recover for purely financial losses in a tort claim — must show physical injury or property damage.
detail: Channels purely economic losses into contract law. Major exceptions: negligent misrepresentation, professional negligence (lawyers, accountants), product liability for failure of product itself. Rationale: prevent unlimited liability ripples through commercial chains. Robins Dry Dock v. Flint (1927) is the seminal authority. Heavily criticised but durably entrenched in most US jurisdictions.

snappy: A state database where men can register potential paternity claims to receive notice of adoption proceedings.
detail: Allows unmarried men who believe they may have fathered a child to receive notice before child's adoption. Required in many states for unmarried biological fathers to protect parental rights. Strict deadlines: typically must register within 30 days of birth or before adoption petition filed. Failure to timely register can foreclose parental-rights claim in adoption — even if man genuinely didn't know about pregnancy. Constitutional challenges based on Lehr v. Robertson (1983) framework largely unsuccessful.

snappy: Government officials are shielded from civil suit unless they violated 'clearly established' law a reasonable officer would have known.
detail: Harlow v. Fitzgerald (1982). Major flashpoint in police-misconduct cases. Courts can grant immunity without deciding whether the conduct was constitutional (Pearson v. Callahan, 2009). 'Clearly established' often interpreted narrowly — requires a closely analogous prior case. Repeatedly criticised by judges across the spectrum. Repeated legislative reform attempts have failed in Congress through 2024; some states have narrowed for state-officer claims.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Recovery of the reasonable value of services rendered when no enforceable contract exists.
detail: Available in quasi-contract claims and as fallback when a contract is unenforceable (e.g., violates statute of frauds, lacks mutual assent on price). Plaintiff proves services were rendered, the defendant was unjustly enriched, and recovery is based on market value — not the parties' (failed) agreed price. Common in construction disputes where work proceeded without a final signed contract.

snappy: Not a real contract — an equitable remedy preventing unjust enrichment.
detail: Also called 'contract implied in law'. Imposed by courts when one party benefits at another's expense and it'd be unjust to let them keep the benefit. The classic example: doctor renders emergency care to unconscious patient — no contract, but the patient is liable for reasonable medical costs. Damages are 'restitution' — the value of the benefit conferred, not expectation damages.

snappy: When job benefits are conditioned on submission to sexual or other harassment.
detail: Title VII theory of sexual or other harassment. Examples: 'sleep with me or you're fired', promotion contingent on dating supervisor. Employer strictly liable when supervisor's conduct results in tangible employment action (firing, demotion, reassignment) — no affirmative defence available. Burlington Industries v. Ellerth (1998) drew this line. Often clearer to prove than hostile-environment claims; one explicit conditioning incident usually suffices.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: A deed that transfers whatever interest the grantor has — without any warranty that the title is good.
detail: If the grantor owns nothing, the grantee gets nothing — no remedy. Used to clear up title clouds (divorcing spouses transferring marital property), correct earlier errors, or transfer property among family members where warranties don't matter. Title insurance generally won't write coverage on quitclaim transactions. Buyers in arm's-length deals should never accept a quitclaim deed without independent title verification.

snappy: The minimum number of shareholders (or directors) needed for a valid meeting — usually majority.
detail: Default in DGCL § 216: majority of voting power for shareholder meetings; bylaws can set higher or lower (within statutory minimums). For boards: majority of total directors (DGCL § 141(b)). Failure to maintain quorum invalidates actions taken. Modern meetings: virtual attendance counts in most states; bylaws often address remote-participation procedures. Adjournments are used to gather absent shareholders for re-vote.

snappy: The deferential standard: government law must merely be 'rationally related to a legitimate government interest'.
detail: Default for most equal-protection and substantive-due-process claims. Almost always upholds the law — rare exceptions (Romer v. Evans, 1996, striking down a Colorado anti-gay-rights amendment as based on 'animus'). The 'rational basis with bite' label captures these occasional unexpected applications. Government doesn't even need to articulate the actual rationale; any conceivable legitimate purpose suffices.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Land and anything permanently attached — buildings, fixtures, trees, mineral rights.
detail: Distinguished from personal property ('chattels'), which is movable. Real estate includes airspace (subject to FAA limits) and subsurface rights (mineral, water). Property law historically split into estates: fee simple (full ownership, the default for buyers), life estate (ownership for someone's lifetime), and remainder (whoever takes after). Modern conveyances usually deal in fee simple unless an estate plan does otherwise.

snappy: A licensed professional helping buyers and sellers, paid by commission (usually 5-6% of sale price, split with buyer's agent).
detail: Brokers (and their sales agents) are licensed by states with continuing-education requirements. Fiduciary duties run to their clients. 'Dual agency' (representing both buyer and seller) requires disclosure and consent. National Association of Realtors' 2024 commission-structure settlement is reshaping how buyer-broker compensation is negotiated. State licensing law violations can result in fines, suspension, and licence revocation.

snappy: The final meeting where the buyer pays, signs documents, and takes title to the property.
detail: Federally regulated by the Real Estate Settlement Procedures Act (RESPA) and the TILA-RESPA Integrated Disclosure rule (TRID). Closing disclosure must be delivered three business days before closing. Common participants: buyer, seller, real-estate agents, lender, title agent or attorney. Funds typically wire-transferred; documents recorded same day or shortly after. 'Wire fraud' targeting closing transfers is a serious and growing problem.

snappy: Adjusting work conditions to enable an employee with a disability or religious need to perform the job — unless doing so causes undue hardship.
detail: ADA Title I and Title VII (religion). Common accommodations: schedule changes, modified duties, assistive technology, accessible workspaces, remote work. Interactive-process duty: employer must engage in dialogue to identify accommodation. Undue hardship: significant difficulty or expense. Groff v. DeJoy (2023) raised the bar for undue-hardship religious-accommodation defence — must be 'substantial' burden, not de minimis. EEOC enforcement and private litigation routinely contested.

snappy: To claim trade-secret protection, the owner must have taken reasonable steps to keep the information secret.
detail: Listed factors typically include: NDAs with employees and contractors, password protection, restricted physical access, marking documents 'confidential', exit interviews, network monitoring, document destruction. The standard is 'reasonable under the circumstances' — not absolute secrecy. Failure to take steps loses trade-secret status; widely-shared 'secrets' aren't protected. Defendants regularly attack this element by showing lax security practices.

snappy: Knowingly buying or accepting goods you know were stolen.
detail: Requires the property to be stolen, the recipient to know (or have reason to know) it's stolen, and acceptance with intent to deprive the owner. Federal statute (18 U.S.C. § 2315) requires interstate transport. Charged against fences, pawnshops, and buyers of obvious bargains (the 'too-good-to-be-true' truck-stop laptop). Some states automatically infer knowledge from price disparity or possession of recently stolen goods.

snappy: State law setting the rules for which competing claimants to land win — usually the first to file with the county recorder.
detail: Three regimes: race (first to record wins), notice (later bona fide purchaser without notice wins), and race-notice (later BFP wins only if they record first). Most states are race-notice. The recording office (county recorder's or registrar's) maintains the chain-of-title index. Failure to record doesn't invalidate the deed between parties but exposes the buyer to losing the property to a later good-faith purchaser.

snappy: Layoffs for business reasons — distinguishable from individual terminations for cause.
detail: Different employment-law analysis: discrimination claims focus on whether protected-class members disproportionately laid off. Statistical adverse-impact analysis common in major RIFs. Best practices: written selection criteria, manager-by-manager validation, statistical review of demographic impact, severance agreements with ADEA-compliant releases. Older Workers Benefit Protection Act requires specific disclosures for group layoffs (job titles, ages of affected and retained employees). WARN Act notice obligations for large RIFs.

snappy: Court rewriting the contract to reflect what the parties actually agreed.
detail: Equitable remedy for scrivener's errors, mutual mistake about a term, or fraud as to the writing. The court fixes the writing to match the original intent. Parol evidence rule is suspended for reformation actions — extrinsic evidence is essential to prove what the parties actually meant. Rare in commercial disputes but common in real-estate deeds with boundary errors.

snappy: Rules issued by executive-branch agencies to implement statutes.
detail: Distinct from statutes (passed by legislature) and case law (issued by courts). Federal regulations are codified in the Code of Federal Regulations (CFR); proposed and finalised through notice-and-comment in the Federal Register. State-level regulatory bodies operate similarly under state administrative-procedure acts. After Loper Bright (2024), courts no longer defer to agencies' interpretations of ambiguous statutes — major shift in administrative law.

snappy: SEC safe harbours for private offerings exempt from registration under Section 4(a)(2) of the Securities Act.
detail: Rule 504: up to $10M to any investors. Rule 506(b): unlimited to accredited investors, up to 35 sophisticated non-accredited; no general solicitation. Rule 506(c): unlimited to verified accredited investors; general solicitation allowed. Form D notice filing required within 15 days of first sale. State 'blue sky' filings often parallel (with Reg D preemption protecting Rule 506 offerings). Workhorse of US private capital formation.

snappy: Government regulation so severe it effectively takes private property — requires just compensation under the Fifth Amendment.
detail: Penn Central Transportation v. New York City (1978) sets the framework — character of government action, economic impact, interference with investment-backed expectations. Lucas v. South Carolina Coastal Council (1992) — total deprivation of economic use is per se taking. Murr v. Wisconsin (2017) refused to treat adjacent parcels as a single unit for takings analysis. Frequent litigation in zoning, environmental, and historic-preservation contexts.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Time-limited alimony designed to let recipient become self-supporting — through education, training, or career re-entry.
detail: Most common alimony form in modern divorces. Typically 2-5 years. Tied to specific plan: complete degree, obtain certification, build practice, transition to full-time employment. Court can extend or modify if rehabilitation efforts fail despite good faith. Trend toward presumptive durations and end dates by statute (Massachusetts Alimony Reform Act 2011; similar New Jersey reform 2014).

snappy: Evidence is relevant if it makes some fact at issue more or less likely — the basic threshold for admission.
detail: Federal Rule of Evidence 401–402. A very low bar — any tendency to alter probability qualifies. Relevance is necessary but not sufficient; Rule 403 lets a judge exclude even relevant evidence if its prejudicial effect substantially outweighs probative value. Prior-bad-acts evidence and graphic injury photos are common Rule 403 battlegrounds. Conditional relevance under Rule 104(b) lets evidence in subject to later proof.

snappy: Money compensating for expenses incurred in reliance on the contract — put plaintiff back where they started.
detail: Alternative when expectation damages are too speculative. Available in promissory estoppel cases and as a fallback when expectation is unprovable. Hoffman v. Red Owl (1965) is the classic application. Capped at the contract price in some jurisdictions — plaintiff can't end up better off than performance would have left them.

snappy: Employers must accommodate workers' sincerely held religious beliefs unless it imposes undue hardship.
detail: Title VII duty. Sincerely held doesn't require formal religious affiliation. Common accommodations: schedule flexibility for sabbath observance, religious attire, time off for holidays, modified job duties to avoid conflicts. Groff v. DeJoy (2023) raised undue-hardship standard from minimal (Hardison, 1977) to substantial. EEOC enforcement and rising state legislation. Vaccine-related accommodation requests surged during pandemic.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: A future interest that takes effect immediately when a prior life estate or term ends.
detail: Created in someone other than the grantor (a reversion goes back to the grantor). Vested remainders (no condition precedent, holder identified) transfer freely; contingent remainders (subject to condition) can fail or be defeated. The Rule Against Perpetuities historically limited certain contingent remainders. Common in trust language: 'income to A for life, remainder to A's surviving children'.

snappy: A defendant's right to move a case from state court to federal court.
detail: 28 U.S.C. §§ 1441–1446. Removable cases must satisfy federal jurisdiction (diversity or federal question). Generally must be removed within 30 days of service. Only defendants can remove; all must join. After removal, plaintiff can challenge with a motion to remand. Class Action Fairness Act and the Bankruptcy Code provide special removal mechanisms. A favoured tool for corporate defendants escaping plaintiff-friendly state forums.

snappy: Immigration-court process to determine whether a non-citizen should be removed from the US.
detail: Conducted by Immigration Judges (DOJ EOIR). Adversarial proceeding: ICE attorneys represent government, respondent represents self or hires counsel. No right to government-provided counsel (only right to counsel at one's own expense). Initial 'Master Calendar' hearings on identity, charges, eligibility for relief. Merits hearings on relief applications. Appeals to Board of Immigration Appeals, then federal Court of Appeals (limited review).

snappy: The constitutional status of abortion and contraception — sharply altered by Dobbs v. Jackson (2022).
detail: Roe v. Wade (1973) recognised constitutional right to abortion via substantive due process; Planned Parenthood v. Casey (1992) replaced trimester framework with 'undue burden' test. Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization (2022) overruled Roe and Casey, returning abortion regulation to states. Result: dramatic state-by-state divergence — 14 states ban most abortions; many others extended protections by statute or ballot. Federal action limited to FDA regulation and travel/funding restrictions.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: A clear statement or act showing one party won't perform.
detail: Must be unequivocal — saying 'I might not perform' isn't enough. Courts have grown more permissive with electronic communications: a clear email refusing performance counts. The non-repudiating party may treat the contract as breached, sue immediately, suspend their own performance, or wait for the actual due date. Each path has different risk profiles.

snappy: A demand asking the other side to admit specified facts under oath — unanswered requests are deemed admitted.
detail: Federal Rule 36. Used to narrow the issues for trial: 'Admit that you signed this contract on 1 March 2024.' If a party fails to admit something later proved true, the requesting party can recover its proof costs. Powerful discipline tool for forcing concessions on undisputed facts so trial focuses on what actually matters.

snappy: A formal demand for documents, emails, or electronic data relevant to a lawsuit.
detail: Federal Rule 34 — the main vehicle for getting at the other side's records. Modern requests routinely cover email, Slack, text messages, cloud storage, and structured data. Disputes over scope, search terms, and custodians eat huge legal budgets. Producing party must search 'reasonably accessible' sources; rare data (backup tapes) requires cost-shifting analysis. Privilege logs accompany withheld documents.

snappy: A contract to buy all of buyer's needs — quantity determined by what the buyer actually requires.
detail: UCC § 2-306. Like output contracts, valid despite quantity indefiniteness. Good-faith requirement: buyer can't dramatically expand or contract requirements to game market moves. Common in industrial supply (automotive parts, oil refining inputs), aerospace, and construction. Famous historical example: HMI v. Trapani — courts apply good-faith analysis to disproportionate-quantity demands.

snappy: Lets a jury infer negligence from the very fact that an injury occurred when ordinarily it wouldn't without negligence.
detail: Classic case: Byrne v. Boadle (1863) — flour barrel falling from a warehouse. Modern requirements (Restatement § 328D): (1) accident type ordinarily doesn't occur without negligence, (2) instrumentality was in defendant's exclusive control, (3) no contribution by plaintiff. Common applications: surgical-instrument left in patient, falling objects, mid-air collisions. Shifts burden of explanation to the defendant.

snappy: Cancellation of the contract — both parties return to their pre-contract positions.
detail: Equitable remedy available for fraud, mutual mistake, misrepresentation, duress, undue influence, or material breach. Must be sought promptly; delay can waive the right. Restitution often accompanies rescission — each side returns what they received. Famously available in real-estate fraud, art-fraud, and securities cases.

snappy: Returning to plaintiff the value of any benefit conferred on defendant.
detail: Restitution recovery aims to disgorge unjust enrichment, not to compensate plaintiff's loss. Used as a contract remedy when the plaintiff doesn't want to enforce the deal (e.g., breaching party seeks return of partial payment). Also stand-alone via quasi-contract. Measured by the value of the benefit, which can exceed contract price in some scenarios.

snappy: A private contract restricting how property can be used — recorded in the deed, running with the land.
detail: Subdivision-wide covenants impose uniform restrictions (no clotheslines, paint-colour rules, fence specifications, building setbacks). Enforced by other lot owners or HOAs. Race-based covenants (excluding Black or Jewish buyers) ruled unenforceable in Shelley v. Kraemer (1948); still remain on many old deed records as historical relics. Modern covenants face occasional First Amendment, fair housing, and antitrust challenges.

snappy: Employer punishment of employees who oppose discrimination or participate in EEO investigations — itself an EEO violation.
detail: Title VII, ADA, ADEA, Equal Pay Act, FLSA all prohibit retaliation. Easier to prove than underlying discrimination — protected activity (complaint, EEOC charge, witness testimony), adverse action, causal connection. Burlington Northern v. White (2006) defined adverse action as anything that would 'dissuade a reasonable worker' from making complaints — broader than ultimate employment actions. Retaliation claims now exceed underlying discrimination claims in EEOC charge volume.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: An acquirer's subsidiary merges into the target — target survives as the acquirer's subsidiary.
detail: The most common M&A structure for public targets. Advantages: target's contracts typically survive (no assignment-consent issues for many), target keeps tax attributes, target's licences and permits unaffected. Triggers shareholder appraisal rights for target. Used for cash acquisitions, stock-for-stock mergers, and mixed-consideration deals. The cousin 'forward triangular merger' has target merge into the subsidiary, with sub surviving.

snappy: A future interest the grantor keeps when conveying a lesser estate — comes back at the end.
detail: Distinguishes from a remainder (which goes to a third party). 'A to B for life' leaves A a reversion: when B dies, the property comes back. Reversions are vested by definition — the grantor still exists (or their estate does). Common in long-term leases (the landlord holds a reversion behind the tenant's leasehold) and in trust contexts. The 'possibility of reverter' is the cousin doctrine for defeasible fees.

snappy: When sale of a company becomes inevitable, the board's duty shifts to maximising short-term shareholder value.
detail: Revlon v. MacAndrews & Forbes (Del. 1986). Triggered by: cash deal, breakup of company, change of control. Once triggered: board must conduct adequate sale process — auction, market check, or post-signing fiduciary out. QVC v. Paramount (Del. 1994) extended trigger to stock deals with change of control. Modern application: 'Revlon mode' analysis frames most public-target sales litigation. Activists invoke against negotiated transactions lacking robust market checks.

full: Racketeer Influenced and Corrupt Organizations Act
snappy: Lets prosecutors bundle a pattern of racketeering crimes into a single overarching organised-crime charge.
detail: 18 U.S.C. § 1962. Originally aimed at organised crime, RICO is now used against street gangs, corrupt police departments, and white-collar enterprises. Requires an 'enterprise' plus a 'pattern' of two or more 'predicate acts' from a long list. Civil RICO (§ 1964(c)) lets private plaintiffs win triple damages. The breadth has invited criticism — RICO charges against the Trump 2020 election efforts (Georgia state RICO statute) drew national attention.

snappy: A person's right to control commercial use of their name, image, voice, and likeness.
detail: State-law doctrine — about half the states have right-of-publicity statutes; others recognise via common law. Often descendible to heirs (variable post-mortem terms: California 70 years, Tennessee 10+ renewable, New York's 2020 statute 40 years). Famous cases: Vanna White v. Samsung (1992) on a robotic look-alike; Hart v. Electronic Arts (NCAA Football, 2013). Tension with First Amendment when commercial use intersects with newsworthy or expressive content.

snappy: The Sixth Amendment right to have a lawyer at every 'critical stage' of criminal prosecution.
detail: Gideon v. Wainwright (1963) extended the right to indigent state-court felony defendants; Argersinger v. Hamlin (1972) extended to any case where jail time is imposed. Critical stages include post-charge interrogation (Massiah v. United States, 1964), preliminary hearings, plea negotiations, trial, and first appeal. Right to counsel of choice for retained counsel (United States v. Gonzalez-Lopez, 2006); no equivalent right to choose appointed counsel.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: An unenumerated constitutional right inferred from multiple Bill of Rights provisions.
detail: Griswold v. Connecticut (1965) found 'penumbras' and 'emanations' of privacy in the First, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and Ninth Amendments. Extended by Eisenstadt (1972), Roe v. Wade (1973), Lawrence v. Texas (2003), Obergefell v. Hodges (2015). Dobbs v. Jackson (2022) overruled Roe; substantive-due-process privacy rights now more vulnerable. Lawrence and Obergefell technically survive — but their continued vitality is contested by some justices.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Growing movement and emerging legislation requiring manufacturers to make tools, parts, and documentation available for independent repair.
detail: First state: Massachusetts automotive R2R law (2012, broadened 2020). New York Digital Fair Repair Act (2022). Colorado wheelchair R2R (2022). Federal FTC report (2021) endorsed the movement. State-by-state expansion through agriculture (John Deere settlement, 2023), consumer electronics, medical devices. Anti-circumvention DMCA tensions (Section 1201) and warranty Magnuson-Moss issues are recurring. Apple, John Deere, and medical-device industry resisted, then partially capitulated.

snappy: Eastern-state water-rights doctrine: landowners along a watercourse share reasonable use of the water.
detail: All riparian owners share access — no priority based on who's been using longer. Use must be 'reasonable' considering watercourse's character and other users. Eastern US states (humid climates) follow riparian regimes; the doctrine doesn't work well in arid areas where supply is constrained. Public-trust doctrine overlays riparian rights with public-access rights to navigable waters. Increasingly stressed by drought and interstate compacts.

snappy: Doctrine preventing courts from deciding cases too early — before the dispute has crystallised.
detail: Article III case-or-controversy requirement plus prudential considerations. Pre-enforcement challenges raise ripeness concerns: hypothetical applications might never occur. National Park Hospitality Association v. DOI (2003) — challenge to regulation lacked ripeness without concrete application. Susan B. Anthony List v. Driehaus (2014) lowered the threshold for pre-enforcement First Amendment challenges with credible threat of enforcement.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: A product's design is defective if its risks outweigh its benefits, considering feasible alternatives.
detail: Dominant test for design defect under Restatement (Third) of Products Liability. Factors: usefulness, safety aspects, availability of safer alternatives, ability to eliminate danger, user's awareness, expected loss spreading. Wade factors (Wade, 1973) framework used widely. Requires expert testimony on alternative designs — often the most contested element in modern products cases.

snappy: Taking property from someone by force or threat of force — theft plus a violent element.
detail: Distinguished from larceny by the use of force or intimidation. 'Armed robbery' adds a weapon and triggers enhanced sentences. Federal robbery statutes (18 U.S.C. §§ 2111–2118) cover bank robbery, postal robbery, and carjacking. State law usually grades robbery by weapon use and injury inflicted. The taking must be from the victim's person or 'immediate presence', distinguishing robbery from burglary of an empty home.

snappy: A payment to the IP owner for each use, sale, or unit licensed.
detail: Music royalties split into mechanical (per copy), performance (per public play), and synchronisation (per use in film/TV). Patent royalties tied to per-unit sales or revenue. Drug patents drive billions in pharma royalties — Humira's loss of exclusivity in 2023 was a $20B/year inflection. Music streaming pays fractions of a cent per stream; ASCAP, BMI, and SESAC are the major US PROs collecting on songwriters' behalf.

snappy: The federal rule governing class actions — lawsuits where one party represents many similarly situated people.
detail: Sets requirements: numerosity, commonality, typicality, and adequacy of representation (Rule 23(a)). Then a class-type test: (b)(1) limited-fund, (b)(2) injunctive, or (b)(3) common-questions-predominate damages classes. Wal-Mart v. Dukes (2011) tightened commonality. Comcast v. Behrend (2013) demanded model-tied damages proof. Class actions consolidate small individual claims into significant litigation — consumer, employment, and securities cases predominate.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Initial information each side must hand over automatically at the start of a federal case — witnesses, documents, damages computation, insurance.
detail: Federal Rule 26(a)(1). Designed to short-circuit discovery games by forcing early disclosure of obvious materials. Due within 14 days of the Rule 26(f) conference. Failure to disclose triggers exclusion of the undisclosed witness or document at trial under Rule 37(c)(1) — a 'self-executing' sanction. Supplementation required as new information surfaces.

snappy: The judge can exclude relevant evidence if its prejudicial effect substantially outweighs its probative value.
detail: Federal Rule of Evidence 403. The most-invoked exclusionary tool: handles unfair prejudice, confusion, misleading the jury, undue delay, and cumulative evidence. Common targets: gruesome photos, prior bad acts, defendant's wealth, plaintiff's collateral sources, and incendiary statements. The balance is judge-discretion; deferential appellate review. Companion provisions: Rule 404 (character), Rule 407 (subsequent remedial measures), Rule 408 (settlement offers).

snappy: Antitrust analysis weighing pro- and anti-competitive effects of conduct — the default standard for most conduct.
detail: Burden-shifting framework: plaintiff shows substantial anti-competitive effect; defendant shows pro-competitive justification; plaintiff shows less-restrictive alternative. Multi-factor balancing makes outcomes uncertain. Applies to most vertical restraints (Leegin v. PSKS, 2007 — resale price maintenance), most horizontal cooperation between competitors, monopolisation cases. Distinguishable from per se rules and 'quick look' analysis.

snappy: A corporation electing pass-through tax treatment — profits taxed only on shareholders.
detail: Internal Revenue Code Subchapter S. Election via IRS Form 2553. Restrictions: 100 or fewer shareholders, US citizens or residents only, one class of stock, no corporate or partnership shareholders. Avoids double taxation but limits flexibility. Self-employment tax savings for active owners often the main driver. Common for closely held businesses with modest investor structures; ineligible for many VC and PE investments due to single-class-of-stock restriction.

snappy: A contract for the sale of goods — governed by UCC Article 2, with relaxed formation and warranty rules.
detail: UCC § 2-204: contract for sale of goods can be made in any manner sufficient to show agreement, even if moment of agreement is undetermined. Quantity term required for Statute of Frauds compliance. Gap-fillers supply price, time, place of delivery if missing. CISG governs international sale of goods between member countries unless opted out. Foundation of commercial goods transactions; substantially more flexible than common-law contract rules.

snappy: Post-Enron 2002 statute imposing accounting and corporate-governance reforms on public companies.
detail: Created the Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (PCAOB). Section 302 requires CEO/CFO certification of financial statements. Section 404 requires internal-controls assessments and auditor attestation. Section 906 imposes criminal liability for false certifications. Section 806 protects whistleblowers. Section 1107 criminalises retaliation. Hugely consequential for public company compliance burden; some provisions remain controversial for cost-benefit balance.

snappy: Court order authorising police to search a specific place for specific evidence.
detail: Fourth Amendment requires probable cause and particularity (where to search, what to seize). Warrantless searches are presumptively unreasonable, with major exceptions: consent, plain view, exigent circumstances, search incident to arrest, automobile, border. Evidence obtained without a warrant or applicable exception is subject to the exclusionary rule (Mapp v. Ohio, 1961). Carpenter v. United States (2018) extended warrant protection to cell-site location data.

snappy: Constitutional right to keep and bear arms.
detail: D.C. v. Heller (2008) recognised individual right to gun ownership for self-defence in the home; McDonald v. Chicago (2010) incorporated against states. Bruen v. New York State Rifle (2022) imposed a 'history and tradition' framework: gun regulations must be consistent with the historical American tradition of firearms regulation. United States v. Rahimi (2024) upheld disarming domestic abusers subject to restraining orders. Frequent ongoing litigation over assault-weapons bans, magazine limits, and concealed carry.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Killing with malice but without premeditation — usually intentional but impulsive, or with extreme recklessness.
detail: The catch-all murder charge when prosecutors can't prove premeditation. Mental state can be intent to kill, intent to cause great bodily harm, or 'depraved-heart' indifference to human life. Carries long sentences but typically not the death penalty. Heat-of-passion killings step down further to voluntary manslaughter; truly accidental killings without recklessness fall outside murder.

snappy: Directors, officers, and 10%+ shareholders of public companies must report stock transactions — and surrender 'short-swing profits'.
detail: Forms 3 (initial), 4 (transaction), 5 (annual). Filing within 2 business days for Form 4. Section 16(b) short-swing rule: profits from purchases and sales within 6 months disgorged to the company — strict-liability; no intent or actual misuse required. Enforceable by shareholder derivative action. SEC's EDGAR system makes filings public — closely watched by analysts and short-sellers for insider sentiment signals.

snappy: Federal statute immunising online platforms from liability for user-generated content.
detail: 47 U.S.C. § 230. Two-part shield: (1) platforms aren't 'publishers' of third-party content, (2) platforms can moderate without becoming publishers (Good Samaritan). Foundation of modern internet — without it, social media as we know it likely wouldn't exist. Exceptions: federal criminal law, IP infringement (DMCA handles separately), human trafficking (FOSTA-SESTA, 2018). Reform proposals from both political parties; Supreme Court tackled adjacent issues in Twitter v. Taamneh (2023) and Gonzalez v. Google (2023).

snappy: Mandatory bankruptcy meeting where the trustee and creditors can question the debtor under oath.
detail: 11 U.S.C. § 341. Held about 30-40 days after filing. Trustee leads questioning about petition accuracy, assets, debts, financial transactions. Creditors may attend and ask questions. Failure to appear can dismiss case. Now often conducted by Zoom rather than in-person. Usually short (5-10 minutes) for routine consumer cases; complex business cases can run hours. Common questions about asset undervaluation, transfers to insiders, identity verification.

snappy: Federal cause of action against false advertising or false designation of origin — even without a registered mark.
detail: Two prongs: (1) false or misleading representations about goods/services (false advertising), (2) false designation of origin causing confusion (unregistered trademark infringement). Standing: competitors only (Lexmark v. Static Control, 2014) for false advertising. Major workhorse of business litigation. POM Wonderful v. Coca-Cola (2014) confirmed § 43(a) claims survive even when FDA-regulated labelling complies with FDA rules.

snappy: A creditor with collateral or lien rights — mortgage lender, car-loan lender, equipment financier.
detail: First in priority for proceeds from sale of the collateral. Adequate protection required during automatic stay — periodic payments, replacement liens, or other protection against collateral value decrease. Can usually recover collateral or vote to accept reorganisation plan tied to collateral retention. Strip-down: writing down secured claim to current collateral value (allowed in Chapter 13 for some collateral). Cram-down: forcing reorganisation terms over secured creditor objection.

snappy: Federal statute regulating the offer and sale of securities — requires registration and disclosure.
detail: 15 U.S.C. §§ 77a–77aa. Reaction to the 1929 crash. Section 5 requires registration of public offerings; Section 10 dictates prospectus content; Section 11 creates strict liability for false registration statements. Section 4 exemptions (private placements, Reg D, Reg A) cover most non-public offerings. Modern updates: JOBS Act (2012) for IPO disclosure relief; Reg Crowdfunding for retail investor access. The 'truth in securities' law.

snappy: The federal statute regulating trading of securities after issuance — created the SEC and the disclosure regime.
detail: 15 U.S.C. §§ 78a–78pp. Section 10(b) (and SEC Rule 10b-5) is the anti-fraud workhorse. Section 12 imposes registration requirements; Section 13 mandates periodic filings (10-K, 10-Q, 8-K). Section 14 governs proxy solicitations. Section 16 covers insider transactions and short-swing profits. Foundation of US disclosure-based securities regulation — far more demanding than most other countries' regimes.

snappy: Deceiving investors through false statements, omissions, or manipulation of stock prices.
detail: Securities Exchange Act § 10(b) and SEC Rule 10b-5 are the workhorses. Covers insider trading, accounting fraud, pump-and-dump schemes, and market manipulation. Both civil (SEC enforcement) and criminal prosecutions available. Sarbanes-Oxley (2002) and Dodd-Frank (2010) substantially raised penalties and added whistleblower bounties. Recent prosecutions: Theranos founder Elizabeth Holmes, FTX's Sam Bankman-Fried.

snappy: Money the tenant pays at lease signing to cover damages, unpaid rent, or other charges.
detail: Most states cap amounts (often 1-2 months' rent) and impose return deadlines after move-out (typically 14-30 days). Landlord must itemise deductions; some states require interest payments on deposits held. Wrongful retention can trigger statutory penalties — sometimes double or triple the wrongfully withheld amount. Move-in/move-out inspections protect both sides. Disputes over deposits are among the most common small-claims actions.

snappy: A director or officer transacts with the corporation on terms benefiting themselves — entire-fairness review applies.
detail: Burden shifts to the conflicted fiduciary to prove the transaction was entirely fair: fair price and fair process. Approval by disinterested directors or fully informed shareholder vote can restore business-judgment-rule protection. Modern cleansing procedure: special committee of independent directors with independent advisors, plus majority-of-minority shareholder vote. Common contexts: management buyouts, related-party contracts, executive compensation.

snappy: A grid that calculates a recommended sentence based on the offence's severity and the defendant's criminal history.
detail: Federal Sentencing Guidelines (1987) created uniformity but were criticised for severity and rigidity. United States v. Booker (2005) made them advisory rather than mandatory after Sixth Amendment concerns. Judges still must calculate guidelines and explain departures. State guidelines vary widely — some advisory, some mandatory, some abolished. Sentencing Commission periodically amends, including recent reductions for drug offences (Amendments 782 and 821).

snappy: Property a spouse owned before marriage, plus gifts and inheritances received during marriage — typically not divided in divorce.
detail: Maintaining separate status requires not commingling with marital assets. Common pitfalls: depositing inheritance into joint account, using separate funds for marital home down payment, retitling property as 'joint tenants with rights of survivorship'. Tracing rules vary by jurisdiction. Active management may convert appreciation to marital; passive appreciation may stay separate. Prenuptial agreements often clarify separate-property maintenance.

snappy: Division of federal power among legislative, executive, and judicial branches.
detail: Article I (Congress makes law), Article II (President executes law), Article III (courts interpret law). Checks: presidential veto, congressional override, impeachment, judicial review, advice and consent for appointments. Modern separation-of-powers fights: administrative-agency authority (Chevron doctrine, overruled in Loper Bright 2024), recess appointments (Noel Canning 2014), executive privilege.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: A trademark for services rather than goods — banks, restaurants, airlines, software-as-a-service.
detail: Lanham Act treats service marks identically to trademarks for most purposes. Common usage and case law refer to 'trademark' to cover both. Use-in-commerce evidence is service-specific: advertising, signage, websites, brochures showing the mark in connection with the service. Registration shown with same ® symbol. Service-mark distinctions matter most in distinguishing the goods/services in trademark applications and infringement analysis.

snappy: Officially handing the lawsuit to the defendant so the court has power to act — done by sheriff, process server, or mail.
detail: Federal Rule 4. Methods: personal service, leaving with a competent household member, mailing under Rule 4(d) waiver, or any method the state where the court sits allows. Foreign service follows the Hague Service Convention or letters rogatory. Defective service is grounds for dismissal under Rule 12(b)(5). 'Service by publication' is a last-resort method for defendants who can't be located.

snappy: The minimum distance a building must be from property lines, streets, or other features.
detail: Zoning ordinance prescribes front, side, and rear setbacks varying by zone. Purposes: light/air access, fire safety, aesthetic uniformity, separation between uses. Setback variance is one of the most common variance applications. Newer 'form-based codes' integrate setbacks with broader urban-design goals. Encroachment into setbacks can trigger compliance orders, building-permit denials, or refusal to issue certificates of occupancy.

snappy: The parties agree to resolve the dispute themselves before the court decides.
detail: Roughly 95% of US civil cases settle. Settlement usually involves a payment plus a release of claims — sometimes confidential, often with a non-disparagement clause. Class-action settlements require court approval to protect absent class members. After Epic Systems v. Lewis (2018), pre-dispute arbitration agreements steer many disputes out of court before any settlement is needed.

snappy: A contract resolving a dispute — typically including release of claims and payment.
detail: Standard provisions: release of claims (broad or narrow), no-admission-of-liability language, confidentiality (NDA), mutual non-disparagement, payment terms, indemnity. Tax treatment varies — physical-injury payments often non-taxable; punitive damages and interest taxable. Federal Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (2017) made sexual-harassment settlement payments non-deductible if subject to confidentiality. Court approval required in class actions and minor settlements.

snappy: A class certified only for purposes of resolving the case, not for litigating it.
detail: Amchem Products v. Windsor (1997) approved settlement classes but cautioned that Rule 23 requirements still apply. Often used to package mass-tort or consumer cases into a single resolution covering thousands or millions of class members. Court must approve the settlement under Rule 23(e) after fairness hearing. Coupon settlements and excessive attorneys' fees draw heightened scrutiny — the Class Action Fairness Act tightened these.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Right to jury trial in federal civil cases involving more than $20.
detail: Applies only to federal courts; not incorporated against states (states have their own jury-trial rights via state constitutions). Triggered by 'suits at common law' — historical inquiry whether the type of action was historically tried to a jury. Modern complications: complex commercial cases, statutory rights without common-law analogs, equitable claims. Tull v. United States (1987) distinguished legal (jury required) from equitable (no jury) claims.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Provision saying that if any part of the contract is unenforceable, the rest still binds.
detail: Also called 'savings clause'. Without it, a single invalid clause can void the whole agreement under the 'blue pencil' rule. Common in non-compete and restrictive-covenant contexts: overbroad geographic or temporal scope can be narrowed to enforceable bounds, with the rest standing. Severability isn't bulletproof — courts can still refuse to enforce if the unenforceable part is essential.

snappy: Compensation an employer pays a departing employee — typically in exchange for a release of claims.
detail: Not legally required in most circumstances (WARN Act provides 60-day pay/notice for mass layoffs). Common structure: weeks of base salary per year of service. Older Workers Benefit Protection Act imposes strict requirements for ADEA waivers: 21-day consideration, 7-day revocation. Modern severance often includes COBRA contributions, outplacement services, accelerated vesting of equity. NDAs and non-disparagement clauses increasingly limited (NLRB's McLaren Macomb 2023 ruling restricts some).

snappy: Contract paying a departing employee in exchange for releases and other obligations.
detail: Employer pays severance; employee waives claims (typically discrimination, wage-and-hour, common-law tort), agrees to confidentiality, sometimes non-disparagement, and post-employment restrictions. Older Workers Benefit Protection Act requires 21-day consideration period plus 7-day revocation period for releases of age-discrimination claims. SEC scrutiny of clauses chilling whistleblower reports has reshaped recent drafting.

snappy: Non-consensual sexual contact — covers rape and a spectrum of other unwanted sexual contact.
detail: Modern statutes have moved from gender-specific 'rape' to gender-neutral 'sexual assault' with degrees by force, age, and capacity. Affirmative-consent standards have spread (especially on college campuses under Title IX). Spousal-rape exceptions have been largely eliminated. The 'rape shield' rule (Federal Rule of Evidence 412) limits evidence of complainant's sexual history. SCOTUS held Kennedy v. Louisiana (2008) that death penalty is unconstitutional for child rape.

snappy: Unwelcome sexual conduct in the workplace — actionable under Title VII as a form of sex discrimination.
detail: Two types: quid pro quo (tangible job benefit conditioned on sexual favours) and hostile work environment (severe or pervasive offensive conduct). Meritor Savings Bank v. Vinson (1986) recognised hostile-environment theory. Same-sex harassment also actionable (Oncale v. Sundowner Offshore Services, 1998). MeToo movement (2017+) accelerated awareness, training mandates, and tightening of NDA practices around settlements. Forced Arbitration of Sexual Assault Act (2022) limits mandatory arbitration.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: An owner of corporate stock — entitled to vote on certain matters, receive dividends, share in dissolution proceeds.
detail: Rights vary by class of stock and charter terms. Common-stock holders typically vote on directors, charter amendments, mergers, dissolutions; preferred stock often non-voting on those matters. Federal proxy rules govern voting solicitations. Modern variants: dual-class stock (Facebook, Google, NYT — founder-control structures), restricted-vote IPO classes. Shareholder voting increasingly subject to proxy-advisor influence (ISS, Glass Lewis).

snappy: Federal antitrust statute prohibiting monopolisation and contracts restraining trade.
detail: 15 U.S.C. §§ 1–7 (1890). Section 1: contracts, combinations, conspiracies restraining trade — covers price fixing, market division, output restrictions. Section 2: monopolisation, attempted monopolisation, and conspiracy to monopolise. Foundation of US antitrust. Civil and criminal enforcement; treble damages for private plaintiffs. Major recent enforcement: DOJ v. Google (2024 search monopoly ruling), FTC and DOJ merger reviews, Big Tech investigations.

snappy: Profits from selling assets held a year or less — taxed at ordinary income rates.
detail: Same rates as wages/salary (10%-37% federal plus state taxes). Significantly higher than long-term capital gains rates for most investors. Strategy: hold assets at least a year and a day before selling appreciated positions. Active traders (frequent buying/selling) generate substantial short-term gains unless trading within tax-advantaged accounts (IRAs, 401(k)s). Day-trader filing status (qualifying as professional trader) allows mark-to-market election.

snappy: Terms inside or on packaging of physical software — agreed to by opening the box.
detail: Older mechanism dating to floppy-disk and CD-ROM era. ProCD v. Zeidenberg (1996) enforced a shrink-wrap restriction. Courts split on cases where the buyer can't see terms before purchase. Largely overtaken by clickwrap as software downloads displaced physical media, but still occasionally relevant for boxed enterprise products and consumer goods with embedded software.

snappy: All copies of a defamatory mass publication are treated as one publication for statute of limitations purposes.
detail: Replaces older common-law rule (every copy = separate publication, refreshing the clock). Uniform Single Publication Act adopted in most US states. Online publication: Firth v. State of New York (N.Y. 2002) and others held a website posting is a single publication on first posting; later edits don't reset unless substantial. Critical to internet defamation cases where content stays accessible indefinitely.

snappy: Speedy trial, jury trial, confrontation of witnesses, right to counsel.
detail: Counsel must be provided to indigent defendants in felony cases (Gideon v. Wainwright, 1963) and misdemeanour cases that result in jail (Argersinger, 1972). Public-defender systems exist in every state, often underfunded. Speedy-trial right is enforced through the federal Speedy Trial Act (70-day limit) and state analogues. Confrontation clause (Crawford, 2004) bars hearsay testimonial statements unless witness is available.

snappy: Courts give weight to agency interpretations based on their thoroughness, consistency, and persuasiveness — not binding.
detail: Skidmore v. Swift (1944). Sliding scale of respect — more for well-reasoned, longstanding, expertise-driven interpretations; less for ad hoc or inconsistent ones. Survived the Loper Bright (2024) overruling of Chevron — likely the dominant deference framework going forward. Less power for agencies, more flexibility for courts. Applied to interpretive rules, advisory opinions, agency policy statements not entitled to higher-level Chevron-style deference even pre-2024.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Spoken defamation — generally requires proof of actual damage.
detail: The exception is slander per se: accusations of crime, professional misconduct, sexual impurity (historically), and loathsome disease — actionable without proof of specific loss. Most defamation cases now travel under libel because so much communication is written. The legal frameworks are largely converging across the two with modern statutes treating them similarly.

snappy: One parent has both physical and legal custody — the other parent typically has visitation rights but no decision-making authority.
detail: Increasingly rare absent serious circumstances: domestic violence, substance abuse, mental illness, abandonment, criminal conduct against child. Modern courts strongly favour joint legal custody even when physical custody is primarily with one parent. Sole-physical custody more common than sole-legal custody. Doesn't terminate non-custodial parent's parental rights — visitation, child support obligation continue.

snappy: A business owned and run by one person — no separate legal entity, full personal liability.
detail: Default form for individual business owners — no filing required to form. Owner reports business income on personal tax return (Schedule C). No limited-liability protection: business creditors can reach owner's personal assets. Simplest, cheapest structure but rarely used by sophisticated businesses given the liability exposure. Often a temporary form before incorporating or forming an LLC. Common for freelancers, consultants, side businesses.

snappy: Asking, encouraging, or hiring someone to commit a crime — itself a crime, even if the target refuses.
detail: Criminalised even when the person solicited never agrees. The classic case: 'I'll pay you $10,000 to kill my spouse' — solicitation is complete on the offer, regardless of response. Some jurisdictions merge solicitation into attempt or conspiracy. Federal Murder-for-Hire statute (18 U.S.C. § 1958) is one specific solicitation crime. Renunciation can be a defence in some states if the solicitor prevents the crime.

snappy: A separately copyrightable work — distinct from the underlying musical composition embodied in it.
detail: Two copyrights in a song: the composition (lyrics, melody — owned by songwriter or publisher) and the sound recording (the specific recorded performance — owned by record label or artist). Federal sound-recording copyright only since 1972; pre-1972 recordings governed by complex state-law mosaic until the Music Modernization Act (2018) brought them under federal law. Different licences needed for using a song (mechanical, performance) versus a specific recording (master use).

snappy: Governments can only be sued with their consent — derived from old English doctrine 'the King can do no wrong'.
detail: Federal Tort Claims Act (1946) waived federal immunity for many negligent torts but retains numerous exceptions (discretionary function, intentional torts, foreign country exception). States have parallel tort-claims acts with their own waivers and caps. Eleventh Amendment provides state immunity in federal court. Pre-suit notice requirements, damages caps, and jury bans are common.

snappy: Suing the federal government requires waiver — Federal Tort Claims Act, Tucker Act, and others.
detail: Federal Tort Claims Act (1946) waives immunity for negligence-like torts (with discretionary-function and other exceptions). Tucker Act (1887) waives for non-tort contract and Constitutional Just Compensation claims. Quiet Title Act for adverse-possession-style claims. APA § 702 waives for review of agency action seeking non-monetary relief. No waiver, no jurisdiction — federal courts must dismiss. Subject to ongoing tort-reform-like debates around cap and exclusion expansions.

snappy: A shell company that raises money in an IPO to acquire a private company — taking it public via merger.
detail: Hot through 2020-2021; collapsed after 2022. Investor protections: trust account holding IPO proceeds, redemption rights at de-SPAC, time limit (typically 24 months) to complete. PIPE investments often fund the merger consideration. SEC's enhanced disclosure and liability under Rule 145(a) and new rules (March 2024) have substantially raised bar. Most de-SPACed companies have traded poorly post-merger; activity has shifted to traditional IPOs and other paths.

snappy: Permission to use property for a purpose specifically authorised by zoning ordinance under certain conditions.
detail: Differs from a variance — special uses are anticipated by the zoning code (churches, schools, daycares, kennels in residential zones) but require case-by-case approval. Standards usually focus on compatibility with the neighbourhood, traffic impact, and adherence to specific conditions imposed. Also called 'conditional use permits' or 'CUPs'. Routine for hospitals, group homes, broadcast towers, and other potentially intensive uses.

snappy: Court order forcing the breaching party to actually do what they promised, not just pay.
detail: An equitable remedy granted when money damages are inadequate — typically real-estate sales (every parcel of land is treated as unique), rare goods, and some custom services. Courts won't order specific performance of personal-service contracts (no involuntary servitude) or where supervision would be impractical. Specific performance is the default remedy in many civil-law countries; in the US it remains the exception.

snappy: Sixth Amendment right (and federal Speedy Trial Act) requiring prompt prosecution after charges are filed.
detail: Constitutional analysis under Barker v. Wingo (1972) weighs four factors: length of delay, reason, defendant's assertion, prejudice. Statutory Speedy Trial Act (18 U.S.C. § 3161) requires federal indictment within 30 days of arrest and trial within 70 days of indictment, with detailed excludable-time provisions. Dismissal for violation may be with or without prejudice. State analogues vary widely. Strategic asymmetry: defendants often want delay, prosecutors usually want speed.

snappy: Congress's power to tax and spend 'for the general welfare' — used to set conditions on federal funds to states.
detail: South Dakota v. Dole (1987): conditions on federal highway funds (raise drinking age to 21) upheld. Five-factor framework: (1) in pursuit of general welfare, (2) unambiguous condition, (3) related to federal interest, (4) not violating other constitutional provisions, (5) not 'coercive'. NFIB v. Sebelius (2012) struck Medicaid expansion as unconstitutional coercion — the first time the Court has found a condition coercive.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Contract between sperm donor and recipient(s) specifying donor's rights and responsibilities.
detail: Crucial for known-donor arrangements outside licensed sperm banks (where donor anonymity and rights waiver are typically agency-administered). Should address: parental rights waiver, child support immunity, future contact, medical-history disclosure obligations. Enforceability varies — some states require physician involvement for donor-shield protections (California Family Code § 7613). DIY at-home insemination has produced significant litigation about donor parental status.

snappy: Destroying or losing evidence that should have been preserved — courts can sanction the party responsible.
detail: Triggers a duty to preserve once litigation is 'reasonably foreseeable'. Sanctions range from adverse-inference jury instructions to default judgment. Federal Rule 37(e) governs electronic data; intentional spoliation gets the harshest treatment. Famous example: Zubulake v. UBS Warburg (2003) imposed sanctions for failure to preserve emails. Modern litigation-hold notices and forensic preservation are direct responses to spoliation risk.

snappy: A spouse can refuse to testify against their husband or wife in many cases.
detail: Two flavours: testimonial privilege (refusing to testify at all in criminal cases against spouse — Trammel v. United States, 1980 vests in witness spouse) and marital communications privilege (confidential statements during marriage stay confidential, vests in both spouses). Exceptions include crimes against the spouse or children, joint criminal activity, and proceedings between the spouses. Some states limit civil application.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: A board divided into classes (usually three) with members elected for staggered terms — only one class up each year.
detail: Makes hostile takeover much harder: at least two annual meetings needed to replace majority. Has been criticised as anti-shareholder; widely declassified at S&P 500 companies through shareholder pressure. Still common in younger public companies. Combined with poison pill, can make takeover near-impossible without negotiation. DGCL § 141(d) authorises classification.

snappy: A pattern of unwanted contact or threats causing the victim reasonable fear.
detail: Federal anti-stalking statute (18 U.S.C. § 2261A) covers interstate stalking, including cyberstalking. State laws vary in required mental state and conduct. Course-of-conduct element distinguishes stalking from single threats. Restraining orders are routine companion remedies. Cyberstalking statutes update older laws for social media, GPS tracking, and doxing — major prosecutions have followed celebrity-stalking and intimate-partner cases.

snappy: A flat dollar amount taxpayers can subtract from AGI — alternative to itemising specific deductions.
detail: 2024 amounts: $14,600 single, $29,200 married filing jointly, $21,900 head of household. Increased dramatically by Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (2017) — nearly doubled, plus elimination of personal exemptions. Result: ~90% of taxpayers now take standard deduction rather than itemising (vs ~30% pre-TCJA). Indexed for inflation annually. Additional standard deduction for 65+ or blind.

snappy: How much deference an appeals court gives the lower court's ruling — de novo, abuse of discretion, or clearly erroneous.
detail: De novo (no deference) for legal rulings; clearly erroneous (high deference) for factual findings under Rule 52(a); abuse of discretion (moderate deference) for case-management calls, evidentiary rulings, and equitable remedies. The standard often dictates the outcome — a discretionary ruling almost never gets reversed, while pure legal questions get fresh appellate analysis. Picking the correct standard is the threshold appellate skill.

snappy: Whether a plaintiff has suffered a real, concrete injury that lets them sue at all.
detail: Article III standing requires injury-in-fact, traceability to the defendant's conduct, and redressability by a court. The Supreme Court has narrowed standing in privacy and statutory-damages cases (Spokeo, TransUnion v. Ramirez 2021) — a bare statutory violation isn't enough. State courts often have looser standing rules than federal courts.

snappy: The Constitution restricts government action, not private conduct — the threshold for constitutional claims.
detail: Civil Rights Cases (1883) limited Congress's 14th Amendment power to state action. Modern doctrine includes 'symbiotic relationship', 'public function', and 'state compulsion or encouragement' tests. NCAA v. Tarkanian (1988) found the NCAA not a state actor; Brentwood Academy (2001) found a state high-school association was. Online platforms remain firmly private actors despite scale and public-discourse role — Manhattan Community Access (2019) rejected public-forum analysis.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: State law overrides local ordinances — particularly important for cities trying to regulate beyond state authorisation.
detail: Dillon's Rule states (most): cities have only powers expressly granted by state law. Home-rule states give cities broader authority. Recent preemption battles: gun safety, minimum wage above state-floor, fracking bans, plastic-bag bans, paid sick leave, ride-share regulation, short-term-rental rules. Preemption can be express (state statute clearly bars) or implied. Constitutional limits on preemption arise rarely.

snappy: Project-specific addendum to an MSA — what's getting delivered, by when, for how much.
detail: Standard SOW content: scope, deliverables, milestones, acceptance criteria, schedule, price (fixed-fee, time-and-materials, or hybrid), assigned personnel, and any project-specific terms. The MSA's legal terms govern unless the SOW says otherwise. Conflict between MSA and SOW often goes to the MSA, but well-drafted MSAs let SOWs override on commercial terms while not on legal ones.

snappy: A written law passed by a legislature — Congress at the federal level, state legislatures at the state level.
detail: Statutes are one of the three main sources of US law alongside the Constitution and case law (judicial opinions). When a statute is clear, courts apply it; when it isn't, judges interpret it using canons of construction. Famous examples: the Civil Rights Act of 1964, the Sherman Antitrust Act, the Affordable Care Act. Statutes are codified into the United States Code (USC) at the federal level.

snappy: Rule that certain contracts must be in writing to be enforceable.
detail: Originating in a 1677 English statute. The classic six: sales of land, contracts that can't be performed within a year, sales of goods over $500 (UCC § 2-201), promises to pay another's debt (suretyship), marriage-consideration agreements, and contracts in consideration of marriage. A 'writing' just needs to identify the parties, subject matter, and essential terms — and be signed by the party to be charged. Email and DocuSign qualify under the federal E-SIGN Act.

snappy: The deadline by which a case must be filed — after that, it's barred.
detail: Criminal limitations vary by offence: typically 3–6 years for felonies, none for murder in most states. Civil limitations vary widely: 2–6 years for personal injury, 4 for fraud, often longer for breach of written contract. Federal Title VII discrimination claims must reach the EEOC within 180–300 days. The clock generally starts when the cause of action accrues — sometimes when discovered ('discovery rule').

snappy: An absolute deadline cutting off liability — runs from a fixed event (sale, completion), regardless of when injury or discovery occurs.
detail: Distinct from statute of limitations, which usually runs from injury or discovery. Common in products liability (often 10-15 years from sale), construction (10 years from completion in many states), and medical devices. Constitutional challenges have generally failed. Major defendant protection: even latent disease cases (asbestos, pharmaceuticals) can fall outside repose periods running from sale.
