snappy: Pre-set damages for copyright infringement — claimable without proving actual loss.
detail: Available only if registration occurred before the infringement (or within three months of publication). Range $750–$30,000 per work, up to $150,000 for willful infringement. Makes copyright litigation viable when actual damages are hard to prove — typical in music, photography, and software. Anchored a generation of file-sharing lawsuits in the 2000s. Different from punitive damages, which are tort-side.

snappy: A right in some states letting a defaulting borrower reclaim foreclosed property within a fixed period after sale.
detail: Period varies — often 6 months to 1 year. Borrower must pay the foreclosure sale price plus interest and costs. Roughly half of US states recognise some form of statutory redemption; mostly in judicial-foreclosure states. Lenders' bidding strategies account for redemption risk. The right can chill third-party bidding at foreclosure sales because purchasers face displacement during the redemption period.

snappy: A stepparent legally adopting their spouse's child from a prior relationship.
detail: Simpler process than non-relative adoption — often single court hearing without home study. Requires consent from biological parent being replaced (or termination of their rights). Effect: stepparent becomes full legal parent; original biological parent loses rights including child-support obligation. Common after long-term step-relationships develop. Adult adoption (stepparent of adult stepchild) used in some inheritance/estate-planning contexts.

snappy: A corporation buying back its own shares — alternative to dividends for returning capital to shareholders.
detail: Authorised by board; can be open-market repurchases (most common), tender offers, or accelerated buyback programs. SEC Rule 10b-18 provides safe harbour from market-manipulation claims. Boost EPS by reducing share count; criticised as boosting executive compensation via stock options without underlying value creation. Inflation Reduction Act (2022) imposed 1% excise tax on net buybacks of public US companies. Buyback authorisations and execution rates are closely watched by investors.

snappy: A right to buy company stock at a fixed 'strike' price during a specified period — common employee compensation.
detail: Two flavours: incentive stock options (ISOs — favourable tax treatment, limited to $100K annual vesting) and non-qualified stock options (NSOs — ordinary income at exercise, employer tax deduction). Vesting schedules typically 4 years with 1-year cliff. Public-company options usually granted at fair market value (Sec. 409A compliance). Compensation expense recognised under ASC 718. Private-company option pricing requires Sec. 409A valuation. Major component of tech compensation packages.

snappy: Buying a company by purchasing its stock from shareholders — full target entity becomes a subsidiary.
detail: Acquires all assets, liabilities, contracts (no assignment issues), permits. Simpler legal structure than asset purchases but more total liability assumption. Buyer takes carry-over tax basis (Sec. 338(h)(10) election sometimes used to treat as asset deal for tax). Reps and warranties insurance increasingly common substitute for traditional indemnification. R&W insurance market expanded dramatically through 2020s.

snappy: Subdividing existing shares — each shareholder gets more shares of proportionally smaller value.
detail: 2-for-1, 3-for-1, 10-for-1 are common. Net effect: no economic change in ownership; just a larger number of lower-priced shares. Motivations: lower per-share price for retail-investor accessibility, increased liquidity, signal of management confidence in future stock-price growth. Reverse stock splits consolidate shares — often used to maintain stock-exchange listing requirements when price drops. Tesla (2020 5-for-1), Apple (2020 4-for-1), Nvidia (2024 10-for-1) recent high-profile examples.

snappy: A police pat-down during a Terry stop, limited to searching for weapons.
detail: Authorised by Terry v. Ohio (1968). NYPD's aggressive stop-and-frisk program (peaking at ~685,000 stops in 2011) was ruled unconstitutional as applied in Floyd v. City of New York (2013) — racial profiling violated equal protection. The frisk must be for officer safety only; finding contraband via 'plain feel' (Minnesota v. Dickerson, 1993) is admissible only if its identity is immediately apparent without manipulation.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Liability without fault — you pay even if you weren't careless.
detail: Applies to abnormally dangerous activities (blasting, keeping wild animals), defective products (the foundation of products-liability law), and in some states to dog bites by particular breeds. Justification: the actor benefits from the activity and is in the best position to insure against losses. Products-liability strict liability is the modern engine of class-action tort law — Bridgestone tires, Vioxx, talc, opioids.

snappy: Manufacturers are liable for defective products that injure users — no need to prove negligence.
detail: Restatement (Second) of Torts § 402A (1965) launched modern doctrine. Defendant: anyone in the product's commercial chain (manufacturer, distributor, retailer). Three defect theories: manufacturing, design, failure to warn. Greenman v. Yuba Power Products (Cal. 1963) was the foundational case. Restatement (Third) of Products Liability (1998) restructured into separate rules for each defect type. Tort-reform movements have substantially trimmed in many states.

snappy: The highest level of constitutional review — government must show its law is 'narrowly tailored' to a 'compelling interest'.
detail: Applies to laws burdening fundamental rights (free speech, voting, interstate travel) or using suspect classifications (race, national origin, alienage). Korematsu v. United States (1944, overruled in dicta by Trump v. Hawaii, 2018) was a rare strict-scrutiny case where government action survived. Modern affirmative-action programs struck down under strict scrutiny in Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard (2023). Almost always fatal in practice — though not 'strict in theory, fatal in fact' is no longer a reliable shorthand.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Workers stopping work to pressure employer in a labour dispute — protected activity under federal law.
detail: Economic strikes: leverage over bargaining issues. Unfair-labour-practice strikes: protest of illegal employer conduct. Difference matters for replacement-worker rules — economic strikers can be permanently replaced; ULP strikers can only be temporarily replaced. Wildcat strikes (unauthorised by union) lose NLRA protection. Sympathy strikes generally permitted. Recent high-profile: Hollywood writers/actors (2023), UAW (2023), Boeing machinists (2024). Strike duration impact on wages and supply chains substantial.

snappy: Streamlined Chapter 11 for small business debtors — added by SBRA 2020.
detail: Debt limit: $7.5M total non-contingent (raised from $2.75M temporarily through 2024). Faster: plan must be filed within 90 days. No creditors' committee. Trustee appointed (but debtor retains operations control). No absolute priority rule — debtor can retain equity if plan is fair and equitable and devotes disposable income for 3-5 years. Significantly cheaper and faster than regular Chapter 11. Adoption rates surged 2020-2024 as small businesses navigated post-pandemic distress.

snappy: Whether the court has authority to hear this *type* of case — federal vs state, civil vs criminal, statutory vs equity.
detail: Federal courts are courts of limited jurisdiction — they hear only what Congress authorises. State courts are courts of general jurisdiction. Subject-matter defects can be raised at any time (including on appeal) and aren't waivable, unlike personal jurisdiction. A court without subject-matter jurisdiction must dismiss — every judgment it issued in the case is void.

snappy: When a tenant rents the property out to someone else for less than the remaining lease term.
detail: Distinguishable from assignment (transferring the entire remaining term and all rights). Original tenant remains liable to landlord; subtenant pays original tenant. Most leases require landlord consent to sublet; landlord cannot unreasonably withhold in some jurisdictions. Common in commercial leases when businesses downsize and in residential leases for sabbaticals or job relocations. Subletting without consent is grounds for eviction in most leases.

snappy: A court-backed order to appear, testify, or produce documents — ignore it and you can be held in contempt.
detail: Two flavours: subpoena ad testificandum (testify) and subpoena duces tecum (bring documents). Issued by parties to a lawsuit, by grand juries, and by congressional committees. Targets can move to quash for overbreadth, undue burden, or privilege. High-profile recent examples: the January 6th committee subpoenas; SEC subpoenas in crypto enforcement actions.

snappy: A subpoena that demands documents or things, not just testimony.
detail: Federal Rule 45 governs federal subpoenas; state rules mirror it. Issued by an attorney as officer of the court, served on a non-party (witnesses, banks, employers, custodians of records). Recipients can object on grounds of overbreadth, privilege, undue burden, or expense. Failure to respond can be enforced by contempt. Distinct from a subpoena ad testificandum, which compels testimony only.

snappy: Performance that's close enough to the contract — entitles the performer to the price, minus damages for the shortfall.
detail: Common-law doctrine softening the harsh 'perfect tender' rule. Jacob & Youngs v. Kent (1921, Cardozo): a contractor used Cohoes-brand pipe instead of the specified Reading-brand. Substantially identical. The owner had to pay the contract price minus the trivial difference in value. Doctrine doesn't apply to UCC sales of goods, which keep something closer to perfect-tender.

snappy: Fourteenth Amendment doctrine protecting fundamental rights against government interference — even though they're not enumerated in the Constitution.
detail: Roots in Lochner v. New York (1905), now-discredited economic-rights cases. Modern doctrine focuses on personal-liberty fundamental rights: contraception (Griswold, 1965), marriage (Loving v. Virginia, 1967; Obergefell v. Hodges, 2015), parental rights (Pierce v. Society of Sisters, 1925). Dobbs v. Jackson (2022) overruled Roe v. Wade (1973) and Planned Parenthood v. Casey (1992), narrowing the doctrine. Test: 'deeply rooted in this Nation's history and tradition'.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: The contract's terms themselves are oppressively one-sided — beyond fair dealing.
detail: Second prong of unconscionability analysis. Classic examples: extreme price gouging, unfair limitation of remedies, harsh fee-shifting only against consumers, mandatory arbitration of consumer claims with insufficient procedural protections. Tested at time of contract formation, not retrospectively from outcome. Severance possible — courts often strike specific clauses rather than voiding entire contracts. Critical analysis in employment and consumer arbitration challenges.

snappy: A mark that hints at a product's qualities without describing them — registrable without showing secondary meaning.
detail: Requires consumer imagination to connect mark to product. Classic examples: 'Coppertone' (sun tan lotion), 'Greyhound' (bus line), 'Microsoft' (software). Mid-tier of distinctiveness in the Abercrombie spectrum: generic (unprotectable) → descriptive (needs secondary meaning) → suggestive (registrable) → arbitrary/fanciful (strongest). The dividing line between descriptive and suggestive is notoriously fuzzy and heavily litigated.

snappy: The official notice telling a defendant they've been sued and must respond.
detail: Served along with the complaint by a process server or sheriff. Improper service can get a case dismissed. The summons states which court, the deadline to answer (typically 21 days in federal court), and the consequences of not appearing — a default judgment that can be enforced like any other.

snappy: Post-prison supervision in the federal system — replaces parole for federal sentences imposed after 1987.
detail: 18 U.S.C. § 3583. Imposed at sentencing, served after prison. Conditions include drug testing, employment, no association with felons, no firearms; specific terms tailored to the offence. Violation results in revocation hearings (Federal Rule of Criminal Procedure 32.1) with potential return to prison — sometimes for years more than the original term. United States v. Haymond (2019) imposed jury-trial requirements on some revocation proceedings.

snappy: When a federal court hears extra state-law claims that are part of the same dispute.
detail: 28 U.S.C. § 1367 codified what used to be called 'pendent' and 'ancillary' jurisdiction. Lets a court resolve all related claims in one proceeding even if some lack independent federal jurisdiction. Court can decline supplemental jurisdiction (and remand state claims) if federal claims drop out early or state-law issues predominate. Common in employment, civil-rights, and IP cases mixing federal and state theories.

snappy: Ownership of the surface of land, when mineral or other subsurface rights have been separately conveyed.
detail: Surface owners must allow reasonable mineral extraction even when they don't own the minerals. Disputes often arise about location of well pads, roads, pipelines, and compensation for surface damage. 'Accommodation doctrine' in some states requires the mineral owner to use methods minimising surface disturbance. Surface-use agreements are negotiated regularly between landowners and oil/gas operators in active producing areas.

snappy: An arrangement where a woman carries a pregnancy for someone else who will raise the child.
detail: Two types: traditional (surrogate's egg) and gestational (intended parents' or donor egg). Legal status varies enormously by state: New York legalised paid surrogacy in 2020; Michigan and others ban or restrict; Louisiana limits to married heterosexual couples; California has robust framework. Pre-birth orders establishing parentage common in surrogacy-friendly states. Pre-conception agreements heavily negotiated: medical decisions, financial terms, parental rights, dispute resolution.

snappy: A claim brought by the deceased's estate for damages the deceased would have recovered if alive.
detail: Statutory: most states allow estates to pursue claims that would have been the decedent's. Covers pre-death pain and suffering, medical expenses, lost wages from injury to death. Distinct from wrongful-death actions (which are the family's loss, not the estate's). Both typically pleaded together. Tort-reform caps and Federal Tort Claims Act limits affect recovery scope.

snappy: Provision listing which obligations continue after the contract ends.
detail: Typical survivors: confidentiality, indemnity, IP ownership, payment for performance before termination, dispute-resolution clauses, governing law, limits on liability. Without a survival clause, courts may infer some — but explicit listing avoids fights. Standard in SaaS agreements, employment contracts, joint ventures, and any contract with information-confidentiality stakes.

snappy: A category (race, national origin, alienage) that triggers strict scrutiny under the Equal Protection Clause.
detail: Footnote 4 of Carolene Products (1938) launched modern analysis: 'discrete and insular minorities' need heightened judicial protection. Sex is quasi-suspect (intermediate scrutiny). Whether sexual orientation and gender identity are suspect remains contested — courts split. Bostock v. Clayton County (2020) applied Title VII to LGBTQ workers; Equal Protection treatment remains less clear. Wealth, age, and disability are not suspect.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Conduct intended to convey a message — flag burning, armbands, draft-card burning — gets First Amendment protection.
detail: Tinker v. Des Moines (1969) — students wearing black armbands to protest Vietnam War. Texas v. Johnson (1989) protected flag burning. Test (United States v. O'Brien, 1968): government regulation OK if (1) within constitutional power, (2) furthers important interest, (3) interest unrelated to suppression of expression, (4) incidental restriction no greater than essential. Modern flashpoints: knee-taking by athletes, vaccine protests, public-property protests.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Article II directive that the President 'take care that the laws be faithfully executed'.
detail: Foundation of presidential power and duty to enforce laws. Source of debate about presidential discretion not to enforce statutes (DACA), prosecutorial priorities, and signing statements. United States v. Texas (2023) addressed standing in immigration-enforcement cases. Limits 'unitary executive' theories about how much control the President has over independent agencies and inferior officers.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Dilution by tarnishment — when a famous mark's reputation is harmed by association with poor-quality or offensive uses.
detail: Federal Trademark Dilution Revision Act (2006). Distinguishable from blurring (which weakens distinctiveness even without harm to reputation). Examples: cheap knock-offs, pornographic uses of famous marks, association with controversial messaging. Limited to 'famous' marks recognised broadly by general consuming public. Free-speech limits significant: Mattel v. MCA (Barbie Girl, 9th Cir. 2002) protected parody and cultural commentary.

snappy: IRS review of a tax return for accuracy — by mail, in-person, or field examination.
detail: Three types: correspondence audit (most common, by mail), office audit (at IRS office), field audit (at taxpayer's location or representative's office). Generally three-year statute of limitations from filing (six years for major omissions, no limit for fraud). Audit rates very low: about 0.4% overall, much higher at extreme high incomes and for certain credit claimants (EITC). Right to representation by attorney or CPA. Disagreement can be appealed via IRS Office of Appeals or Tax Court.

snappy: Legally minimising tax liability through proper use of deductions, credits, and structuring.
detail: Distinguishable from illegal tax evasion. Famously: Justice Learned Hand: 'there is nothing sinister in so arranging one's affairs as to keep taxes as low as possible'. Modern boundary: economic substance doctrine (IRC § 7701(o)) bars transactions lacking business purpose beyond tax benefits. Step-transaction doctrine recharacterises related transactions. Sham-transaction doctrine ignores form. Aggressive tax planning challenged by these doctrines and reportable-transaction rules.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Income ranges taxed at progressively higher marginal rates.
detail: 2024 brackets for single filers (taxable income): 10% to $11,600; 12% to $47,150; 22% to $100,525; 24% to $191,950; 32% to $243,725; 35% to $609,350; 37% above. Brackets adjusted annually for inflation. Marginal vs effective rate distinction important: only the income within each bracket gets that rate. Doubled for married filing jointly. State income tax brackets vary widely — some states have flat tax.

snappy: A dollar-for-dollar reduction in tax owed — more valuable than deductions which reduce taxable income.
detail: Refundable credits (EITC, Child Tax Credit partial) can produce refunds beyond tax owed. Non-refundable credits limited to tax liability — unused portion sometimes carries forward. Major credits: Child Tax Credit, Earned Income Tax Credit, American Opportunity (education), Saver's Credit, foreign tax credit, residential energy credits. Subject to AGI phase-outs at higher incomes. Often most efficient way to deliver tax benefits to lower- and middle-income taxpayers.

snappy: A subtraction from taxable income — reduces tax owed by your marginal tax rate times the deduction.
detail: Less valuable than equivalent tax credit (which reduces tax dollar-for-dollar). $1,000 deduction saves $220 for someone in 22% bracket; $1,000 credit saves the full $1,000. Two main categories: above-the-line (reduces AGI; available to all) and below-the-line (only useful if itemising). Subject to phase-outs and limits. Many deductions eliminated or capped by TCJA (2017).

snappy: Wilfully evading the assessment or payment of federal taxes — felony, up to five years.
detail: 26 U.S.C. § 7201. Requires (1) a substantial tax due, (2) an affirmative act of evasion (false return, hidden assets, sham transactions), and (3) wilfulness — knowing of the duty and intentionally violating it. Distinct from tax avoidance (legal minimisation) and tax negligence (civil). Al Capone famously convicted of tax evasion (1931) after racketeering prosecutions failed. Often charged alongside money laundering and false-statement counts.

snappy: Wilfully evading taxes through false statements, false documents, or hidden assets — both civil and criminal liability.
detail: Civil fraud penalty: 75% of underpayment attributable to fraud (IRC § 6663). Criminal: tax evasion (§ 7201, felony, up to 5 years), filing false return (§ 7206, felony, up to 3 years). Burden of proof: clear and convincing for civil fraud; beyond reasonable doubt for criminal. Distinct from tax avoidance (legal minimisation) and tax negligence (civil 20% penalty). High-profile prosecutions: Hunter Biden (2024 plea then conviction), various tax-shelter promoters.

snappy: A government claim on property for unpaid taxes — federal, state, or local.
detail: Federal tax liens (26 U.S.C. § 6321) attach to all of a taxpayer's property and rights to property upon assessment. Property tax liens (state and local) typically prime all other liens, even pre-existing mortgages. Tax-lien certificates and tax deeds are investment instruments — buyers acquire the government's lien interest. Failure to pay property taxes can ultimately result in loss of the property in a tax sale.

snappy: An annual filing with the IRS reporting income, deductions, credits, and tax owed.
detail: Individual return: Form 1040 due April 15 (extension to October 15 available). Business returns vary by entity: 1120 (C corp), 1120-S (S corp), 1065 (partnership/LLC). E-filing nearly universal; paper filings less than 10%. IRS Free File for AGI under $79,000 (2024). State returns parallel federal in most states; nine states have no income tax. Audit rates vary by AGI level and return complexity — 0.4% overall but higher at extremes.

snappy: An investment or transaction designed primarily to reduce tax liability.
detail: Many historically promoted shelters now restricted: at-risk rules (IRC § 465), passive-activity rules (§ 469), economic-substance requirements (§ 7701(o)). Listed transactions and reportable transactions trigger disclosure requirements (Form 8886). Legitimate vehicles: qualified retirement accounts (401(k), IRAs), HSAs, qualified-opportunity-zone investments, conservation easements. Abusive shelters: syndicated conservation easements, micro-captive insurance arrangements, monetised installment-sales.

snappy: Employer's deduction of estimated tax from wages — sent directly to IRS and state on employee's behalf.
detail: Form W-4 determines withholding rate. Insufficient withholding leads to underpayment penalty at year-end (safe harbour: 90% of current year or 100%/110% of prior year tax paid). Excessive withholding produces tax refund (interest-free loan to government). Self-employed and investment-income earners use quarterly estimated tax payments via Form 1040-ES instead. Backup withholding (24%) imposed when payors lack valid TIN.

snappy: What you have when a former tenant overstays — you're not legally a tenant, but you're not yet a trespasser.
detail: Arises when a tenant remains in possession after lease expiration without landlord consent. The landlord's choices: accept rent (creating periodic tenancy), accept holdover under lease (continuing term), or initiate eviction. Until landlord acts, holdover tenant has duty to pay rent and avoid waste. Eviction process required — self-help is barred in nearly all states.

snappy: A rental terminable at any time by either party — no fixed term and no formal periodicity.
detail: Created by express agreement or by implication when a tenant occupies with permission but without specified term. Modern statutes often impose minimum notice periods (30 days common), reducing the at-will quality. Distinguishable from periodic tenancy by the absence of automatic renewal. Death of either party terminates a true tenancy at will. Most modern leases avoid the structure due to its instability for both sides.

snappy: A special form of joint ownership between spouses — protected from one spouse's individual creditors.
detail: Recognised in roughly half of US states (and DC). Both spouses own the whole, with right of survivorship. Crucially: creditors of one spouse alone can't reach the property. Some states extend protection to same-sex spouses post-Obergefell; some still don't. Common asset-protection structure: titling the family home as tenancy by the entirety shields it from one spouse's business or tort creditors.

snappy: Co-ownership where each owner has a separate, freely transferable share — no automatic right of survivorship.
detail: When one tenant in common dies, their share passes by will or intestacy, not to the surviving co-owners. Shares can be unequal. Any co-tenant can demand partition (forced sale or division). Default form of co-ownership in most US states. Common among siblings inheriting a family home; common among unmarried couples buying together. Often a litigation flashpoint when co-tenants disagree about use or sale.

snappy: A person or entity renting property under a lease.
detail: Owes rent and lease compliance; entitled to habitability (residential) and quiet enjoyment. May withhold rent or repair-and-deduct for landlord violations, depending on state. Can be evicted only after notice and judicial process. Holdover tenants (staying past lease end) become month-to-month tenants in most states unless lease language says otherwise. Subletting and assignment usually require landlord consent.

snappy: Offering to perform a contract — putting yourself in position to perform.
detail: Restatement (Second) § 234. For sale of goods: seller must put conforming goods at buyer's disposition. For services: ready, willing, and able to perform. Successful tender entitles the tendering party to enforcement or damages if the other side breaches. Unjustified refusal of tender is a breach. Concept central to anticipatory-repudiation analysis and to determining who breached first in disputes.

snappy: Acquirer offers to buy shares directly from target's shareholders — usually for cash, at a premium.
detail: Williams Act (1968) imposed disclosure rules: Schedule 14D-9 (target's response), Schedule TO (offer terms). Modern law requires open offer to all shareholders (best-price rule), minimum 20-business-day open period, withdrawal rights. Hostile tender offers face poison pills, staggered boards, white-knight bids. 'Friendly' tender offers commonly used in two-step transactions: tender offer first, back-end merger to acquire remaining shares.

snappy: Powers not given to the federal government, nor denied to the states, are reserved to the states or to the people.
detail: Foundation of federalism. Largely declaratory of the structure created elsewhere. New York v. United States (1992) and Printz v. United States (1997) gave it teeth: federal government can't 'commandeer' state legislatures or officials to implement federal regulations. Murphy v. NCAA (2018) extended anti-commandeering to invalidate the federal sports-betting ban. Regularly invoked against unfunded federal mandates.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Concise summary of proposed deal terms — usually non-binding, used as a negotiating tool.
detail: Standard in venture-capital financings, M&A, and major contracts. Lists key economic terms (valuation, share class, liquidation preference, vesting, board composition) without the legal mechanics. Once signed, definitive documents are drafted to mirror the term sheet's economics. Most are explicitly non-binding except for exclusivity and confidentiality. The Y Combinator SAFE term sheet is a standardised version for early-stage rounds.

snappy: Contract provision specifying when and how the contract can be ended.
detail: Common varieties: termination for cause (material breach), termination for convenience (without cause, usually with notice), termination for change of control (acquisition trigger). Notice periods and cure periods often required. Termination fees common in service contracts. Government contracts have special 'termination for convenience' rules (FAR clauses). Drafted carefully to avoid disputes about whether termination was valid and what survives termination.

snappy: Severing the legal parent-child relationship — by consent or court order.
detail: Highest stakes in family law — Supreme Court applies 'clear and convincing evidence' standard (Santosky v. Kramer, 1982). Grounds: abandonment, abuse, neglect, parental incapacity, conviction of specific crimes against the child. Prerequisite to adoption (without consent). Adoption and Safe Families Act (1997) shortened federal timelines for moving from foster care to adoption: TPR petition within 15 months in most cases. Indian Child Welfare Act adds higher standards for Native American children.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Authors can take back copyright they previously assigned — 35 years after the original grant.
detail: 17 U.S.C. §§ 203 and 304. Designed to let authors recover rights once a work's commercial value is clear. Procedure-heavy: written notice in specific window (2-10 years before effective date), filed with Copyright Office. Recent waves: musicians reclaiming songs from labels (Prince, Eddy Grant, Duran Duran), authors reclaiming early novels. Termination cannot be contracted away — Congress made it inalienable.

snappy: Contract governing use of a website or online service.
detail: Often used interchangeably with 'terms of use' or 'user agreement'. Standard contents: account rules, acceptable use, IP ownership and license-back of user content, disclaimers, limitation of liability, dispute resolution (often arbitration with class waiver), and the right to modify terms. Modification clauses are heavily litigated — Douglas v. Talk America (2007) bars unilateral changes without notice and assent.

snappy: A brief investigative detention based on reasonable suspicion — less than the probable cause needed for arrest.
detail: From Terry v. Ohio (1968). Officer can stop and briefly question a person when articulable facts suggest criminal activity is afoot. May pat down outer clothing for weapons if reasonable suspicion the person is armed. Stops must be limited in scope and duration; prolonging beyond reasonable necessity converts a Terry stop into an arrest requiring probable cause. Foundation of modern stop-and-frisk practice and its critiques.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: A defendant bringing in a new party who may be liable for plaintiff's claim against the defendant.
detail: Federal Rule 14 — also called 'impleader'. Defendant says: 'If I'm liable, this third party owes me indemnity or contribution.' Common in construction defect and insurance coverage cases. Third-party defendant becomes a full party with discovery and trial rights. Different from joinder under Rule 20 (additional defendants for the original claim) and intervention under Rule 24 (outsiders inserting themselves).

snappy: Statute imposing a very long or life sentence on a defendant with two prior 'strike' convictions for serious crimes.
detail: California's 1994 version was the most severe — life sentence for any third felony, including non-violent ones. Reformed by Proposition 36 (2012) to require the third strike to be serious or violent. Federal three-strikes (18 U.S.C. § 3559(c)) applies to serious violent felonies and certain drug crimes. Lockyer v. Andrade (2003) upheld California's pre-reform regime against Eighth Amendment challenge. Critics tied disproportionate sentences and racial disparities to these laws.

snappy: A clause making timely performance a material requirement — late performance breaches the contract.
detail: Default rule otherwise: minor delays usually treated as immaterial breach absent express clause. Standard provision in real-estate contracts and high-stakes commercial deliveries. Tested strictly: courts look for clear language, not boilerplate fluff. Material delays without time-is-of-essence clause typically don't justify rescission but can support damages. Common modern application: tech-deal closing deadlines tied to financing windows.

snappy: An employer can pay tipped workers a lower base rate, counting tips toward minimum wage — federal allows $2.13 base.
detail: FLSA § 3(m). Conditions: written notice to employee, full tips retained by employee or in lawful tip pool, total compensation reaching minimum wage. State laws vary widely: California, Oregon, Washington require full minimum wage plus tips (no tip credit); New York and others reduced tip credit. DOL '80/20' rule limits time spent on non-tipped duties. Restaurant industry lobbying and worker activism keep tip-credit policy in flux.

snappy: Legal ownership of property — and the documentary trail proving it.
detail: Real-estate buyers get title insurance to protect against undiscovered defects: forged deeds, missing heirs, unpaid liens. Title companies search county records before closing. Clear title means no clouds; a 'cloud on title' is a third-party claim that needs to be resolved or insured around. Marketable title is the standard contracts typically require — title clean enough that a reasonable buyer would accept.

snappy: Insurance protecting the owner (or lender) against losses from defects in the property's title.
detail: Unlike most insurance, title insurance protects against past events not yet known — undisclosed liens, forged deeds, missed heirs, errors in the public record. One-time premium paid at closing. Owner's policy protects the owner; lender's policy protects the mortgage holder. Coverage runs for as long as the insured holds an interest. Major underwriters (Fidelity National, First American, Stewart) dominate the market. Required by virtually all mortgage lenders.

snappy: Investigation of public records to confirm the seller has clear title and to identify liens or encumbrances.
detail: Title companies and real-estate attorneys conduct searches before closings. Records examined: deeds, mortgages, judgments, tax records, divorce decrees affecting property, probate filings. 'Chain of title' must be unbroken back to a sovereign source (or marketable title act cutoff). Issues found ('cloud on title') must be cleared before closing — typically via affidavits, partial releases, or quitclaim deeds from prior interest holders.

snappy: Federal statute banning employment discrimination based on race, colour, religion, sex, or national origin.
detail: 42 U.S.C. §§ 2000e et seq. (1964). Applies to employers with 15+ employees. EEOC enforcement. Includes disparate treatment (intentional discrimination) and disparate impact (neutral policies with discriminatory effect). Bostock v. Clayton County (2020) held 'sex' includes sexual orientation and gender identity. Class actions, individual suits, and EEOC enforcement. Foundation of US employment discrimination law.

snappy: A civil wrong that lets the injured person sue for money — negligence, defamation, trespass, fraud.
detail: Torts are how the legal system handles harm that isn't a crime and isn't a broken contract: a driver running a red light, a company spilling toxic waste, a publisher printing a damaging lie. The classic tort claim is negligence — the defendant owed a duty of care, breached it, and that breach caused harm. Mass tort cases (asbestos, opioids, talc) consolidate thousands of plaintiffs against one defendant.

snappy: Intentionally causing someone to breach their contract with you — the meddler is liable to the wronged party.
detail: Elements: valid contract, defendant's knowledge of it, intentional and improper interference, breach, damages. The defendant must induce or cause the breach without privilege or justification. Recent flashpoints: poaching star employees subject to non-competes, suppliers withdrawing under competitor pressure, hedge funds disrupting acquisitions. Lumley v. Gye (1853) is the founding English authority. Heavy fact-and-context dependent.

term: Tortious interference with prospective business relations
snappy: Disrupting an expected business relationship — even without a binding contract — by improper means.
detail: Standards stricter than for contract interference because no signed agreement exists. 'Improper means' typically requires independent wrongfulness: fraud, defamation, threats. Della Penna v. Toyota Motor Sales (Cal. 1995) required wrongful means beyond simply intent to harm. Common in employee-poaching disputes, customer-list cases, and exclusive-dealing arrangements. First Amendment limits when interference is via truthful public criticism.

snappy: Country-specific designation letting nationals of countries in crisis temporarily remain and work in the US.
detail: 8 U.S.C. § 1254a. DHS Secretary designates countries facing armed conflict, environmental disasters, or extraordinary conditions. Beneficiaries: physically present in US at designation, continuously since, register and pay fees. Designations: El Salvador, Honduras, Nicaragua (multi-decade), Haiti, Venezuela (recent surge), Ukraine, Sudan, others. Beneficiaries don't have path to LPR through TPS alone. Sanchez v. Mayorkas (2021) limited TPS as path to adjustment for entry-without-inspection cases.

snappy: The overall visual appearance or packaging of a product — protectable as a trademark if distinctive and non-functional.
detail: Classic examples: Coca-Cola bottle shape, Tiffany blue box, restaurant decor (Two Pesos v. Taco Cabana, 1992). Wal-Mart v. Samara Brothers (2000) held product-design trade dress always requires secondary meaning. Functional features can't be trade dress (TrafFix Devices v. MDI, 2001) — that's patent territory. Modern battles: phone-case design, app-icon design, restaurant chain interiors. Survey evidence often decides.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: Confidential business information that derives value from being secret.
detail: Coca-Cola's formula, Google's search algorithm, KFC's spice blend. Federally protected since the Defend Trade Secrets Act (2016); also state law (Uniform Trade Secrets Act, adopted by most states). Owner must take reasonable steps to maintain secrecy — NDAs, access controls, exit interviews. Loses protection if it becomes public, even through wrongful disclosure. Reverse engineering from a lawfully obtained product is not misappropriation.

snappy: A word, logo, or design that identifies the source of goods or services.
detail: Rights come from use in commerce; federal registration (USPTO) provides stronger protection and constructive nationwide notice. Trademark owners can stop others from using confusingly similar marks. Famous marks (Apple, Coca-Cola) get extra anti-dilution protection. Generic terms can't be trademarked, and once-strong marks can become generic ('escalator', 'aspirin'). Trademark trials happen at the TTAB or in federal court.

snappy: Weakening of a famous mark even without consumer confusion.
detail: Federal Trademark Dilution Act (1995, amended 2006) protects 'famous' marks against blurring (third-party use weakening distinctiveness) and tarnishment (associations harmful to reputation). Standard is higher than ordinary infringement — only nationally recognised marks qualify (Apple, Nike, Tiffany). Free-speech parody and noncommercial use are safe harbours; Louis Vuitton v. Haute Diggity Dog (2007) upheld a 'Chewy Vuiton' dog toy.

snappy: Recording your trademark with the USPTO — gives nationwide priority and presumptions of validity.
detail: Principal Register provides strongest rights — prima facie validity, constructive nationwide notice, eligibility for incontestability after 5 years, and right to use ®. Supplemental Register for descriptive marks lacking secondary meaning provides fewer benefits. Filing basis: actual use (§ 1(a)) or intent-to-use (§ 1(b), 'ITU'). Madrid Protocol applications offer international filing through WIPO. Maintenance: Section 8 declaration (5th-6th year), Section 9 renewal (10th year).

snappy: USPTO tribunal that hears trademark registration disputes — oppositions, cancellations, and appeals from refusals.
detail: Administrative panel of judges. Most common proceedings: opposition (challenging a published application before registration) and cancellation (challenging a registered mark). Cannot award damages or injunctions — only decides registration status. Appeals go to the Federal Circuit or by civil action to district court. B&B Hardware v. Hargis (2015) held TTAB decisions can have preclusive effect on later infringement suits.

snappy: Shares the corporation has repurchased and now holds — they don't vote and don't get dividends.
detail: Created by stock buybacks. Available for reissuance (e.g., to fund stock-option exercises). Reduces outstanding-share count, boosting EPS. Tax-deductible at corporate level only in limited circumstances. SEC Rule 10b-18 safe harbour for repurchases. Treasury stock distinct from authorised-but-unissued shares. Stock buybacks have boomed since 2017 TCJA — driver of much of S&P 500 capital return. Inflation Reduction Act (2022) imposed 1% excise tax on net buybacks.

snappy: Intentionally entering someone else's land without permission.
detail: Includes physical entry by a person, throwing objects onto the land, or even causing chemicals or smoke to cross the boundary. Trespass to chattels covers interference with personal property — the basis of early computer-network-intrusion cases (eBay v. Bidder's Edge, 2000). Nominal damages are recoverable even without proof of harm. Continuing trespass (e.g., a structure built on a neighbour's land) creates ongoing claims until removed.

snappy: Intentional interference with someone else's personal property — short of full conversion.
detail: Restatement (Second) of Torts § 217. Liability where the interference causes harm to the chattel, dispossesses the owner, or impairs its quality. Internet-era revival: eBay v. Bidder's Edge (N.D. Cal. 2000) applied to scraping/bots. Intel v. Hamidi (Cal. 2003) declined to extend in California to unsolicited email lacking system damage. Foundation for modern computer-trespass theories in absence of CFAA jurisdiction.

snappy: A legal arrangement where one person (trustee) holds property for another's benefit (beneficiary).
detail: Three parties: grantor/settlor (creator), trustee (manager), beneficiary (recipient). Two main flavours: revocable (changeable during grantor's life; doesn't avoid estate tax but does avoid probate) and irrevocable (permanent transfer; potential estate-tax savings). Common types: living trusts, testamentary trusts (created by will), special-needs trusts, charitable trusts, asset-protection trusts. Used for: probate avoidance, tax planning, asset protection, multi-generational wealth transfer, special-needs planning.

snappy: Conditioning sale of one product on buying another — antitrust violation if seller has market power in the tying product.
detail: Clayton Act § 3, Sherman Act § 1. Rule of reason analysis post-Jefferson Parish (1984), though strong-market-power cases face quasi-per-se treatment. United States v. Microsoft (D.C. Cir. 2001) addressed tying in software-platform context. Modern flashpoints: app-store tying (Apple v. Epic, 2021), printer-ink tying, healthcare hospital-physician tying. Distinct from bundling (which doesn't condition one purchase on the other) and exclusive dealing.

snappy: The Uniform Commercial Code provisions governing the sale of goods.
detail: Adopted in some version by all 50 states and DC. Covers formation, terms, warranties, performance, breach, and remedies for goods sales. 'Goods' defined broadly: movable personal property. Excludes services, real estate, securities, intellectual property (those have separate UCC articles or non-UCC regimes). 1990s-era UCC Article 2 revisions (proposed Article 2A and Article 2B) were never widely adopted; original Article 2 remains.

full: Uniform Child Custody Jurisdiction and Enforcement Act
snappy: Uniform state law determining which state has jurisdiction over child custody decisions when families move.
detail: Adopted by 49 states and DC (not Massachusetts). 'Home state' jurisdiction: state where child has lived for past 6 months. Continuing jurisdiction in initial state even after move. Designed to prevent parent-shopping for favourable forums and back-to-back relitigation. Federal Parental Kidnapping Prevention Act provides parallel rules. International parental abduction governed by Hague Convention on International Child Abduction.

snappy: State-uniform law making electronic records and signatures legally equivalent to paper.
detail: Adopted in 47 states (notably not New York, which has its own equivalent statute). Establishes legal equivalence: electronic signatures meet writing and signing requirements; electronic records meet writing-retention requirements. Companion to federal E-SIGN Act (which preempts inconsistent state law). Modern application: DocuSign, Adobe Sign, online consumer e-signatures. Specific carve-outs: wills, family-law documents, court orders.

snappy: So unfair that no reasonable person would agree and no fair person would impose it.
detail: Two prongs: procedural unconscionability (unfair surprise, hidden terms, take-it-or-leave-it) and substantive unconscionability (terms grossly favouring one side). Courts can refuse to enforce the contract or reform the offending clause. Arbitration clauses with class waivers and one-sided fee provisions are the modern battlefield. Carnival Cruise v. Shute (1991) and AT&T v. Concepcion (2011) sharply limited the doctrine's reach.

snappy: Improper persuasion exploiting a relationship of trust — voidable contract.
detail: Restatement (Second) § 177. Requires (1) trusting or dependent relationship and (2) unfair persuasion overcoming free will. Common in elderly-relative cases, family disputes, attorney-client situations, religious settings. Burden often shifts to the dominant party to prove fairness. Recent litigation: cases of capacity-impaired seniors persuaded to transfer assets, suspicious eleventh-hour will modifications. Probate courts particularly attentive.

snappy: State-administered insurance paying benefits to laid-off workers — funded by employer payroll taxes.
detail: Federal Unemployment Tax Act (FUTA) provides federal floor and administrative funding. States set benefit levels and eligibility. Disqualifications: voluntary quit without good cause, misconduct termination, refusing suitable work. Duration: typically 26 weeks; emergency extensions during recessions. Pandemic experience exposed system limitations — fraud, payment delays, gig-worker coverage gaps. Tax-funded, not means-tested. Job-search documentation requirements common.

snappy: One party was wrong about a basic fact — usually not grounds to rescind unless the other party knew or caused it.
detail: Restatement (Second) § 153. Much narrower relief than mutual mistake. Available when (1) the other party knew or had reason to know of the mistake (snap acceptance of obviously erroneous price), or (2) the mistake makes enforcement unconscionable. Computer or arithmetic errors in bids occasionally qualify. Burden on mistaken party is heavy — courts are reluctant to undo deals just because one side miscalculated.

snappy: An organisation of workers formed to collectively bargain with their employer.
detail: Formed via NLRB election (typical) or voluntary recognition. Authorisation cards collected to demonstrate majority support; 30% required to file for election, simple majority to win. Recognised unions exclusively represent the bargaining unit. Dues collection depends on contract and state law (right-to-work limits). Union membership has declined from ~35% (1950s) to ~10% (2024) overall; private-sector membership at ~6%. Public-sector remains stronger.

snappy: Standard for reviewing defensive measures against hostile takeovers — board must show reasonable threat and proportionate response.
detail: Unocal v. Mesa Petroleum (Del. 1985). Two-prong test: (1) reasonable grounds to believe takeover poses threat; (2) defensive measures proportionate to threat. Stricter than business-judgment rule, less strict than entire-fairness. Modern application to poison pills, board procedural moves during deal contests, and litigation defences. Air Products v. Airgas (Del. Ch. 2011) upheld pill against persistent hostile bid — a touchstone for activist defence.

snappy: A creditor without collateral — most credit card debt, medical bills, personal loans.
detail: Lowest priority in bankruptcy distribution. Often receives pennies on the dollar (Chapter 7 sometimes nothing) or modest percentages over plan term (Chapter 13). Vote in Chapter 11 by class — creditor classification affects voting power. Unsecured Creditors' Committee in major Chapter 11 cases negotiates plan terms. Unsecured priority claims (employee wages, support) get full payment before general unsecured. General unsecured typically last in line.

snappy: Specialised federal court hearing tax disputes — without requiring prepayment of the disputed tax.
detail: Article I court (legislative court, not Article III). 19 presidentially appointed judges. Petitions must be filed within 90 days of statutory notice of deficiency. Small Tax Case procedure for disputes under $50,000 (simplified, no appeal). Alternative venues: pay tax and sue for refund in district court or Court of Federal Claims. Tax Court's specialised expertise often preferred by tax professionals.

snappy: DOJ component overseeing bankruptcy cases — distinct from individual case trustees.
detail: Within DOJ since 1986 (replacing prior bankruptcy-judge supervision). Supervises private trustees, investigates fraud, ensures procedural compliance. Reviews Chapter 11 cases for plan feasibility and good faith. Files motions challenging exemption claims, dismissals for abuse, conversion of cases. Funded by debtor fees, not appropriations. North Carolina and Alabama use separate Bankruptcy Administrator system within the courts rather than US Trustee.

full: United States Citizenship and Immigration Services
snappy: DHS component administering immigration benefits — applications for visas, green cards, citizenship, asylum.
detail: Established 2003 (formerly INS). Funded mainly by application fees, not appropriations — fee increases (2024) substantial. Adjudicates: I-130 family petitions, I-140 employment petitions, I-485 adjustment of status, N-400 naturalisation, I-589 asylum (affirmative), I-918 U visa, employment authorisation, advance parole. Service centres around US handle paper processing. Field offices conduct interviews. Backlogs significant — particularly post-pandemic.

snappy: Prosecutor grants immunity for testimony so a witness can't take the Fifth — but charges based on independent evidence still possible.
detail: Distinguished from 'transactional' immunity (full immunity from prosecution for the transaction). Kastigar v. United States (1972) held use immunity is the constitutional minimum the Fifth Amendment requires. Used routinely in congressional and grand-jury investigations. Oliver North's conviction was reversed (1990) because trial evidence was tainted by his immunised congressional testimony — a cautionary tale about immunity's downstream consequences.
sources: Supreme Court of the United States

snappy: The standard patent — covers new and useful inventions, processes, machines, or compositions of matter.
detail: 20-year term from filing under 35 U.S.C. § 154. Most patents granted by the USPTO are utility patents. Subject-matter eligibility under § 101 (Alice Corp., 2014) limits abstract ideas and natural phenomena. Examination involves prior-art search, claim analysis, and prosecution between applicant and examiner. Annual maintenance fees due at 3.5, 7.5, and 11.5 years post-issue; non-payment causes lapse.

snappy: Permission from a zoning board to use property in a way that doesn't comply with zoning rules.
detail: Two types: area variance (relief from dimensional rules like setbacks, height, lot coverage) and use variance (allowing a use not permitted in the zone). Standards vary by state — generally require 'unnecessary hardship' or 'practical difficulty' unique to the property, no adverse effect on neighbours, and consistency with zoning intent. Granted by local zoning boards of adjustment. Often opposed by neighbours; granting variances is a frequent appeal subject.

snappy: The geographic court where a case should be heard, even if multiple courts have jurisdiction.
detail: Federal venue rules generally permit suit where the defendant resides or where a substantial part of the events occurred. Venue can be transferred 'for the convenience of parties and witnesses' under 28 USC § 1404. Forum-selection clauses in contracts pre-pick the venue and are usually enforced absent strong policy reasons not to.

snappy: The jury's or judge's formal decision at the end of a trial.
detail: In jury trials the verdict is the jury's answer to the questions put to it. Most civil verdicts in federal court require unanimity; some states allow non-unanimous civil verdicts. Bench trials produce 'findings of fact and conclusions of law' rather than a jury verdict. Either way, the verdict becomes a judgment when the court enters it on the docket, starting the appeal clock.

snappy: Liability for someone else's wrongdoing — most often, an employer for an employee.
detail: Respondeat superior makes employers liable for employees' negligence committed within the scope of employment. Independent contractors generally don't trigger vicarious liability — but the line between contractor and employee is contested (especially in gig-economy litigation: California's AB5, Uber/Lyft Prop 22). Parents are vicariously liable for children's torts only in narrow circumstances set by state statutes.

snappy: An entry document allowing a non-citizen to seek admission to the US — issued by State Department consulates abroad.
detail: Two main types: immigrant (intent to remain permanently — leads to green card) and non-immigrant (temporary purpose: tourism, business, study, work). Visa allows seeking admission at port of entry; CBP officer makes admission decision. Visa Waiver Program (ESTA): 41 countries allow visa-free travel for tourism/business under 90 days. Visa appointment backlogs at many consulates routine. Validity period varies enormously (10 years for many B-1/B-2; shorter for work visas).

snappy: The schedule for time a non-custodial parent spends with their child.
detail: Many states now use 'parenting time' to avoid possessive 'visitation' language. Schedules range widely: every-other-weekend (traditional minimum) to substantially equal time. Supervised visitation when there are safety concerns — at a facility or with a designated supervisor. Virtual visitation (video calls) increasingly recognised by courts. Restrictions for safety: substance-use monitoring, prohibition of certain associates, third-party transport.

snappy: A contract one party can choose to cancel — distinct from a 'void' contract (no contract at all).
detail: Common voidable scenarios: a minor's contract (the minor can disaffirm), contracts induced by fraud or duress, contracts entered while intoxicated or mentally incapacitated. The non-injured party can ratify (confirm) or rescind. Void contracts — illegal subject matter, missing essential elements — were never enforceable in the first place. The distinction matters for who can do what about the deal.

snappy: Jury-selection questioning to identify biased or unsuitable jurors.
detail: Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 47; in criminal cases governed by Federal Rule of Criminal Procedure 24. Lawyers and judges question prospective jurors about backgrounds, biases, and case-specific knowledge. Strikes for cause (unlimited but require proof) and peremptory strikes (limited number, no reason needed except no race/sex discrimination per Batson). 'Stealth jurors' lying about biases is a recurring concern; some courts allow social-media research on the panel.

snappy: The form employers send employees showing annual wages and tax withholding.
detail: Reports: gross wages, federal income tax withheld, Social Security and Medicare wages and tax, state tax withheld. Due to employees by January 31. Employer also files Copy A with SSA, Copy 1 with state tax authority. W-2 distinguishes from 1099 — employee vs independent contractor classification implications. Box 12 codes report retirement contributions, healthcare benefits, and other items. Mistakes require employer-issued corrected W-2c.

full: Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification Act
snappy: Federal statute requiring 60-day advance notice of mass layoffs or plant closings.
detail: 29 U.S.C. §§ 2101 et seq. (1988). Triggers: 100+ employee layoff threshold, plant closing, mass layoff. Notice to: affected workers, union, state dislocated-worker unit, local government chief elected official. Damages: 60 days' back pay and benefits to each violation-affected worker. Exceptions: faltering company, unforeseeable business circumstances, natural disaster. State 'mini-WARN' laws often more protective. Recent COVID-era enforcement: many wrongful-WARN-violation class actions.

snappy: A guarantee about a fact or future performance — break it and you're liable.
detail: Express warranties are stated outright ('this car has 50,000 miles'). Implied warranties arise automatically: merchantability (the product works for its ordinary purpose) and fitness for a particular purpose (the seller knew the buyer's use case). The UCC governs goods; service contracts use 'workmanlike' standards. Disclaimers must be conspicuous (the famous 'AS IS' in all caps) to be effective.

snappy: A deed where the seller makes legal promises about the title — the strongest form of buyer protection.
detail: Six traditional 'covenants of title': seisin, right to convey, no encumbrances, quiet enjoyment, warranty, and further assurances. The seller is liable to the buyer (and successor buyers) if title turns out defective. Standard in modern residential sales. Often paired with title insurance as belt-and-suspenders protection. Distinguish from quitclaim deeds (no warranties) and special warranty deeds (warranties only as to seller's period of ownership).

snappy: An implied warranty that goods sold by a merchant are fit for their ordinary purpose.
detail: UCC § 2-314. Applies only when seller is a merchant in goods of that kind. Covers fitness for ordinary purposes, fair average quality, conformity to label claims, even running quality. Can be disclaimed only by conspicuous language mentioning 'merchantability' (or 'as is', 'with all faults'). Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act (1975) limits consumer-product disclaimers. Foundation of consumer-protection product cases.

snappy: An employee who reports wrongdoing — protected by various federal and state laws from retaliation.
detail: Federal whistleblower protections include: Sarbanes-Oxley (public company financial reporting), Dodd-Frank (SEC violations, plus bounty program), False Claims Act qui tam (government contractors), OSHA, FLSA. State laws vary widely. SEC whistleblower program has paid over $1.9 billion in awards. Trade-secret-protection statutes (DTSA) require whistleblower-immunity notices in confidentiality agreements. Increasingly active enforcement area.

snappy: A document specifying how you want your property distributed after death.
detail: Requirements vary by state but commonly: testator over 18, of sound mind, written, signed, witnessed by 2-3 witnesses (some states permit self-proving affidavits). Holographic wills (handwritten) recognised in some states. Updates via codicil or new will. Major provisions: executor designation, beneficiaries, specific bequests, residuary clause, guardian designation for minor children. Probate process administers will distribution. Common pitfalls: outdated wills, missing beneficiaries, ambiguous bequest language.

snappy: 1968 amendments to the Exchange Act regulating tender offers and large block accumulations.
detail: Two main disclosure requirements: Schedule 13D for 5%+ stock ownership (10-day filing window); Schedule 14D for tender offers. Substantive tender-offer rules: open 20+ business days, withdrawal rights, pro-rata acceptance, best-price rule. SEC 2023 amendments shortened 13D filing window from 10 to 5 business days. Foundation of modern hostile takeover regulation. Recent activist-investor enforcement battles over wolf-pack 13G filings vs 13D thresholds.

snappy: Using phone calls, emails, faxes, or internet communications in a scheme to defraud — the prosecutor's Swiss Army knife.
detail: 18 U.S.C. § 1343. Just one wire transmission across state lines (often any email) brings the federal hammer down. Used to prosecute everything from Ponzi schemes to college-admissions fraud (Operation Varsity Blues). Each transmission can be a separate count, stacking exposure. 20-year max per count, 30 years for financial-institution fraud. The breadth gives prosecutors enormous leverage in plea negotiations.

snappy: Mandatory protection from removal for someone with 'more likely than not' likelihood of persecution at home.
detail: INA § 241(b)(3). Higher standard than asylum (well-founded fear); doesn't lead to permanent status, no family inclusion, no travel rights. Available even when asylum barred (one-year rule, prior denial, terrorism-related grounds). 8 U.S.C. § 1231 governs. Convention Against Torture (CAT) provides parallel non-removal protection for likelihood of torture. Often pursued as alternative when asylum unavailable.

snappy: Trying to influence a witness's testimony — through threats, payments, or other corrupt persuasion.
detail: 18 U.S.C. § 1512 codified after Watergate. Covers physical intimidation, killing, harassing, and 'corruptly persuading' witnesses. Even seemingly benign communications can qualify if the intent is to alter testimony. Murder of a federal witness is a death-eligible offence. Sentencing enhancements often added to underlying cases when tampering is shown. Smith v. United States (2023) held the statute also reaches anticipated proceedings.

snappy: Work whose copyright belongs to the employer or commissioner, not the actual creator.
detail: Two paths under the Copyright Act: (1) work prepared by an employee within the scope of employment, or (2) certain commissioned works (contributions to collective works, movies, translations, etc.) where the parties expressly agree in writing. Without a work-for-hire arrangement or written assignment, the freelancer retains copyright even if you paid for the work. Critical concept for any commissioned content, software, or design.

snappy: Materials a lawyer prepares for litigation are shielded from the other side's discovery.
detail: Hickman v. Taylor (1947) recognised this protection; Federal Rule 26(b)(3) codified it. Covers attorney notes, case analysis, witness interview memos, and litigation strategy. 'Fact' work product can be discovered upon substantial-need showing; 'opinion' work product (mental impressions) gets near-absolute protection. Distinct from attorney-client privilege — work product protects the lawyer's work, privilege protects the communication.

snappy: State-mandated insurance that pays employees injured at work — exclusive remedy in most cases.
detail: Trade-off: employee gets no-fault coverage (medical bills, lost wages, disability benefits) without proving employer negligence; employer gets immunity from tort suits ('exclusive remedy'). State-administered programs; varies enormously. Texas allows opt-out. Coverage scope debates: telecommuting injuries, work-related stress, gig-economy classifications. Workers' comp fraud (both employee malingering and employer underreporting) is significant enforcement focus.

snappy: Repeated mistreatment of an employee — no federal law specifically bans it, but related theories apply.
detail: Distinct from harassment (which requires protected-class basis). General bullying targeting personality, work style, or perceived weaknesses isn't actionable under Title VII alone. Theories that may reach: hostile-environment claims when based on protected characteristic, IIED tort, common-law negligent supervision, state-specific bullying statutes (limited adoption). Healthy Workplace Bill model legislation pending in many states for over a decade. EU and Australia have more robust anti-bullying frameworks.

snappy: A statutory cause of action letting surviving family members sue when someone's death was wrongfully caused.
detail: Common law barred suits when the victim died (Baker v. Bolton, 1808). Lord Campbell's Act (1846) and US state versions reversed this. Eligible plaintiffs typically spouses, children, sometimes parents — varies by state. Damages: financial support, loss of consortium, emotional distress (in some states), medical and funeral expenses. Distinct from survival actions (covering pre-death pain and suffering). Tort-reform caps in many states.

snappy: An employer's firing that breaks the law — typically discrimination, retaliation, or breach of contract.
detail: Common bases: Title VII discrimination, ADA retaliation, public-policy exceptions to at-will, breach of employment contract, breach of implied covenant of good faith. Damages: back pay, front pay, emotional-distress damages, punitive damages, reinstatement. Insurance-defence side: employment practices liability insurance (EPLI) covers most claims. Settlement common — only a tiny fraction reach trial. Discovery typically focuses on similarly situated comparators and pre-termination performance evaluations.

snappy: An X used as a signature by someone unable to write — still legally valid.
detail: The UCC defines 'signed' as 'any symbol executed or adopted with present intention to adopt or accept a writing' (§ 1-201(b)(37)) — an X qualifies. State statutes typically require witnesses to attest that the marker intended the X as their signature. Common historically on wills, contracts, and deeds executed by illiterate parties. Modern equivalents include digital signatures under the federal E-SIGN Act and state UETA statutes.

snappy: Old common-law rule: if the victim survives more than a year and a day, the death isn't murder.
detail: Originated when medieval medicine couldn't reliably trace cause of death over long periods. Modern medicine and life-support technology made the rule absurd: a victim kept alive for over a year by ventilator couldn't yield a murder conviction. Most US jurisdictions have abolished it by statute or judicial decision. Rogers v. Tennessee (2001) upheld retroactive abolition against an ex post facto challenge.

snappy: Employment contract requiring the worker to promise not to join a union.
detail: Common in the late 19th and early 20th centuries; effectively eliminated by the Norris-LaGuardia Act (1932) which made yellow-dog contracts unenforceable in federal court. The National Labor Relations Act (1935) formalised the right to organise. Modern non-compete and non-solicit agreements occupy adjacent ground; class-action waivers in employment arbitration (Epic Systems, 2018) revived analogous concerns about workers contracting away collective rights.

snappy: Lawyer's duty to represent a client vigorously within the bounds of law.
detail: Drawn from the ABA Model Rules of Professional Conduct. Often phrased as 'zealous advocacy' — the rules now require 'diligent representation' (Rule 1.3) plus an obligation of candour to the tribunal (Rule 3.3). Zealous representation has limits: lawyers can't suborn perjury, mislead the court, or pursue frivolous claims. The competing duties (zealous to client vs honest to court) generate much of legal ethics.

snappy: Local government regulation dividing land into use categories — residential, commercial, industrial.
detail: Upheld by the Supreme Court in Village of Euclid v. Ambler Realty (1926). Modern zoning addresses density, setbacks, height limits, parking minimums, and use restrictions. Hotly contested in housing-policy debates: NIMBY-driven single-family-only zones have been blamed for the US housing shortage. State preemption laws (California SB 9, Oregon HB 2001) have started overriding local zoning to allow more density. Variances and conditional-use permits handle case-by-case relief.
